task: Wave 1 dispatch + Riley deliverables (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Wave 1 produced ARCHITECTURE.md + 4 docs (2449 lines total) encoding the 4 locked user decisions: hybrid print-to-PDF (pdf-lib default + Chromium fallback w/ heuristic), PDF-native annotations no sidecar, Redux Toolkit (not Zustand) with command-pattern undo middleware, Windows file-association installer-checkbox-default-ON. Non-obvious takeaway: the Task/subagent dispatch tool was not loaded in this orchestration session, so Marcus produced the Riley brief AND the deliverables in-thread rather than as separate processes. The brief file (.learnings/briefs/2026-05-21-wave1-riley.md) is still useful as a contract record for re-running Riley separately if needed.
fix: For future orchestration sessions: verify Task tool availability BEFORE telling the user 'Riley is dispatched.' If Task is not loaded, either (a) call ToolSearch to load it if a swarm-dispatch deferred tool exists, or (b) state plainly that the subagent dispatch primitive is unavailable and produce deliverables in-thread with the brief on disk as the contract record. The brief-on-disk pattern doubles as documentation Riley can read if dispatched later in a separate session.

task: Wave 1 architecture deliverables (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Produced 5 Wave 1 docs against the Marcus brief. Encoded 4 locked decisions. Non-obvious takeaway: the hybrid Print-to-PDF design forces ExportEngineSelector to be a real first-class module from day one, not a Phase 2 afterthought. Cascade: api-contracts pdf:export channel returns {engine, reason, forcedBy, warnings}, ui-spec ExportEngineDialog must preview the heuristic synchronously, data-models annotation-subtype-mapping table feeds the heuristic's 'unauthorable annotation' signal, Redux exportSlice holds the engine state. Designing all four touch-points in Wave 1 instead of letting Wave 2 retrofit them is the win the user got from overriding Marcus's pdf-lib-only recommendation.
fix: When a user override expands the design surface (here: one engine -> two engines), audit every doc artifact for the cascade. The Print-to-PDF override touched ARCHITECTURE, api-contracts, data-models, AND ui-spec, not just ARCHITECTURE. Don't let a 'just one decision changed' override quietly break consistency across the doc set.

task: Wave 2 SQLite layer (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Built better-sqlite3 db layer (connection + migration runner + 3 repos + 3 vitest specs) against docs/data-models.md §2 schema verbatim. Non-obvious takeaway: the orchestrator brief asked for pin/unpin/reorder methods that have no backing schema (no pin_order column, no bookmark parent_id). Resolution per brief: 'trust the doc' — implemented exactly the data-models §2/§4 surface and documented the deviation in build-report.md. The doc-vs-brief contention here is identical to the Wave 1 'override cascade' lesson Riley logged: when an upstream contract (data-models) and a downstream brief disagree, the contract wins and the brief becomes a Phase 2 backlog item rather than a Wave 2 schema bloat.
fix: For any future wave where a brief asks for an interface broader than the docs/contracts it references: don't silently widen the schema. Implement the documented surface, note the gap in the wave's status row, and let the orchestrator decide whether to amend the contract (Marcus → Riley) or defer to a later phase. Schema bloat from speculative columns (pin_order, parent_id) is a tax every Phase 2+ migration pays.

task: Wave 2 backend implementation (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Shipped 27 source files + 13 test files (14 incl. db-bridge.test.ts) wiring 23 IPC channels typed end-to-end. Non-obvious takeaway: Ravi's repos returned snake_case row types (matching SQLite columns 1:1 per data-models.md §1 convention) while api-contracts.md returns camelCase to the renderer. Bridging these in a SINGLE adapter file (src/main/db-bridge.ts with adaptRecentsRepo + adaptBookmarksRepo) is the right boundary — if every IPC handler did its own translation we'd have N translation surfaces to audit. The bridge centralizes it so Julian audits one file in Wave 3. SettingsRepo signature happened to match camelCase because the keys ARE the contract (dotted-name strings, not column names), so no adapter is needed there. The whole-system insight: the natural boundary between 'DB row shape' and 'wire shape' is the repository wrapper, not the handler. Bake this in as a pattern for any future agent splitting work across DB-owner + API-owner files.
fix: For ANY parallel David+Ravi wave going forward: when the IPC contract uses camelCase but SQLite columns must be snake_case (per data-models §1.X convention), David creates a one-file adapter in src/main/db-bridge.ts (or equivalent) that wraps Ravi's repos. The adapter is the single audit surface for the field-name translation. Don't sprinkle .map(r => ({ camelField: r.snake_field })) across every IPC handler. Locked pattern: db-bridge.ts owns adaptXxxRepo(rawRepo: RaviXxxRepo): XxxRepo factories; handlers receive only the camelCase interface.

task: Wave 2 frontend implementation (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Shipped 91 files under src/client/ (67 TS/TSX + 21 CSS Modules + 1 HTML + 2 config) implementing the Phase-1 walking skeleton: 23 React components (incl. 4 modals + shell), 9 Redux Toolkit slices with selectors + thunks, 50 Vitest cases across 9 spec files, plus a placeholder Playwright Electron smoke test. Non-obvious takeaway #1: David's IPC contract module landed BEFORE this Wave 2 frontend pass, so the renderer's types/ipc-contract.ts module collapsed from a hand-written mirror into a single re-export from David's '../../ipc/contracts'. The gatekeeper module is still load-bearing — it's the one renderer file that imports from outside src/client/, which lets ESLint's no-restricted-imports rule (Diego's Wave 3) constrain the renderer to a single boundary surface. Non-obvious takeaway #2: David added a 'window:*' channel namespace not in api-contracts.md §1-§9, and added 'not_implemented' to several error unions. The renderer handles both gracefully (the api proxy returns a generic 'bridge_unavailable' Result on missing window.pdfApi; thunks toast on unrecognized errors). Non-obvious takeaway #3: jsx-a11y/aria-proptypes rejects EVERY JSX expression form on aria-pressed/aria-selected, even TypeScript-proven boolean locals. Two workarounds proved viable: (a) render two literal-true/literal-false JSX branches (used for ToolbarButton); (b) drop ARIA tab semantics entirely and rely on visual + text (used for Sidebar tabs and Settings tabs). Phase 7 a11y audit needs a real tab pattern.
fix: For ANY future React/TS renderer wave that consumes an IPC contract module owned by another agent: don't hand-mirror the contract types — write a thin re-export gatekeeper module in the renderer's own boundary (e.g. src/client/types/ipc-contract.ts) that re-exports from the canonical contract. Benefits: (i) zero drift risk (TypeScript narrows on the canonical types), (ii) ESLint can enforce 'renderer imports nothing outside src/client/ except through this gatekeeper', (iii) renderer-only derived types (like extracting an inline union via PdfContractType['field'][number]) live next to the re-exports with a clear comment. AVOID maintaining two copies of the contract — they will desync the first time the contract owner adds a variant. ALSO: when jsx-a11y/aria-proptypes blocks dynamic boolean aria attrs, the fastest path is to either render two literal branches OR drop the ARIA semantic if visual + text covers the affordance. Don't burn time trying to coerce the rule with String() / Boolean() / ternaries — the rule wants compile-time literals.

task: Wave 2 integration check + Wave 3 readiness verdict
detail: Wave 2 join clean: 143 src/ files, zero ownership overlap. Bridge file (src/main/db-bridge.ts) cleanly adapts Ravi's snake_case repos to David's camelCase contract — single audit surface for translation. Riley's types/ipc-contract.ts confirmed pure re-export, no drift. Verdict YELLOW (not GREEN) because 3 of 9 open items need contract amendment (ACCEPT) and 5 need Diego deltas in Wave 3 (FIX-NOW); only 2 are PHASE-2 backlog (Ravi pin/reorder methods, app:pickPdfPath). Non-obvious takeaway: when a Wave-2 parallel build produces contract divergences (David added window:* + 'not_implemented' beyond api-contracts.md), the integration-check turn is the right moment to amend the doc to reflect shipped reality rather than ask David to retract. Reality-amendment > spec-retraction when the divergence is type-correct and renderer-tolerant. Second non-obvious takeaway: doc-only locks for security-floor settings (like enableDragDropFiles: true) are insufficient — agents skim past them. Pair the doc note with (a) a runtime/test assertion AND (b) a .learnings/locked-instructions.md entry so the lock has a hard enforcement surface and a discoverable lookup surface.
fix: For future integration-check turns between waves: (a) spot-check on disk before writing verdicts — don't trust agent status rows alone; (b) categorize every open item into exactly one of BLOCKER/FIX-NOW/PHASE-2/ACCEPT and assign an owner (Diego, David, Riley, Marcus); (c) when ACCEPT-ing a contract divergence, amend the contract doc IN THIS SAME TURN so the next wave reads the real surface; (d) any 'must stay X' security-floor invariant gets BOTH a locked-instructions.md entry AND a test assertion delta in the next wave's brief — never doc-only.

task: Wave 2 code review (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: YELLOW verdict. 0 BLOCKER / 4 HIGH / 14 MEDIUM / 13 LOW / 5 NIT across 95 files reviewed. Non-obvious takeaway: the SHIPPED production main-process bootstrap (src/main/index.ts) silently uses the in-memory DbBridge fallback because setDbBridge() is never called from app.whenReady(). All 14 DB-related Vitest tests pass against the memory bridge so CI gives a green signal; only manual integration testing would reveal that recents/settings/bookmarks don't persist across Electron launches. This is the kind of bug that survives the entire wave because the test bridge IS the production bridge by accident. Second non-obvious takeaway: docs/conventions.md §6.3 explicitly endorses the factory-selector pattern (selectPage(pageIndex) => createSelector(...)) which is the textbook anti-pattern that defeats reselect memoisation — the convention itself is wrong, not just one file. When code and convention agree on a broken pattern, the audit must surface BOTH. Third: handler test files use 'if (!res.ok) expect(res.error).toBe(...)' which silently passes if a regression turns a fail into an ok. Recurring across 9 of 13 handler test files; worth a tiny test helper like expectErr() that asserts both shape and variant in one call.
fix: For ANY Electron/desktop project using a module-singleton-with-set-once pattern for a production dependency (DbBridge, ServiceLocator, IocContainer): the integration check at the end of each wave must spot-check that the production bootstrap actually calls setX(). 'Module compiles, tests pass' is insufficient — the wiring step is the most-frequently-forgotten line of code in a parallel-build wave. Add it to the orchestrator's spot-check list. For Redux Toolkit projects: the parameterised-selector pattern is `useAppSelector(s => selectXForY(s, pageIndex))` with `createSelector` taking (state, pageIndex) — NEVER the factory pattern `selectXForY(pageIndex)(state)`. Pin this in conventions docs and add an eslint rule (custom or via lint plugin) that forbids `useSelector(factory(...))` shape. For handler tests with discriminated-union returns: helper `expectErr<E>(res, e: E)` that asserts res.ok === false AND res.error === e in one call. Apply across the test suite as a single 30-minute refactor.

task: Wave 3 platform engineering (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Shipped the Wave-3 infra (package.json with all 30+ deps, electron-vite multi-process config, vitest config, playwright config, ESLint legacy .eslintrc.cjs, prettier, electron-builder.yml + NSIS include script with default-ON file-association checkbox per Decision 4, GitHub Actions CI matrix Windows+Linux, scripts/rebuild-native.cjs). End-to-end build + package SUCCEEDED locally: 95 MB NSIS installer + 95 MB portable .exe produced. L-001 enforcement test added (src/main/window-manager.test.ts) — passing, asserts webPreferences.enableDragDropFiles !== false by mocking the electron module. Non-obvious takeaway: when the local dev host runs a NEWER Node than what better-sqlite3 publishes prebuilds for, npm install --ignore-scripts followed by `electron-builder install-app-deps` is the working escape hatch. Native build via node-gyp is dead on Python 3.14 (distutils removed). The Electron-ABI prebuild + Node-ABI prebuild are mutually exclusive in the build/Release/ folder — local tests fail with ERR_DLOPEN_FAILED if you rebuild for Electron, but CI on Node 20 has matching Node-ABI prebuilds so tests pass there. The CI must order tests BEFORE the rebuild-for-Electron step, then run rebuild, then build/e2e. Second non-obvious takeaway: package.json `build` field cannot contain a `//` comment key — electron-builder validates against schema and rejects unknown properties. Use a top-level non-`build` key (like `_build_note`) for human-readable notes, not the `build` block. Third: eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y 6.9 does NOT support an `allowedDynamic` option on aria-proptypes (verified vs plugin source on disk); the brief's documented Plan B (downgrade rule to warn) was used. allowedDynamic is a ticket-tracker discussion only, not a shipped option.
fix: For ANY Electron + native-module project on a host with the latest Node: (a) install with --ignore-scripts, (b) postinstall: electron-builder install-app-deps, (c) accept that local node-only tests of native-module-using code may fail until the Node-ABI binary is also fetched (CI with Node LTS sidesteps this entirely). Pattern to absorb across projects: CI matrix MUST use Node LTS (20 or 22) not Node 'latest' for any native-module-bearing project — prebuilds lag by months. Also: when authoring electron-builder.yml, NEVER add a `//` comment key inside the package.json `build` block — use a sibling `_build_note` key instead, or omit and rely on the .yml entirely. Promote both lessons to dev-ops-agent Hard-Won Playbook if they recur in a second project.

task: Wave 3.5 remediation H-1 + H-3 + D-2 (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Shipped 4 file edits (src/main/index.ts, src/main/db-bridge.ts, src/ipc/handlers/bookmarks.ts, src/client/state/thunks.ts) + one minimal-touch config expansion (tsconfig.main.json include adds src/db/**/*.ts so the H-1 imports compile). Verification: typecheck:main 0 errors (was 5 TS2379), typecheck:preload clean, vitest src/main+src/ipc 86/86 tests pass including L-001 window-manager enforcement. Meta-lesson: green build + passing tests does NOT mean Phase-1 features work. Two HIGH bugs (H-1 setDbBridge never called, H-3 thunk always sends invalid kind:'ops' payload) shipped through Wave 2 + Wave 3 with full green CI because (a) handler tests stub out the very dep that wasn't wired in production (mocked-deps tests verified handlers reject ops as invalid_payload, which made the test correct AND the renderer broken simultaneously), and (b) the in-memory DbBridge fallback IS the production bridge by accident when nobody calls setDbBridge(). The wiring step is the most-frequently-forgotten line of code in a parallel-build wave. Second observation: when the renderer holds NO original bytes (PDFDocumentModel has no bytes field per conventions section 10, pdf-edit.ts takes originalBytes as a parameter it cannot itself provide), a Phase-1 kind:'bytes' switch requires either (i) a new fs:getDocumentBytes channel (out of scope) or (ii) synthesizing a placeholder document via pdf-lib in the thunk. Chose (ii) inline with a PHASE-1 INLINE comment because pdf-edit.ts is Riley-owned. The Marcus brief favored a main-process applyOpsToBytes shim returning originalBytes unchanged (option 3) which would have preserved the contract-correct kind:'ops' path AND shipped real bytes; the user dispatch overrode to option 2. Both leave Phase-2 with identical engine work.
fix: For ANY Electron/desktop project using a module-singleton-with-set-once pattern (DbBridge, ServiceLocator) AND parallel-build waves: the orchestrator's integration-check turn MUST include a grep for setX( call sites in production bootstrap, not just 'tests pass + build green'. The bug-shape 'memory fallback IS the production bridge by accident' is invisible to unit tests by construction. Second prevention: when an IPC contract surface has two payload kinds and Phase 1 only implements one, the renderer thunk that defaults to the unimplemented kind needs a test that fires the REAL handler with the REAL Phase-1 deps (not the test deps that inject the missing function), so the invalid_payload return is caught. Third prevention: when one agent owns the contract (David) and another owns the consumer thunk (Riley), file ownership for the consumer thunk should temporarily flip to the contract-owner during any wave that touches the payload union — file ownership follows contract-knowledge, not directory boundaries. Fourth: tsconfig.main.json include MUST extend to src/db/**/*.ts whenever the main bootstrap imports a repo factory; the original Wave 3 config omitted src/db and the H-1 fix forces a single include line. Bake into the dev-ops-agent checklist for any Electron + repo-factory project.

task: Wave 3.5 remediation: H-2 factory-selector fix + TS4114 + D-3 (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Fixed H-2 (Redux factory selectors defeating memoization) by replacing selectAnnotationsForPage/selectPage with parameterized createSelector form (state, pageIndex) using Reselect 5's default weakMapMemoize. Updated docs/conventions.md §6.3 to retract the broken factory pattern and document the right pattern with anti-pattern callout. Fixed TS4114 (override modifier needed on ErrorBoundary.state field, not on lifecycle methods — they already had it). Fixed D-3 (selection-slice.test.ts mutating frozen RTK reducer result via .sort() — clone-before-sort). Three NON-OBVIOUS takeaways. First: a code-convention doc that endorses a bug is MORE dangerous than the bug itself — conventions §6.3 had endorsed the factory pattern as the canonical form, so every future selector with a runtime arg would have replicated H-2. Fixing both the code and the doc in the same wave is non-negotiable; either one alone leaves a time bomb. Second: Reselect 5's parameterized createSelector produces a deeply-generic OutputSelector type that, combined with composite:true in tsconfig, makes TypeScript declaration emit walk into every sibling slice's State type and produce TS4023 cascades that flood every other createSelector export in the SAME file. The fix is to isolate parameterized selectors in their own file AND add explicit return-type annotations on the exports — even THEN the cascade can leak across re-exports. Net result: split the parameterized selectors into a separate file (document-parameterized-selectors.ts) with explicit annotations. Reduced the renderer TS4023 count from 92 to 74 — the remaining 74 are pre-existing on other plain createSelector exports and want a Diego Phase-1.1 sweep (drop composite or annotate every export). Third: Diego's reported '1 typecheck:renderer error' was masked by tsbuildinfo cache — the true cold-start renderer typecheck has 74-92 TS4023 errors depending on file layout. Trust cold builds, not warm-cache reports.
fix: For any Redux Toolkit project: convention docs MUST illustrate parameterized selectors with the (state, arg) form using `const selectArg = (_s, arg) => arg` + createSelector input array — never as `(arg) => createSelector(...)`. The factory form is a paper anti-pattern that propagates across the codebase. For any TS project using `composite: true` + Reselect 5: explicit return-type annotations on createSelector exports are the cheap insurance against TS4023 cascades; or drop composite if the build pipeline does not require project references. For any RTK + Immer test that asserts on reducer output: assume the output is frozen and clone before any in-place mutator (.sort/.reverse/.fill/.splice). Add a one-line `// frozen-result: clone before sort` comment so the next reader doesn't strip the spread.

task: Wave 3.5 integration verdict + Wave 4 readiness call (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Verdict GREEN. All 3 of 3 in-scope HIGHs (H-1 setDbBridge wiring, H-2 parameterized selectors + conventions §6.3 retraction, H-3 Save payload switch) closed at the source. Spot-check confirmed all four artefacts match the agents' status rows; cold renderer typecheck verified 74 TS4023 errors are 100% in pre-existing slice files (zero in any Riley-edited file), supporting interpretation (a) latent-cascade for Decision B. Non-obvious takeaway: when 'fix the contract drift' reveals the contract assumes infrastructure that does not yet exist (H-3: renderer Save assumed access to original PDF bytes the renderer never holds, because conventions §10 forbids Uint8Array in store AND no fs:getDocumentBytes channel exists), the right move is to ship a degraded-but-honest Phase-1 boundary (synthesize a placeholder PDF that round-trips the IPC pipe) rather than expand scope mid-wave to add the infrastructure. The architecture is what's being proven — not the fidelity. Phase-2 layers the real engine on the proven pipe. This pattern (degrade-but-honest > scope-expansion mid-wave) needs a name: call it 'walking-skeleton fidelity boundary' — fix the breakage at the wire-level so end-to-end is observable; document the fidelity gap loudly in the user guide; defer the deep work to a labelled phase. The user-guide doc step is non-negotiable: a Save that 'technically works' but doesn't preserve content is a UX trap that destroys trust the moment a user re-opens the saved file. Nathan's Wave 4 brief must call this out as the FIRST honesty obligation. Second takeaway: TS4023 cascades from Reselect 5's deeply-generic OutputSelector under composite:true are MASKED by factory-selector patterns (factory functions are not createSelector outputs at the file's export boundary). Removing the factory pattern can APPEAR to introduce cascade errors when in fact the factory was hiding pre-existing structural issues. Always cold-build to validate cascade attribution; warm tsbuildinfo caches lie. Third takeaway: blocked-on-tooling at the orchestrator level (no Task tool exposed in this session) is a real risk in multi-wave runs — when the orchestrator agent's own dispatch surface is constrained, document the dispatch path for the user explicitly and treat the Wave 4 brief itself as the dispatchable artefact a fresh session can pick up. Brief-as-artefact > orchestrator-as-required-runtime.
fix: For any walking-skeleton fix where the contract-correct path requires renderer-side state the renderer is forbidden to hold (conventions ban on Uint8Array, no fs:get* channel, etc.): default to Phase-1 degrade-but-honest at the wire-level AND require the user guide to surface the fidelity boundary in the SAME wave the fix lands. Never split fix-the-code from fix-the-doc across waves — the doc step is the trust-floor, not the cleanup. For TS4023 cascade triage on any Reselect 5 / composite:true project: cold-build first (delete tsbuildinfo), attribute by file, accept that factory-selector patterns mask cascades. Phase-1.1 sweep is the right scope for the structural fix; never block a wave on a structural cascade. For orchestrator dispatch when subagent tooling is not exposed in-session: treat the wave brief as the dispatchable artefact, document the resume path in the verdict section of the build report, and surface the constraint as a blocker for the user (not a self-correction loop). Brief-as-artefact pattern means the next session can pick up the build from a single document, no orchestrator state required.

task: Wave 4 documentation (PDF_Viewer_Editor Phase 1)
detail: Shipped 5 docs totaling 1544 lines: README.md (120, REPLACED), docs/user-guide.md (338, NEW), docs/developer-guide.md (547, NEW), docs/api-reference.md (342, NEW), LICENSES.md (197, NEW). Phase 1 fidelity boundary (H-3) surfaced in three escalating impressions: README front matter, user-guide top-of-doc Known Limitations section, then again at the point-of-action in the Saving section. Non-obvious takeaway: when a walking-skeleton ships a Save that produces a valid file WITHOUT replaying edits, the natural temptation is to mention this once and move on. That is wrong — the user encounters Save from THREE entry points (Ctrl+S, File menu, Save As) and each of those needs to land on documentation that surfaces the limitation IN THAT FLOW, not buried in a generic limitations appendix. The trust-floor for a Save-related doc is repetition at point-of-action, not a single up-front disclaimer. The README also needs the limitation prominently because that is the front-door for new users. Second takeaway: LICENSES.md is not optional ceremony — the actual node_modules walk surfaced one not-declared license (spawn-command@0.0.2, transitive of concurrently, build-only) plus one MPL-2.0 (axe-core, file-scoped copyleft, compatible) plus a CC-BY-4.0 (caniuse-lite, data not code, attribution-required). All three are acceptable but only because the LICENSES.md doc actually lists them and the analysis is written down — without the doc, a future audit (or a downstream consumer) has no record that these were considered.
fix: For ANY walking-skeleton release where one of the headline features has a documented fidelity gap (Save without edit-replay, render without text-extraction, etc.): the user guide MUST surface the limitation at EVERY entry point that exercises the gap, not just in a generic appendix. Pattern: (a) README front-door mention, (b) user-guide Known Limitations section at TOP of doc (not bottom), (c) restate at point-of-action in the feature's own section, (d) link from any UI tooltip or toast through to the relevant guide anchor. Three impressions minimum. The trust-floor is repetition where the user is about to trip, not honesty up front then silence. For LICENSES.md on any project: walk node_modules directly (not just package.json) because transitive deps surface license issues invisible at the top level. Flag every non-MIT entry explicitly with a one-sentence justification. The doc is the audit trail; without it, the policy compliance claim is unprovable.

task: Phase 1.1 + Phase 2 scoping (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: User locked four decisions in a single turn: MIT license, edit-replay with main-keeps-bytes, replace-only text editing with original font, image-import dual-mode (new page + overlay) with PNG/JPEG/TIFF. Produced three plan-on-disk artefacts (phase-1.1-brief.md, phase-2-plan.md, wave-6-brief.md) and triaged Julian's 14 MEDIUM findings into 1.1-now / Phase-2 / Phase-2.1 / wontfix buckets. Non-obvious meta-insight: the 'main keeps original bytes per handle' decision is the lynchpin that makes everything else in Phase 2 cleanly composable — without it, every feature (text-replace, image-overlay, Print-to-PDF, real Save) requires either a new fs:getDocumentBytes channel (contract expansion + Julian re-audit) or a Uint8Array smuggle into the renderer Redux store (conventions section 10 violation). One architectural decision dissolves three independent design tensions at once. The companion insight: 'replace-only with original font' is the decision that makes Phase 2 actually shippable rather than infinite — reflow + multilanguage shaping is a Phase 4+ effort and including it in Phase 2 would have ballooned the wave from 18-29h to 60-90h with no compounding upside (every feature pre-replace would still ship independently). Cost-discipline through hard scope-fences > comprehensive-but-eternal. Ordering call: Phase 1.1 BEFORE Wave 6, not parallel, because the TS4023 cascade fix means Wave 7 implementation runs against a clean renderer typecheck; running parallel would mix Phase-1.1 latent cascades with Phase-2 new errors during the implementation wave's triage cycles. 1-2h sequential pre-clean is cheap insurance against a multi-hour Wave-7 triage hit.
fix: For any phase-planning turn where the user locks multiple decisions in sequence: identify which decision is the LYNCHPIN (the one that dissolves multiple independent design tensions). Mark it in the plan's locked-decisions table as the lynchpin and write the architectural cascade explicitly — every other feature should compose ON the lynchpin decision, not in parallel with it. Second pattern: identify the SCOPE-FENCE decision (the one that bounds an infinite feature). Document the explicit alternative-rejected (here: 'no reflow + no font substitution' rejects the full text-engine path). Future agents will be tempted to expand scope; the explicit rejection is the durable answer. Third pattern: cleanup waves run BEFORE design waves when the cleanup affects build/typecheck signal quality. Cost-of-mixing-signal in the next implementation wave > cost-of-serializing-the-cleanup. Apply this rule whenever a parallel-vs-sequential phase ordering question comes up after a code-review wave.

task: Wave 5 / Phase 1.1 — N-1.1 LICENSE, N-1.2 LICENSES.md intro, N-1.3 README Phase 1.1 note
detail: Wrote MIT LICENSE (21 lines, SPDX text, copyright `2026 PDF_Viewer_Editor contributors`). Updated LICENSES.md intro paragraph (`Project license: MIT` linking to new LICENSE, plus reinforced 'no AGPL/GPL/commercial' line). Added `### Phase 1.1 updates (2026-05-21)` subsection to README inside `## Phase 1 known limitations` with three bullets (LICENSE link, TS4023 fix → build-report, Phase 2 → phase-2-plan). Also updated stale `## License` block at README:99 that still said 'license to be selected'. Appended Nathan status row to docs/build-report.md.
takeaways: When the project-plan brief carves a single file (LICENSES.md) between two agents (Diego owns spawn-command row; Nathan owns intro paragraph), do the disjoint-section edit immediately and don't wait for the other agent — the lock-free merge works as long as you stay strictly within your assigned section. Re-reading the file before write is the only correctness requirement.

Phase 1.1 brief N-1.3 says 'add a Phase 1.1 row to limitations', but the existing README structure has bulleted lists not rows. A `### Phase 1.1 updates` subsection inside the existing `## Phase 1 known limitations` H2 reads more cleanly than forcing a 'row' into a non-table section. Always honor brief intent over brief surface.

Stale-doc sweep: when adding a LICENSE file, audit the README's existing License section too — it carried a 'to be selected' line that would have shipped contradicting the new LICENSE. The brief didn't explicitly bid this edit, but it's a one-line fix and the same file (within scope) so it's safer to fix than leave dangling. Doc honesty pattern from Wave 4.

task: DV-1.1 Wave 5 expectErr/expectOk helper + refactor 11 handler test files (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Shipped src/ipc/handlers/test-support.ts (57 lines) with expectErr<E>(res, e) and expectOk<T>(res) helpers. Refactored all 11 handler test files (Wave 2 brief said 13; actual disk count is 11 — multi-channel handlers like recents/settings/app/window each share one test file). 63/63 tests still pass; 0 tsc errors. Non-obvious takeaway: the brief's stated motivation (closing the 'silent-pass on wrong variant' risk) is real but ALSO uncovered a different latent test-laxity bug: dialog-save-as.test.ts 'rejects suggestedName containing path separators' had ONLY expect(res.ok).toBe(false) with NO variant check — strictly weaker than the silent-pass pattern, and invisible to a grep that targets only the if (!res.ok) chain. The helper-driven refactor forces every error-path test to declare the specific error code, which surfaces this entire CLASS of incomplete assertions, not just the if-branch class Julian flagged. Recommendation: when porting a 'tighten assertions' helper across a test suite, do a final grep for any remaining bare `expect(res.ok).toBe(false)` without a `.error` follow-up — those are the laxer-than-silent-pass leftovers. Second observation: the `asserts res is { ok:false; ... }` annotation on expectErr lets callers read res.message after the assertion without a redundant if-guard, which is a Phase 2 ergonomics win when handlers carry richer error details payloads.
fix: For ANY parallel-wave 'tighten test assertions' refactor: (1) ship the helper with an `asserts` annotation so callers can drill into the narrowed variant for free; (2) treat the refactor as a TWO-pass grep — first the `if (!res.ok)` chains the brief targets, second a follow-up grep for bare `expect(res.ok).toBe(false)` with no `.error` follow-up (the laxer-than-silent-pass class). Bake into the dispatch brief for the next 'test-quality ratchet' wave so future-David doesn't re-derive. Third prevention: when a wave brief gives an exact file count (here: '13'), do a `find ... | wc -l` BEFORE accepting the count — the actual number was 11 because multi-channel handlers (recents-list/add/clear, settings-get/set/getAll, app's 5 sub-channels, window's 4 channels) each share one test file. Off-by-2 doesn't affect deliverable but the discrepancy is worth surfacing in the status row so the orchestrator's brief generator learns to count file-shape, not channel-count.

task: Wave 5 (Phase 1.1) cleanup — drop composite:true, resolve spawn-command license
detail: D-1.1: dropping `composite: true` from tsconfig.renderer.json collapsed the entire 74-error TS4023 cascade (cold typecheck went 74 -> 0). Riley's Wave-3.5 hypothesis about Reselect 5 OutputSelector x composite-declaration-emit was mechanically correct, but the fix did NOT require any (state: RootState) => T return-type annotations on the existing createSelector exports — the composite drop alone is sufficient because tsc --noEmit no longer asks the declaration emitter to NAME the transitive *State types at the export boundary. fix-option #2 from the Marcus brief was not needed. D-1.2: bumping concurrently 8.2.2 -> 9.2.1 dropped spawn-command@0.0.2 entirely (concurrently 9.x replaced spawn-command with tree-kill + Node child_process). License walk after the bump: 0 undeclared-license packages remain (down from 1), 0 GPL/LGPL/AGPL/EPL. Path (a) was unambiguously the right choice: concurrently is a devDep we don't even invoke from any script (grep confirms zero usage in package.json scripts / CI YAML), so the upgrade risk was zero. D-1.3: no CI changes required — CI uses npm run typecheck (per-project --noEmit), not tsc -b; the lockfile bump is picked up by npm ci's cache key.
fix: For ANY future Electron + Reselect + multi-project tsconfig stack: if you typecheck via tsc --noEmit and don't consume the renderer config from project-reference declaration emit, `composite: true` is a footgun on the renderer. It forces declaration emission, which trips TS4023 on every createSelector export that transits a slice boundary because TS tries to NAME every transitive *State type the OutputSelector's deeply-generic signature references. Default to composite:false (the TS default) on any renderer/UI-only tsconfig; enable it explicitly only when you have a downstream .d.ts consumer. SEPARATELY: when a 'transitive with no declared license' shows up, check the immediate parent dep's release notes for a major-version bump BEFORE opening upstream issues — the cheapest fix is often a parent bump. concurrently 9.0.0 dropped spawn-command over a year ago; we were still on 8.2.2 because nothing forced an upgrade. Generalisable across projects.

task: Wave 5 / Phase 1.1 R-1.1 — F1 Help in-app modal (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Shipped HelpModal (component + CSS module + 7-case Vitest spec) + wired F1 in use-app-shortcuts.ts + extended ModalKind union with 'help' + added openHelpModal/closeHelpModal convenience action creators + added selectHelpModalOpen selector + rendered HelpModal in app.tsx + replaced Help-menu Documentation entry with Help (F1). Touched 10 files (3 NEW + 7 EDIT). 33-row shortcut table hand-ported from docs/user-guide.md; 4-bullet Phase 1 limitations summary leading with Save fidelity boundary; 6-bullet Phase 2-7 roadmap. Verification: typecheck:renderer 0 errors, vitest 61/61 incl. 7 new HelpModal cases, ESLint clean on all Riley files. Non-obvious takeaways: (1) The brief asked for `helpModalOpen: boolean` UiState but the existing codebase pattern is `activeModal: ModalKind` (single-modal-at-a-time union). Honoring the existing pattern by extending the union AND exposing `openHelpModal/closeHelpModal/selectHelpModalOpen` as thin convenience wrappers satisfies the literal brief API surface without forking the modal state model. Always treat orchestrator briefs as INTENT — when the literal surface conflicts with an existing pattern, layer to honor both. (2) The brief said modal-shell handles click-outside dismiss; it didn't. The acceptance criterion required it. Adding click-outside to modal-shell (not just HelpModal) is the cheaper long-term decision because every existing modal benefits with zero source changes — the right boundary for cross-cutting interaction patterns is the shared shell. The a11y wrinkle: jsx-a11y/no-noninteractive-element-interactions flags onMouseDown on a div with role='dialog'. Split role='dialog' onto the inner panel and use role='presentation' on the overlay backdrop — that's the clean a11y boundary, not eslint-disable. (3) shortcuts.ts has an `enabledInPhase1` flag the matcher does NOT consult — it's documentation-only. F1 was already being matched and routed to a catch-all 'coming soon' toast bucket. Flipping the flag is cosmetic; the real wire is the dispatch switch arm. Worth a future audit to either consume the flag in the matcher or delete it.
fix: For ANY future React/Redux modal addition where the brief proposes new state but the codebase has an existing modal-state pattern: respect the existing pattern AND expose the brief's named API surface as convenience wrappers. Don't fork. For click-outside dismiss patterns on a shared modal-shell: implement on the shell, not per-modal — one site, cross-modal benefit. For jsx-a11y/no-noninteractive-element-interactions on overlay backdrops: split role='dialog' onto the inner panel and use role='presentation' on the backdrop. For Phase-flag fields in shortcut/feature registries: if the runtime doesn't consume the flag, it's documentation-only — either wire it or remove it; a flag that lies about runtime behavior is a worse-than-no-flag situation. Promote to convention if it recurs.

task: Wave 6 Phase 2 architecture (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Produced 2 NEW design docs (edit-replay-engine.md 857 lines, architecture-phase-2.md 641 lines) + 4 amended docs (api-contracts §12 +297, data-models §7 +287, ui-spec §11 +295, conventions §13 +198) = 2575 lines of Phase-2 design. All 4 locked decisions (P2-L-2/3/4/6) encoded in the doc set per matrix in build-report row. All 7 phase-2-plan §7 open questions answered with concrete designs. Non-obvious takeaway: the 'main keeps original bytes per handle' lynchpin (P2-L-2) composes perfectly with the existing Phase-1 src/main/pdf-ops/document-store.ts — Phase 2 adds two methods (getBytes/setBytes) + one Uint8Array slot, NO structural refactor. The conventions §10 ban on renderer-side Uint8Array gets STRENGTHENED rather than weakened, because the explicit 'main owns the bytes' rule plus history-slice compaction (§13.3 image-bytes-zero-on-history-push pattern) creates a single audit-able boundary. Second non-obvious takeaway: bookmarks ops deliberately do NOT join the EditOperation union despite being mutations — they flow through bookmarks:* IPC + SQLite directly. Justification: bookmarks don't live in PDF bytes, and coupling bookmark CRUD to PDF re-emission breaks the read-then-bookmark UX. This is the 'natural-boundary' lesson from David Wave 2's db-bridge.ts pattern — the right boundary follows DATA OWNERSHIP, not feature category. Third takeaway: when Phase 2 introduces a fidelity boundary (text-replace clipping + missing-glyph), the failure-mode UX MUST live in the renderer (font-metrics shim) not main, because per-keystroke IPC round-trips for measurement would tank the editor. Cached glyph widths from pdf:identifyTextSpan response work because glyph metrics are tiny JSON (~1KB), not bytes — conventions §10 ban survives intact. The Phase-2 design avoided three classes of architectural debt: (a) no fs:getDocumentBytes channel needed (would have leaked bytes to renderer), (b) no separate text-preview channel needed (would have been an IPC chatfest), (c) no separate bookmark-EditOperation union needed (would have coupled doc-save to bookmark-save in the wrong direction).
fix: For any future Phase-N architecture wave where a 'lynchpin' decision dissolves multiple design tensions: explicitly check whether the existing Phase-(N-1) module can absorb the change with additive surface only. If yes, do not propose a new module — extend in place and document the lifecycle additions. document-store.ts went from metadata-only Phase 1 to bytes-bearing Phase 2 with TWO new methods and ONE new field; no rename, no replacement, no parallel module. For any feature that ISN'T a PDF-byte mutation (here: bookmarks), keep it off the EditOperation union. The discriminator question is 'does this mutation produce different bytes in the saved PDF?' If yes, it's an EditOperation. If no (bookmarks, settings, recents, viewport state), it lives in its own slice + IPC namespace. Coupling everything into one union is the wrong simplification — Phase 5+ multi-document and Phase 3 forms will further validate this split. For renderer-side failure-mode UX that needs cheap data from main: ship the data ONCE in the identify/lookup response (here: glyph widths from pdf:identifyTextSpan), cache renderer-side keyed by the identifier, never per-keystroke IPC. Generalize across projects: any 'live preview' feature that depends on server-side state should fetch a metric bundle on entry and measure locally.

task: Wave 7 Phase 2 database (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Shipped migrations/0002_phase2_bookmarks.sql + extended bookmarks-repo (listTree, move w/ cycle detection, rename) + extended types (BookmarkRow Phase-2 cols, BookmarkNodeRow, Phase-2 SettingKey extension). 27 new tests across 5 describe blocks. tsc clean for src/db. Non-obvious takeaway #1: data-models §7.3 specified an INSERT INTO schema_migrations(version=2,...) inside the migration SQL, but 0001 does NOT do that — the migrate.ts runner records the row in the wrapping transaction. Including it in 0002 would cause a PRIMARY KEY conflict on first run. The doc spec was illustrative; the actual Phase-1 PATTERN (which I matched) is omit-and-let-runner-record. This is exactly the 'trust the doc... unless the pattern says otherwise' tension Wave 2 raised. When the doc and the existing code contradict on a mechanical detail, the code's pattern wins; the deviation is documented in build-report and the doc should be amended later. Non-obvious takeaway #2: the brief said data-models §7.5 has `move(...): boolean` but api-contracts §12.6 demands a `'cycle_detected'` error variant the IPC layer must distinguish. Boolean can't carry that info. Returned a discriminated MoveBookmarkResult union — the right boundary because David's bridge maps repo result directly to IPC Result without try/catch. Treat cross-doc contradictions as drift to resolve, not pick-one. Non-obvious takeaway #3: SettingKey extension was NOT explicitly in my brief but was required to unblock tsc because David already wired the four Phase-2 keys (export.deterministic, export.includeBookmarksInOutline, editing.confirmDelete, editing.commitTextOnBlur) into src/ipc/contracts.ts; structural-type compatibility of SettingsRepo fails if src/db/types.ts lags. data-models §7.6 + api-contracts §12.9 specify these keys; src/db/types.ts is the canonical registry per Phase-1 convention. Took ownership and added them — this is the parallel-wave coordination payoff: when David lands the IPC-side, Ravi MUST land the DB-side in the same wave for typecheck to converge.
fix: For future Phase-N parallel waves where David lands IPC contract additions and Ravi owns the DB types: scan ipc/contracts.ts for SettingKey / RowDto / channel-name unions and check that src/db/types.ts mirrors them. Mismatches are a structural-typing time bomb. Make the SettingKey/SettingValue/KNOWN_SETTING_KEYS triple-update part of every wave checklist that adds new settings. Also: for any new migration that requires ALTER TABLE (non-idempotent in SQLite), match the existing 0001 convention of NO explicit INSERT INTO schema_migrations — the runner handles it. If a doc spec includes that INSERT in the SQL, treat it as illustrative; verify against 0001 before copying.

task: Wave 7 Phase 2 main-process — replay engine + 6 new IPC channels (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Shipped 4 NEW main-process modules (replay-engine.ts, text-replace.ts, image-embed.ts, tiff-decoder.ts), 7 NEW IPC handlers (fs:applyEditOps, pdf:embedImage, pdf:replaceText, pdf:identifyTextSpan, pdf:print, pdf:export Live, bookmarks-phase2 trio), 11 NEW test files covering 103 new tests (189/189 total pass; 0 regressions from Wave 5's 86). The H-3 PHASE-1 INLINE block in src/client/state/thunks.ts is REMOVED — saveDocumentThunk now calls api.fs.applyEditOps(...) directly. Non-obvious takeaway: the H-3 lesson (Wave 3.5: green-build-can-hide-broken-features) shaped the test strategy this wave — added a golden-bytes DETERMINISM test that asserts two replay() invocations over the same input produce byte-equal output. If a pdf-lib upgrade breaks determinism, the test fails loudly. The deterministic round-trip is what makes the H-3 walking-skeleton goal #8 truly verifiable: real ops in -> real bytes out -> reload-stable. Second observation: the conservative dispatch-stub pattern (Chromium export engine + pdf:print dispatchPrint adapter return engine_failed_chromium / print_dispatch_failed) keeps the channel surface fully type-checked and unit-tested while deferring the Electron-window plumbing to Diego's Wave 8 — the renderer can wire to the channels NOW and see honest error toasts until Wave 8 lights up the real adapter. Third observation: adaptSettingsRepo absorbs cross-wave SettingKey skew with Ravi (Phase-2 keys silently degrade to null/no-op until Ravi widens her union) — pattern locks in a 'try-then-degrade-on-unknown-key' adapter for any parallel David+Ravi wave that grows a settings union.
fix: For any Wave introducing N new IPC channels where ONE upstream piece (Electron BrowserWindow / native dialog / etc.) is owned by Diego in a LATER wave: ship the channel + the validate-and-replay layer LIVE now, and stub ONLY the final adapter call with a typed Result-fail variant. The renderer-side test/dev experience improves dramatically because they can wire to a real channel + see a real error toast, instead of a 'channel not found' surprise on day 1 of the integration. Lock pattern: handlerDeps.dispatchPrint / .chromiumExport return Result<_, errorCode> stubs in register.ts with a comment 'Phase-2 conservative; Wave 8 wires the real adapter.' Second prevention: any new EditOperation variant SHOULD also extend the engine's defaultPickEngine heuristic in pdf-export-pdf.ts — locked overlay-count threshold (>10 -> pdf-lib direct write) + text-replace ALWAYS -> pdf-lib (Chromium would font-substitute, violating P2-L-3). Third prevention: when adding native deps that are dev-only or installed-later (utif here), use a dynamic-import with a STRING VARIABLE for the module name (NOT a string literal) so TypeScript's strict module-resolution doesn't fail the build — let utifModule = 'utif'; const m = await import(utifModule). The graceful-degradation branch in tiff-decoder.ts then returns 'tiff_decoder_unavailable' until Diego adds the dep.

task: Wave 7 Phase 2 renderer (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Shipped 6 NEW files (image-import-modal + text-edit-overlay × 3 each), 1 REWRITE (bookmarks-panel), 22 EDITs across slices/middleware/components. Renderer typecheck 0 errors. 86/86 renderer tests pass (61 pre-existing + 25 new: 9 component + 13 bookmarks-slice + 3 history-middleware). 0 lint errors in Riley files. Three non-obvious takeaways. First: David's Phase-2 contract types landed during this same wave (parallel dispatch), so the renderer gatekeeper module collapsed from a forward-declaration scaffold back to a pure re-export — same Wave 2 lesson #5 vindicated. Critically, David's H-3 inline-removal of saveDocumentThunk direct-imported `FsApplyEditOpsRequest` from `../../ipc/contracts` BYPASSING the renderer gatekeeper. Caught it and re-routed through `src/client/types/ipc-contract.ts`. Generalisable lesson: when a contract owner edits a consumer file in parallel, the consumer's gatekeeper boundary degrades silently — it must be actively defended on every touch, NOT assumed to be self-enforcing. ESLint's no-restricted-imports rule (Diego Wave 3) is the long-term enforcement but only kicks in on next lint pass. Second non-obvious takeaway: the history middleware's re-entrancy flag (`meta.__history: true`) is the load-bearing detail. When undo dispatches `applyEdit(inverse)`, the middleware re-enters — without the flag it would push another history entry, infinite loop, store corruption. Same pattern as RTK's `createListenerMiddleware` internal guard. Pin this into the conventions §6.5 (undo metadata) when Phase 2 closeout audits the docs. Third takeaway: Phase-2 EditOperation variants compose perfectly with the existing applyOperationToDocument exhaustive switch — three of the five new variants (image-overlay, -edit, -delete) are PRESENTATIONAL-only at the renderer (no PageModel mutation; canvas overlay reads from dirtyOps until next save round-trip). Only `image-insert` (new-page) mutates pages. `text-replace` is also presentational because PageModel doesn't store run text — engine resolves objectId at save time. This validates the architecture-phase-2.md §4 split: renderer owns the operation log; main owns the bytes; round-trip refresh closes the loop. No need for a renderer-side replay engine in Phase 2 — saved Wave 8 significant scope.
fix: For ANY parallel-wave where two agents edit the same file in their respective domains (contract-owner + consumer-thunk-owner): the consumer agent must actively defend their boundary file (gatekeeper module) on every touch, not assume the existing ESLint config will catch it on the next lint pass. Run a quick `grep -n 'from .../ipc/contracts' src/client/` after every parallel-wave handoff to detect direct imports that should route through the gatekeeper. Two complementary techniques: (a) the ESLint rule (long-term enforcement), (b) the per-wave grep (in-wave catch). Belt + suspenders. Second prevention: for ANY Redux middleware that dispatches re-entrant actions (history undo/redo, optimistic update rollbacks, etc.), the re-entrant action MUST carry a flag like `meta.__history: true` and the middleware MUST check for the flag at the top of the function. Document this as a convention in §6.5 (undo metadata). Without it, the middleware will re-enter its own push branch, causing infinite loops or stack corruption. Third prevention: when a Phase-2+ feature adds EditOperation variants that don't mutate the renderer's state model (text-replace, image overlays), document them as PRESENTATIONAL in the applyOperationToDocument switch — leave the case branches empty with explicit comments. The exhaustive `never` assertion in the default branch still catches missing variants; the empty cases make the architectural split (renderer = operation log, main = bytes) visible at code-review time without scattering the rationale across multiple docs.

task: Wave 7 integration verdict + Wave 8 readiness call (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Verdict YELLOW (proceed). 11/11 on-disk spot-checks PASS (replay-engine purity, gatekeeper boundary held, H-3 closed, migration DDL matches, atomic-save present, SettingKey union zero-drift, file ownership clean, utif MIT verified). The 5 known follow-ups from agent rows triage into FIX-NOW Diego (utif dep, Chromium adapter, print adapter) + ACCEPT-defer (text-span scanner Phase 2.5) + ACCEPT-coordination (Ravi's SettingKey pre-emption was zero-drift with David). One NEW finding from spot-check that NONE of the three Wave 7 agent rows flagged: the bridge collapses Ravi's MoveBookmarkResult discriminated union to boolean, then bookmarks-phase2 handler heuristically infers cycle_detected from request shape — mis-classifies legitimate not_found-with-non-null-parent. This is the schema-deviation-becomes-contract pattern: Ravi shipped a BETTER shape than docs/data-models.md §7.5 spec (boolean), but the downstream consumer (David's bridge) re-typed it as the OLD spec shape, defeating the improvement. Three-way fix in Wave 8.5: amend doc (reality-amendment > spec-retraction per Wave 2 lesson #5), patch bridge to preserve union, patch handler to switch on variant. Non-obvious meta-insight: when an upstream agent ships a better-than-spec shape AND a parallel downstream agent compiles against the OLD spec shape, the type system passes (boolean is assignable from a strict subset of the union once you cast) — the bug is invisible at typecheck. The integration-check turn is the ONLY catch surface; no green-build signal would have flagged it. This generalizes the Wave 3.5 'green build + tests can hide a broken feature' lesson: green build + tests + matching agent status rows can ALSO hide a contract drift when a structural-typing match is too permissive at the boundary cast. Second insight: a Wave-N 'parallel + matching' that produces a contract-improving-deviation needs the integration-check to explicitly diff upstream-shipped shape vs downstream-consumed shape, not just upstream-vs-spec.
fix: For ANY parallel-wave where one agent ships a richer-than-spec shape (Ravi: MoveBookmarkResult union > spec boolean) and a downstream agent in the same wave consumes via the spec shape (David: bridge typed move as boolean): the integration-check turn MUST cross-grep the UPSTREAM ACTUAL signature against the DOWNSTREAM ADAPTER signature, not just upstream-vs-spec. Permissive structural typing (boolean acceptable from a forced cast of the discriminated union) means the typecheck NEVER catches this. Locked pattern for Marcus integration-check: (1) for every agent X that produces a typed surface consumed by agent Y in the same wave, run a 3-way diff: docs/spec vs src/X-output vs src/Y-input. (2) Where the three disagree, identify whether (a) X over-shipped vs spec (improvement candidate — amend spec) or (b) Y under-consumed vs X (drift candidate — patch consumer). Both shapes are real issues even when typecheck is green. Generalisable across projects: any time agent A ships 'better than docs says' and agent B compiles against docs's older shape with a cast, type-system silence is structurally guaranteed at the boundary cast. The catch surface is exclusively human review of cross-agent signatures during integration. Bake into orchestration-manager Hard-Won Playbook.

task: Wave 8 Phase 2 code review (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Audited 38 of ~60 Phase 2 source files (David's pdf-ops + handlers + db-bridge + preload, Ravi's bookmarks-repo + migration, Riley's history-middleware + slices + components + thunks). Findings: 2 BLOCKER + 5 HIGH + 11 MEDIUM + 9 LOW + 4 NIT. Verdict RED — Wave 8.5 fixup mandatory before Wave 9. Two structural defects in replay engine: (1) applyReorder calls removePage without re-insert (reorder becomes deletion at save); (2) applyInsert no-ops on source.kind='original'|'image'|'inserted' (undo-of-delete silently drops the restored page at save). Renderer-side ops apply correctly so UI looks right during the session — corruption surfaces only after save+reload. Both blockers slipped past 12-case unit-test suite because no test exercises reorder or undo-of-delete round-trip. Third HIGH is the MoveBookmarkResult discriminated-union boundary collapse Marcus flagged in Wave 7 verdict — confirmed HIGH (not MEDIUM): invalid_parent variant completely unreachable, not_found mis-classified as cycle_detected. Fourth HIGH is the .eslintrc.cjs no-restricted-imports pattern group missing src/ipc/contracts — Riley's manual Wave 7 catch on David's drift was load-bearing but the lint cited by conventions §4.3 doesn't enforce it.
fix: For future code review: when auditing a 'replay engine' or any pure-function transformation over a fixed set of operation variants, do not trust passing unit tests alone. Walk through the inverse-of-X composition explicitly for every variant: for each EditOperation kind, trace forward op → undo (via inverseOf) → redo → save → reload → assert byte-equivalence with the no-undo-no-redo baseline. Phase-2 lesson: the renderer-model engine and the save-time engine are TWO IMPLEMENTATIONS that must stay congruent; divergence between them is silent data loss. The Wave 7 test gap was 'no reorder test + no undo-of-delete round-trip' — these are the two test cases that should exist for any future op-list system. Promote to global JSONL because this 'two implementations of the same op set' pattern appears in Electron-Redux apps, Yjs CRDT systems, and any client-server eventual-consistency layer. Also worth promoting: when an interface return type is a discriminated union and downstream consumers want a boolean, FLAG ANY COLLAPSE — the heuristic-to-recover-variant pattern (bookmarks-phase2.ts:64-76) is a future-bug magnet because tests will encode the heuristic instead of catching the contract violation.

task: Wave 8 Phase 2 platform — utif + Chromium export + Print dispatch + H-3 retirement smoke (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Shipped 4 new files (print-window.ts + .test.ts + h3-retirement.test.ts + the LICENSES.md row) and 6 surgical edits (package.json utif dep, register.ts dep swap from stub to real factory, electron-builder.yml defensive negation, 2 handler tests, smoke.spec.ts extension). All 8 brief tasks (D-8.1 through D-8.8) complete. typecheck 0 errors x3 projects. 296/296 tests pass in main+ipc+client (was 275 baseline -> +21 new). Critically D-8.7 H-3 retirement smoke PASSES: a synthesized 3-page PDF goes through real handleFsApplyEditOps -> replay() -> atomic temp-rename pipeline; reloads show page count dropped from 3 -> 2, page 2 rotated 90 deg. The walking-skeleton fidelity boundary from Phase 1 is RETIRED at the engine+handler integration level. L-001 holds on BOTH the main window AND the new offscreen print/export BrowserWindow factory. Three non-obvious takeaways. First: when a Wave-N agent (David) ships a handler with PROPER dependency injection (chromiumExport + dispatchPrint deps), the Wave-N+1 wiring agent (Diego) can do surgical 2-line edits at the REGISTRATION site (register.ts) rather than ANY edit to the handler files themselves. The brief authorized handler edits as a precaution; the actual wiring needed zero handler edits because the dep interface was already correct. Saved ~40 lines of cross-agent file touches AND preserved David's test surface untouched. Second non-obvious takeaway: the H-3 retirement smoke is BEST written as a vitest unit-level integration test against the real engine + handler + filesystem, NOT as a Playwright Electron e2e flow. The Playwright path requires dialog mocking that is notoriously fragile in Electron-driver land; the unit-level integration uses dependency injection to wire the real engine + a Map-backed mock store + real fsPromises against a temp directory. Result: the H-3 closure proof runs in ~50ms, deterministic, no Electron boot. Generalizes: for cross-module integration tests that hit the filesystem, the pluggable-deps pattern beats the boot-the-real-app pattern every time. Third takeaway: L-001 was originally locked against ONE BrowserWindow factory (the main window in window-manager.ts). When Wave 8 added a SECOND factory (print-window.ts), the lock had to be REPLICATED in a new test file with the same assertions. The principle 'uniform application of the lock across all factories' is what makes audit-trail uniform — even though the new factory creates a HIDDEN window that never accepts user input, omitting enableDragDropFiles is the audit-friendly choice (rather than explicitly setting it to true or false). Pattern: any time a new BrowserWindow factory is added, add a security-floor test that asserts the L-001 invariant alongside the rest of the security floor. Lint and typecheck will NOT catch this — only a dedicated test will.
fix: For ANY Wave-N+1 wiring agent inheriting a Wave-N agent's dependency-injection-style handler: ALWAYS look at the handler's dep interface BEFORE assuming you need to edit the handler file. If the upstream agent did the right thing (pluggable factory + Result-typed return), the wiring is a 2-line swap at the registration site. The brief's authorization to edit handler files is a fallback in case the dep interface needs widening; it should not be the default action. Second prevention: for any new BrowserWindow factory added in a future wave, the locked-instruction (L-001 in this project) MUST be re-asserted in a dedicated test file paired with the factory. The test asserts both the lock invariant (enableDragDropFiles !== false) AND the full security floor (contextIsolation, sandbox, no nodeIntegration, webSecurity, allowRunningInsecureContent) so a degraded webPreferences object cannot accidentally pass the lock test alone. Treat the lock as a per-factory contract, not a per-app contract. Third prevention: for the H-3 / walking-skeleton-fidelity-retirement smoke at the close of any Phase-N+1 wave: write a vitest-level integration test that uses dependency injection to wire the real engine + handler + filesystem against a temp directory. Synthesize fixtures via the same library the engine uses (pdf-lib here), assert on the round-trip (load -> edit -> save -> reload -> verify). This proves the H-N closure at the algorithmic level and runs in tens of milliseconds. A Playwright e2e is the boots-the-real-app proof; pair the two when both fit the wave budget, but the integration test is the load-bearing assertion.

task: Wave 8.5 H-2 ESLint no-restricted-imports patch (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Three-line additive patch to .eslintrc.cjs (technically 9 lines with the Wave-8.5/§G provenance comment) closes Julian's Phase 2 §G HIGH finding. The Wave 3 config already had the renderer-boundary override with excludedFiles:['src/client/types/ipc-contract.ts'] in place — the gatekeeper-exclusion plumbing was correct from day one; only the pattern list missed src/ipc/contracts. Adding the entry was a one-edit fix. Negative test fired the exact custom message on a deliberately violating temp file. Full npm run lint regression: zero — error count matches Wave 8 baseline (10) exactly. Non-obvious takeaway: boundary rules documented in convention docs (conventions §4.3 'ESLint no-restricted-imports enforces') drift from reality silently when the lint plumbing exists but a pattern entry is missing. Riley's Wave 7 manual review CAUGHT David's drift, but the convention's claim of automatic enforcement was a doc-vs-code lie for five waves. This is the third project where 'docs say X is enforced; actually a manual review caught the only violation' has shown up — the pattern is: when convention docs assert ESLint enforcement, an agent should grep the actual ESLint config in the same pass and either (a) confirm the rule exists with the right pattern, or (b) flag it for the platform owner. The reviewer (Julian) did exactly this on the Phase 2 pass, which is why it surfaced. Second observation: relative-path globs matter — the renderer imports via '../../ipc/contracts' (two-dot, no /src prefix), so the brief's '**/ipc/contracts' shape is the load-bearing pattern; '**/src/ipc/contracts' would NOT catch the actual import shape today. Added both as belt-and-suspenders. Third: dynamic import('.../ipc/contracts') would bypass no-restricted-imports in ESLint 8 legacy — not in scope here but worth a note for the Phase 2.5 flat-config migration to swap in eslint-plugin-import's no-restricted-paths.
fix: For ANY project that documents 'ESLint enforces boundary X' in a conventions doc: code-reviewers and dev-ops agents should grep the actual ESLint config for the rule + pattern in the SAME pass that touches the doc. The doc-vs-config drift is invisible to lint/CI by construction (lint passes because the rule's there with a TRUNCATED pattern list). The reviewer (Julian) did this on Phase 2; bake the check into code-review-agent's standing playbook so future projects catch it Phase 1 rather than Phase 2. Second prevention: when ANY agent owns ESLint config (Wave 3 platform), add a brief section to the build-report listing every boundary pattern enforced + every glob in each pattern group, so a future reviewer can diff doc claims against the table without re-reading .eslintrc.cjs. Saves Julian's 5-wave drift window. Third prevention: pattern globs should be DOUBLE-EXPRESSED (with and without /src prefix) when the project mixes relative imports and aliased imports — '**/ipc/contracts*' AND '**/src/ipc/contracts*' as belt-and-suspenders. Cheap; covers the case where a future tsconfig alias is added and the convention is preserved.

task: Wave 8.5 B-2 inverse-map fix (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Fixed B-2 renderer-half: src/client/state/slices/document-inverses.ts `case 'delete'` now branches on op.preservedSource.kind. When the deleted page was an image-page (preservedSource.kind === 'image'), the inverse emits `image-insert` (not generic `insert`), routing the restore through David's applyImageInsert at save time. For original/blank/inserted source kinds, the uniform data-models.md §3.2 contract still applies — emit `insert`. Tests: new file document-inverses.test.ts with 8 cases (4 per-variant emission + 3 round-trip identity + 1 annotation behavior pin). Full renderer suite 94/94 pass (was 86, +8 new, 0 regressions). Renderer typecheck 0 errors. Three non-obvious takeaways. First: the brief proposed adding a `PageModel.provenance` field if missing — turned out unnecessary. PageModel.sourcePageRef (Phase 1 type) already encodes provenance for every source kind including the Phase 2 `image` variant, and the delete dispatcher at thumbnail-strip/index.tsx:89 already copies page.sourcePageRef → op.preservedSource directly. Saved a model widening + matching apply-slice churn. Second: data-models.md §7.1.3 only spells out `image-insert → delete (preservedSource: image)` as the FORWARD inverse — it omits the reverse direction (`delete-of-image-page → image-insert`). The omission is what allowed the bug to ship cleanly through Wave 7 review. Lesson: an inverse-table spec MUST be bidirectional (both directions explicit) or it's an under-specified contract that compilers can't catch. Cross-reference: the Phase-1 §3.2 table HAS this symmetry (both `insert→delete` AND `delete→insert` rows are listed); the Phase-2 §7.1.3 amendment only added forward rows. Marcus may want to amend §7.1.3 to include the four reverse-direction rows for completeness. Third: the history middleware's compaction order matters for the image-insert inverse — middleware calls compactImageOpForHistory on BOTH fwd and inv AFTER inverseOf returns. So when the inverse `image-insert` reaches main on a redo dispatch, its `image.bytes` may be Uint8Array(0). David's applyImageInsert MUST look up by contentHash, not consume bytes from op payload. Flagged this for David in build-report contract-observations row.
fix: For the inverse-map / provenance-aware op problem: when a forward op (e.g. image-insert) and its 'undo' partner (delete) have an inverse table that says `forward → delete { preservedSource: ... }`, ALWAYS also explicitly document `delete { preservedSource: X } → image-insert` (the reverse direction) in the same table. Asymmetric inverse tables hide ship-blockers because each direction unit-tests separately. Bake into data-models doc-template: 'every inverse table must list every kind in BOTH directions, not just forward.' Second prevention: when a renderer's delete dispatcher captures preservedSource from PageModel.sourcePageRef, the inverse computation MUST branch on preservedSource.kind to choose the correct restore-op variant. A naive `inverse-of-delete = insert` is only correct for the kinds whose `apply<kind>` handler is the single restore path; once Phase 2 added a SEPARATE applyImageInsert (because image pages need image-cache lookup), the inverse computation gained a fork. Bake into conventions §6.5: 'when adding a new apply-X handler that creates pages with a distinct SourcePageRef variant, audit inverseOf(delete) for the corresponding fork.' Third prevention: when computing an inverse that carries an ImageEmbedPayload (or any Uint8Array), assume the bytes will be compacted (zeroed) before main consumes the op. Main-side handlers MUST look up bytes via contentHash from a long-lived cache (per-handle DocumentStore image-cache), never trust op.image.bytes to be non-empty.

task: Wave 8.5 BLOCKER remediation B-1 + B-2 + H-1 (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Fixed two structurally-incomplete op handlers in src/main/pdf-ops/replay-engine.ts that silently destroyed user data on the most common Phase-2 edit flows (reorder, undo-of-delete-original-page). applyReorder used removePage() with no re-insertion -> page silently deleted; applyInsert no-op'd for source.kind in {original, image, inserted} -> undo-of-delete didn't survive the save. Both shipped with 296/296 tests green because the test surface was structurally incomplete: zero coverage of reorder (verified by grep) and zero coverage of insert-from-original. This is the H-3 lesson (Wave 3.5) recurring exactly: green build hides broken feature. The meta-lesson is sharper than 'add more tests': the RIGHT recurrence prevention is a *paired test contract* between the renderer slice and the engine. Riley's document-slice-apply.ts:applyReorder splices+reindexes correctly, but no golden test EVER round-tripped that op through the engine to assert the on-disk page count was preserved. Two implementations of the same operation diverged in production with zero alarm because they had no shared golden test. Generalisable to any project where the same conceptual mutation is implemented in two layers (e.g. optimistic UI + server, renderer + main process, in-memory model + DB). Fix approach for B-1: capture PDFPage ref via doc.getPage(fromIndex) BEFORE removePage, then doc.insertPage(toIndex, captured) — pdf-lib's insertPage(index, page) accepts a PDFPage and re-wires the parent ref via catalog.insertLeafNode, verified against node_modules source. Fix approach for B-2 'original': lazily load a SECOND PDFDocument from ctx.originalBytes (memoized on context for multi-op chains), then doc.copyPages(originalDoc, [origIdx]) + insertPage(atIndex, copied). Made applyInsert async; applyOp was already async so the await ripples cleanly. H-1 fix: defined MoveBookmarkResult locally in src/main/db-bridge.ts (David's boundary file, not imported from src/db to keep cross-wave compile-path isolation), widened 3 sites (BookmarksRepo interface, MemoryBookmarksRepo impl, RaviBookmarksRepoPhase2 interface, adaptBookmarksRepo wrapper), then switched on variant in the handler with an exhaustive-never guard. Mapped repo 'invalid_parent' -> IPC 'invalid_payload' because api-contracts §12.6's BookmarksMoveError union doesn't include 'invalid_parent' as a variant and the brief explicitly forbids touching api-contracts.md. invalid_parent semantically reads as 'the parent reference is invalid' which is a structural-input failure -> 'invalid_payload' is the closest match. Test rewrite: REPLACED (not extended) the two heuristic-encoding tests in bookmarks-phase2.test.ts that Julian flagged as 'future-bug magnet' — extending them would have ratcheted in the wrong-shaped contract.
fix: For ANY future Phase-N wave where the same conceptual mutation is implemented in TWO layers (renderer slice + main engine, optimistic UI + server, etc.): the orchestrator brief MUST require AT LEAST ONE end-to-end golden-round-trip test PER OPERATION VARIANT. The brief language to add is: 'For each EditOperation kind (or equivalent two-layer operation), write a test that applies the op via the renderer/client layer, serializes the result to the wire format, applies it via the main/server layer, deserializes back, and asserts identity on a structural property the operation is supposed to preserve (page count, content hash, primary key, etc.).' Single-layer unit tests are NECESSARY but not SUFFICIENT — the contract violation B-1 + B-2 represent only manifests in the layer-crossing fold. Lock this as a meta-rule in conventions §13.6 (or equivalent). Second prevention: when a single-op handler hits an early-return 'ok' with a comment like 'Phase-N conservative — handled by another variant' or 'this is a bridge', that is ALWAYS a code smell. The handler either does work or it doesn't; an ok-return with no side-effect is silent data loss disguised as politeness. Add a lint or grep ratchet: any `return ok(undefined)` in a handler file MUST be either (a) the genuine no-op (e.g. annotation-ops dispatching to step 4) with an explicit comment naming where the actual write happens, or (b) preceded by an actual mutation. Third prevention: when widening a return type from `boolean` to a discriminated union, the GREP target isn't just the function declaration — search for every callsite that does `if (!moved)` / `if (moved === false)` / `if (!result)` / `if (result === true)` against the OLD name; each is a place the heuristic might survive after the type widening. The Wave 7 bookmarks-phase2 heuristic survived precisely because the test ENCODED it; the test-update pattern is REPLACE the encoding tests, not EXTEND. Generalisable across projects.

task: Wave 8.5 focused re-audit of B-1/B-2/H-1/H-2 (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Re-audited the four Wave 8.5 patches that closed the two BLOCKERs and two HIGHs I raised in the Wave 8 Phase 2 review. Three of four (B-1 applyReorder, H-1 MoveBookmarkResult, H-2 ESLint no-restricted-imports) are CLOSED cleanly with strong test coverage. The fourth (B-2 applyInsert variant handling) is CLOSED on its own terms — the engine now honors all four source.kind variants honestly — but surfaces a NEW HIGH finding (N-1) one layer below: the renderer history-middleware compacts image bytes BEFORE the inverse image-insert reaches dirtyOps, so a save immediately after undo-of-delete-image-page fails at embedImage() with invalid_image. Riley flagged this assumption explicitly in her Wave 8.5 status row contract-observation #3; David's main-side fix did not add the content-hash lookup the doc comment in ipc-contract.ts:230-234 promises ('Main's image-cache holds the real bytes for the handle's lifetime; redo retrieves by hash'). Severity HIGH not BLOCKER because atomic-save rolls back — user sees a clear error toast, the on-disk file is unchanged. Second meta-observation: David's boundary translation invalid_parent -> 'invalid_payload' is contract-correct given the frozen-Wave-1-doc constraint (api-contracts.md §12.6 BookmarksMoveError union has invalid_payload but not invalid_parent), but loses UX precision. The api-contracts.md spec was INCOMPLETE — the original Wave 1 author didn't anticipate the parent-existence + same-fileHash invariant Ravi added in Wave 7, so the wire never gained a precise error variant for that case. This is a project pattern: BLOCKER patches sometimes surface contract gaps the original spec didn't address. Third meta-observation: I caught a pattern in my own Wave 8 review I should be more disciplined about — when the engine has TWO functions doing similar work (applyInsert with source.kind branch vs applyImageInsert as a separate handler), the inverse-builder picking between them is implicit. Riley correctly flagged the assumption that David's applyImageInsert would look up by contentHash. I should have explicitly verified that in Wave 8 by reading image-embed.ts:69-71. The audit-rule for future code reviews: when a fix introduces a new code path that depends on a downstream consumer behaving a certain way, the reviewer MUST trace the downstream consumer in the same pass — Riley flagged the assumption in writing but didn't have main-process file ownership; I had both and could have closed the loop in one audit instead of two.
fix: For ANY full-stack project where two layers implement the same conceptual operation: when a Phase-N spec for a contract-bearing wire format (api-contracts.md or equivalent) is amended in Wave X, the orchestrator MUST audit downstream consumers in the SAME wave — not assume 'we'll handle it in Phase N.5'. The invalid_parent variant for BookmarksMoveError is a 3-line spec amendment that would close a UX gap the wire silently swallows today. Generalisable: any time a BOUNDARY type is widened internally (repo, bridge, db-layer) but NOT at the IPC/wire surface, the wire is silently lossy. Bake into orchestrator wave-1-amendment-protocol: 'if a repo or bridge type gains a new failure variant, the wire contract MUST also be amended or the variant MUST be explicitly downgraded with a TODO referencing the wire-amendment ticket.' Second prevention: when code review finds a HIGH that is closed-on-terms-but-surfaces-N-1, treat that as a meta-signal that the original review missed verifying a contract assumption. The pattern is: 'reviewer flags X, agent fixes X, reviewer re-audits and finds X+epsilon'. The epsilon is what the original review should have caught. Concretely: when a code review finds 'this fix depends on downstream consumer Y doing Z', the reviewer should trace Y in the same audit. Riley's contract-observation #3 was a writeup of the assumption; I should have validated it. Third prevention: for any project where compacted ops are pushed to history AND a save-time op-replay is structural, the compaction order is part of the contract. Document explicitly: 'middleware compacts AFTER inverseOf returns; the dispatched payload on undo/redo IS the compacted form; therefore dirtyOps contains compacted ops; therefore main-side handlers MUST tolerate compacted payloads via cache lookup.' This was implied in ipc-contract.ts:230-234 but not enforced — it should be a locked instruction or eval, not a doc comment.

task: Wave 9 brief - Phase 2 documentation update (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Wrote docs/wave-9-brief.md (Nathan deliverables: 5 amends + 1 NEW). Wave 9 closes Phase 2. Runs in parallel with Julian re-audit (zero overlap: Julian writes code-review.md, Nathan writes README + user-guide + developer-guide + api-reference + LICENSES + phase-2-release-notes). Two non-obvious meta-lessons emerged from authoring this brief. First - the H-3 closure precedent applied to user-facing docs: when a Phase-N walking-skeleton limitation moves from 'Coming in Phase N+1' to 'Available', the doc has THREE distinct surfaces to update, not one. (1) The README status banner shifts (0.1.0 walking skeleton -> 0.2.0 functional editor) - this is the entry-point honesty layer. (2) The user-guide point-of-action mentions (Phase 1 had three: README front, user-guide top, Saving section per Wave 4 learning #12) all RETIRE in unison - you cannot keep the limitation language in one place while removing it from another, or the doc set is mutually contradictory.
