(3) The release notes RECORD the transition explicitly under 'Behavior change' in Upgrade Notes - a Save that produced a valid PDF without edits in 0.1.0 and a Save that produces a valid PDF WITH edits in 0.2.0 is a behavior change downstream consumers need to know about, even though the IPC contract is additive (no breaking changes). Pattern name: 'walking-skeleton retirement triple-update'. Second meta-lesson - doc accuracy as part of shippability: a Phase that ships green code + green build + green tests is NOT shippable if the docs claim something the code doesn't do (or fail to claim something the code DOES do). The Phase-N+1 release closeout MUST audit user-facing docs against on-disk reality. The acceptance criteria for Wave 9 are deliberately written as 'verifiable on disk' checks (shortcuts table matches use-app-shortcuts.ts, test counts match the Wave 8.5 verification matrix, channel surfaces match src/ipc/contracts.ts, dependency rows match npm ls). Nathan must grep before he writes - 'no aspirational claims' is enforced not by trust but by mechanism. Third observation - the brief-as-artefact pattern (Wave 3.5 learning #11) keeps working: this Marcus session has no Task dispatch primitive loaded; the brief on disk IS the dispatchable contract for Nathan. Verified via ToolSearch - no Task / subagent tool surfaced; only TaskStop (background-task stop), RemoteTrigger (claude.ai routines), and CronCreate (session-scoped cron). Brief-as-artefact > orchestrator-as-required-runtime.
fix: For any phase-closeout documentation wave where a Phase-N limitation moves to Phase-N+1 reality: explicitly enumerate every surface in the doc set that carries the limitation (README banner + README limitations section + user-guide top-of-doc + user-guide point-of-action + roadmap + release-notes Behavior-change row) and update ALL of them in the SAME wave. Partial updates create a mutually-contradictory doc set that will be discovered by the next phase as a doc-drift bug. The trust-floor for a phase closeout is total consistency, not just the loudest entry point. Second prevention: write the wave's acceptance criteria as 'verifiable on disk' checks wherever possible - 'shortcuts table matches X' is better than 'shortcuts table is accurate', because the former points the doc author at a specific grep target. Generalisable across projects: prefer mechanical verification gates over judgment gates for documentation accuracy. Third prevention: for the dispatch-primitive-missing case (subagent tool not loaded in an orchestrator session), confirm via ToolSearch BEFORE writing the wave's dispatch path. Brief-as-artefact patterns work indefinitely if the brief itself carries the dispatch path; orchestrator-as-runtime patterns only work when the runtime is actually exposed. The two patterns are NOT interchangeable - if you tell the user 'Nathan is dispatched' when no dispatch primitive is loaded, you have lied. Verify first, then declare.

task: Wave 8.6 N-1 history-compaction-defeats-dispatch fix (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Closed Julian's Wave 8.5 re-audit N-1 HIGH finding via two-state HistoryEntry. Pre-fix: history middleware compacted image bytes BEFORE pushing to history slice AND dispatched the SAME compacted op on undo/redo — so dirtyOps accumulated image-insert ops with empty bytes, and David's main-side embedImage() rejected invalid_image on save (correctly per its contract at image-embed.ts:69-71). Post-fix: HistoryEntry shape is { fwd, inv, rawFwd, rawInv } — compacted forms for storage footprint (conventions §13.3), raw forms for dispatch. Undo branch dispatches rawInv; redo branch dispatches rawFwd. Renderer typecheck 0 errors. 97/97 renderer tests pass (was 94 → +3 N-1 regression tests, 0 regressions). The load-bearing test asserts dirtyOps[1].image.bytes.byteLength === payload.bytes.byteLength after delete-image-page → undo (pre-fix this assertion would fail with received 0). Three non-obvious takeaways. First: a SINGLE representation cannot satisfy both 'audit-trail compactness' AND 'on-the-wire byte-bearing' contracts simultaneously. Phase 2's original compaction-only design conflated the two because the assumption was 'main looks up by contentHash' — but the actual implementation checks bytes first. When two contracts conflict, the cheaper fix is to dual-store at the OBSERVER (history slice) rather than amend the consumer (main's embedImage). This is the inverse pattern of 'observer pays' from CS lore. Generalisable to any system where 'what is stored' and 'what is dispatched' have differing footprint/correctness requirements (Redux history, CRDT compaction, IPC batch coalescing). Second non-obvious takeaway: the renderer fix CLOSES the user-facing symptom unilaterally — no main-side amendment required. The doc comment in ipc-contract.ts:230-234 ('main's image-cache holds the real bytes...; redo retrieves by hash') is NOW MISALIGNED with renderer behavior in a HARMLESS direction: renderer always re-sends bytes, so the cache-lookup-by-hash path is dead code on the main side. Marcus rules on whether to (a) fix the comment to match reality or (b) fix the main side to honor the comment — both are valid; option (a) is doc-only, option (b) is 3 LOC in image-embed.ts. The fact that renderer-only fix closes the symptom is non-obvious because the BRIEF originally framed N-1 as a 'David vs Riley coordination' problem; turns out Riley's territory alone is sufficient. Third takeaway: compactImageOpForHistory is INCOMPLETE — it handles image-insert/image-overlay/image-overlay-delete but NOT delete{preservedSource:{kind:'image'}}. So the forward delete-of-image-page accumulates byte-bearing delete ops in 'compacted' history. Latent storage-footprint bug for users who delete many image pages; not user-visible because Phase 2 only loads ~3 image pages in tests. Flagged for Phase 2.5 with a 5-line fix in ipc-contract.ts:236-253. The asymmetric compactor + the asymmetric §7.1.3 inverse table (Wave 8.5 lesson) are part of a pattern: Phase 2 image-related contracts have systematic gaps in the 'INVERSE direction' that the forward-direction tests don't catch.
fix: For ANY Redux middleware (or analogous CRDT/event-sourcing layer) that intercepts an op for storage AND for replay, treat 'what gets stored' and 'what gets dispatched on replay' as TWO DISTINCT CONTRACTS. A single representation can only satisfy both if the storage form is byte-equivalent to the dispatch form — which is rare once compaction enters the picture. The robust pattern is dual-store on the entry: { storedForm, dispatchedForm } pair. When the two forms differ (image bytes compacted vs raw, encrypted vs decrypted, snapshot vs delta), document the divergence in the entry's type with comments cross-referencing both consumers. The cost is one extra reference per entry; for non-divergent ops the extra field aliases the same object (effectively zero overhead). Second prevention: when a doc comment promises a contract behavior (e.g. 'cache lookup by hash on redo') AND the implementation does something else, treat the doc as the contract and the implementation as the bug — OR amend the doc explicitly. Silent doc-vs-implementation drift means downstream agents make decisions on the doc that the implementation contradicts. Both Riley's Wave 8.5 contract-observation #3 and Julian's Wave 8.5 re-audit N-1 found the same issue from opposite ends; the meta-prevention is for the agent that owns the contract to either (a) verify the doc claim is implemented in the same wave, or (b) downgrade the claim with a TODO referencing the wave that will implement it. Third prevention: when a compactor function (compactImageOpForHistory or analogous) handles SOME op variants but not OTHERS in the same type family (e.g. compacts image-insert but not delete{preservedSource:image}), audit the exhaustive switch. The default branch will compile-pass any unhandled variant because the function returns `op` as fallback — invisible at typecheck, visible only at runtime when the un-handled variant is the largest payload in production. The fix-template: every compactor should have an exhaustive switch with a `never` default, just like the apply-op functions, even if the un-handled variants are 'pass-through' — make the pass-through explicit and code-reviewable. Fourth: for renderer-only fixes that close a symptom by working AROUND a main-side contract issue, log the residual contract observation explicitly in build-report (not just learnings) so the next wave's orchestrator can decide whether to land the corresponding main-side fix or formally retract the dead-code path.

task: Wave 9 Phase 2 documentation update (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Closed Phase 2 by amending 5 user-facing docs (README, user-guide, developer-guide, api-reference, LICENSES) and creating phase-2-release-notes.md. Total written: 2123 lines across 6 files (133+521+694+505+192+78). The H-3 retirement story is the central narrative: the Phase 1 user-guide carried the 'Save does not yet preserve your edits' caveat three times (status banner, top-of-guide trust floor, Saving section), and Phase 2 retired all three by carrying the inverse claim 'Save preserves your edits via the edit-replay engine' with the same triple impression (README status banner, user-guide What changed in 0.2.0 preamble, Saving section What happens behind the scenes subsection). The fidelity-boundary discipline INVERTED but the doc structure didn't change. Three patterns reinforced from Wave 4 + this wave: (1) the trust-floor pattern works in both directions — limitation impressions in Phase 1 and capability impressions in Phase 2 occupy the same doc real estate; (2) test counts and shortcut bindings MUST be cross-checked against source (use-app-shortcuts.ts revealed Ctrl+I migrated from Toggle-inspector in Phase 1 to Insert-image in Phase 2 — a silent shortcut change users will hit on upgrade); (3) the brief said 'pdf:exportToPdf' but the actual channel name is 'pdf:export' (Phase 1 stub now Live in Phase 2) — documented under its actual name. The 'How to add an EditOperation variant' walkthrough became a 10-row recipe with concrete file paths, presentational-vs-model-mutating distinction explicit, paired Riley/David ownership called out. Treats EditOperation extension as a recipe a future contributor can follow without orchestrator handholding.
fix: For documentation-as-product work on multi-phase projects: the doc structure should be SYMMETRIC across phase transitions — every limitation in Phase N gets a 'retired' or 'reframed' entry in Phase N+1 at the same doc location. The trust-floor pattern (status banner + top-of-guide preamble + the affected feature section) is the strongest place to impress a constraint OR a capability. Doc the SHIPPED reality always, even when it diverges from the brief's loose language (channel names, file paths). Cross-grep all 'X works' claims against build-report status rows before writing. Limitations are features when documented honestly — every 'doesn't work' line earns user trust by being explicit about why and when it ships.

task: Wave 10 Phase 2.5 cleanup — R-10.1 + R-10.2 + R-10.3 (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: All three R-10 tasks closed. R-10.1: extended compactImageOpForHistory in ipc-contract.ts with the missing fourth variant (delete{preservedSource:image}); added regression test pinning fwd compaction + rawFwd byte preservation symmetric to image-insert. R-10.2: chose Path B (document + pin via test). Investigation revealed dedup is ALREADY in place automatically via JS reference semantics — inverseOf in document-inverses.ts aliases op.image / op.preservedSource.image directly (no structuredClone, no byte copy) so rawFwd.image.bytes === rawInv.preservedSource.image.bytes by IDENTITY for every image-op history entry. The engine holds ONE copy of bytes per entry, not two. Added top-of-file comment block in history-middleware.ts documenting the invariant + regression test pinning identity (NOT equality) so any future inverseOf change that introduces a clone fails fast. Architectural ~50MB ceiling claim was actually wrong — it's ~25MB at maxHistory=100. R-10.3: client gatekeeper ipc-contract.ts at line 174 is already a pure re-export so it auto-picks-up David's D-10.1 amendment with zero edits. moveBookmarkThunk got a dedicated invalid_parent branch (toast 'Cannot move bookmark to that location.' — distinct from cycle_detected toast). Implementation uses `const wireError: string = res.error;` widened comparison to be forward-compatible regardless of David's parallel landing order. Renderer typecheck 0 errors. 99/99 renderer tests pass (was 97 baseline, +2 new R-10.1 + R-10.2 invariant pin, 0 regressions). Five non-obvious takeaways. First: when an inverse-table is implemented by aliasing input fields (vs cloning them), JS reference semantics automatically produce buffer dedup between forward and inverse — the user never asked for it but the implementation gave it for free. The dedup is INVISIBLE in code review (no `===` assertion exists, no comment mentions it) so a downstream refactor that adds structuredClone for 'safety' silently doubles memory. The fix is an explicit IDENTITY-pinning test (`expect(a).toBe(b)`) backed by a doc comment naming the invariant. Generalizable across CRDT/event-sourcing/immutability-by-convention codebases. Second: the Wave 8.6 architectural-ceiling claim (~50MB) was upstream-pessimistic by 2x because the analyst (me, in Wave 8.6) assumed dual-store doubled the bytes without verifying. Lesson: when flagging an architectural ceiling for follow-up, the Phase-2.5 follow-up's first task should be VERIFY THE CEILING, not implement a fix. The ceiling may already not apply. Promote: 'verify the cost before implementing the optimization' as a self-improvement pattern. Third: forward-compatible thunk update across a parallel contract amendment — the `const x: string = narrowed; if (x === 'newVariant') ...` pattern is the cleanest way to land downstream consumer code for an upstream contract amendment that hasn't landed yet, while staying type-safe under BOTH the old AND new contract shapes. Avoids the orchestration headache of 'David must land first' or 'rebase after David lands'. Generalizable to any wave where a parallel agent amends a discriminated-union surface and a downstream agent consumes the new variant. Fourth: my Wave 8.6 contract-observation #2 (compactImageOpForHistory missing the delete{preservedSource:image} branch) was reachable via TWO independent contract-style checks that BOTH should fire on any future image-op handler addition: (a) is the compactor exhaustive over all image-bytes-carrying variants, (b) does every variant in the EditOperation union that contains a Uint8Array field have a compactor branch. The Wave 8.6 reviewer (me) caught it via inspection; the test suite did NOT — no forward-direction test asserted compaction on a fwd-delete-of-image-page. Future test-design template: for any compactor over a discriminated union with payload-bearing fields, parameterize the test by variant and assert byte-zeroing for every variant exhaustively. Fifth: a comment-only change that documents an implicit invariant can be functionally as load-bearing as a code change. The history-middleware.ts top-of-file 'Memory model' block now codifies the buffer-sharing reality that was previously only readable by inspecting inverseOf line-by-line. Future contributors will be guided by the comment + the identity-pinning test, not by archaeological code reading. The cost of the comment block was ~30 lines; the value is preserving a load-bearing invariant against drift.
fix: For ANY future agent extending compactImageOpForHistory (or any compactor over a discriminated union with payload-bearing fields): (a) add the new variant branch BEFORE the trailing `return op` so the pass-through fallback doesn't silently leak un-handled variants, (b) add a regression test asserting byte-zeroing on the new variant, (c) cross-check the exhaustiveness against the EditOperation union manually — TypeScript's structural typing will NOT catch a missing variant in a non-exhaustive switch because the pass-through is type-correct. Second prevention: when flagging an architectural-ceiling concern at code-review time (e.g. '~50MB doubled storage') the FIRST follow-up task should VERIFY the ceiling by tracing the code path with a debugger or identity-pinning test. The ceiling may already not apply due to implementation choices the original analyst didn't account for. The wasted-fix cost of implementing an optimization that wasn't needed is the prevention target. Third prevention: for downstream agents consuming a parallel-agent contract amendment, use the `const x: string = narrowed; if (x === 'newVariant') ...` widened-comparison pattern to land forward-compatible code without depending on landing order. Document the pattern at the use site with a comment explaining both contract shapes the code handles. Fourth: for compactor / serializer / projector functions over discriminated unions, the test-design template is 'parameterize by variant, assert post-condition for every variant exhaustively' — non-exhaustive coverage of variants is the silent-leak surface. Fifth: invariants implemented by reference-aliasing (vs deep-clone) MUST be pinned by IDENTITY-assertion tests (`expect(a).toBe(b)`), not equality tests, AND documented in a top-of-file comment block at the consumer site. Identity invariants are the most fragile because any future refactor that 'safely clones for purity' silently defeats them.

task: Wave 10 Phase 2.5 cleanup (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Closed Di-10.1 (Julian MEDIUM picks — path-sanitizer hardening test fixtures: 47/47 tests pass, up from 7; ESLint safety-floor: 5 new rules verified zero-impact on current codebase, lint baseline 10 errors/87 warnings preserved), Di-10.2 (package.json 0.2.0 + LICENSES.md utif row + scan basis refresh + npm ls clean of UNMET PEER), and Di-10.3 (dist:win produced 95.8 MB NSIS installer + 95.6 MB portable for 0.2.0). Non-obvious takeaway #1: when a path-sanitizer source belongs to another agent's domain but the test deferral is yours, the right move is a two-block test file: (a) regression-pin block asserting documented REJECTIONS so a defense regression fails loud, plus (b) KNOWN GAP block asserting current ACCEPTANCES of risky inputs with an inline comment naming the hardening that should flip the assertions. The second block is a 'live ticket' for the next wave: it documents the gap in executable form, is greppable (KNOWN GAP - Phase 2.5.1), and forces a deliberate decision when the source is patched (flip the assertions and migrate the block). Probing the sanitizer's actual behavior with a temporary throwaway test inside src/ (so it's picked up by vitest's include pattern) was the fastest way to learn ground truth before writing the pin assertions — never assume sanitizer behavior from reading the source, ALWAYS probe. Non-obvious takeaway #2: ESLint rules that read like obvious safety floors (eqeqeq, no-var, no-throw-literal, no-debugger, no-alert) can be safely added in a config-only wave IF and ONLY IF they're verified zero-impact against the current code. Run each rule individually with `--rule 'X: error'` against the codebase before merging — if it adds even one finding outside the wave's source scope, defer the rule to the wave that touches that file. Otherwise the dev-ops agent ends up gated on another agent's fix. Non-obvious takeaway #3: dist:win on a host with Node-ABI better-sqlite3 prebuilt will swap it to Electron-ABI as part of `electron-builder install-app-deps` (or the rebuilding-native-dependencies step inside the builder itself). Subsequent local `npm test` (vitest on Node) will fail with ERR_DLOPEN_FAILED on every better-sqlite3 test. This is the documented D-1 ABI flip from Wave 3 — CI on Node LTS sidesteps it because the matching Node-ABI prebuild is present. If a smoke verification is part of the same wave as a test verification, ORDER MATTERS: test FIRST, dist LAST, OR document the test-baseline before dist runs so the post-dist test count isn't read as a regression.
fix: For any future agent dispatched to 'absorb test backfill for a security-critical module they don't own': write the test file in a two-block structure — regression-pin (current rejections) + KNOWN GAP (current acceptances of risky inputs, with explicit inline comment naming the hardening that flips the assertions). The KNOWN GAP block IS the executable backlog item — it's discoverable by grep, fails the build if behavior changes accidentally, and forces a deliberate decision when the source is patched. NEVER write tests that assert what the sanitizer 'should' do — write tests that pin what it ACTUALLY does, classified honestly. For ESLint rule additions in a config-only wave: verify each rule independently with `npx eslint --rule 'X: error' .` against the current codebase BEFORE adding to .eslintrc.cjs. A rule that adds zero findings is a ratchet (safe); a rule that adds even one finding outside your touch surface is gated on another agent. For Electron projects with native-module deps: any wave that runs both `npm test` and `npm run dist:win` MUST sequence test FIRST (Node-ABI prebuild in place) then dist LAST (which swaps to Electron-ABI), OR document the pre-dist test count separately so the post-dist regression on db tests isn't misread.

task: Wave 10 Phase 2.5 cleanup — invalid_parent wire variant + identifyTextSpan scanner + dual-store comment + R-10.3 flag (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Shipped 6 file edits (api-contracts.md §12.6 amendment, src/ipc/contracts.ts widening + dual-store comment block, bookmarks-phase2.ts handler retiring the invalid_parent->invalid_payload wire translation, bookmarks-phase2.test.ts updated assertion, pdf-identify-text-span.ts full scanner rewrite with manual content-stream tokenizer, pdf-identify-text-span.test.ts +3 scanner tests / -1 conservative test). Main+IPC baseline 224 -> 266 (David +2 net, Diego +40 parallel path-sanitizer). All 3 typecheck projects clean. Non-obvious takeaway #1: my dispatch brief AND the phase-2.5-brief.md both routed D-10.3 'doc comment at src/ipc/contracts.ts:230-234' to me, but lines 230-234 of src/ipc/contracts.ts are just the // 4. fs: channels section banner — the matching narrative comment about the dual-store actually lives at src/client/types/ipc-contract.ts:230-234 (Riley's gatekeeper). docs/code-review.md §486 confirms the renderer-side location is the historical reference. Resolved by adding the dual-store narrative as a NEW comment block in my owned contracts.ts at the fs-channels boundary, NOT touching Riley's gatekeeper, and flagging the routing typo in the build-report. Non-obvious takeaway #2: pdf-lib does NOT parse content streams loaded from existing PDFs into PDFOperator instances — PDFContentStream.operators is populated only on the write path (newly-constructed pages). For the D-10.2 real scanner I had to write a manual content-stream tokenizer + text-state simulator from scratch, decoding the raw streams via pdf-lib's decodePDFRawStream (which handles Flate/LZW/ASCII85/AsciiHex/RunLength). The tokenizer handles literal strings with balanced parens + escapes (including octal up to 3 digits), hex strings, names, arrays (for TJ), numbers, operators, nested dicts skip, and PDF comments. Text-state machine tracks BT/ET, text matrix, text line matrix, leading, font family + size; emits one ScannedTextRun per Tj/TJ/'/" with an honest 0.5em-per-character bbox approximation (the embedded font's real widths aren't loaded). Locked pattern: any future agent needing main-side text scanning over an existing PDF should use this tokenizer + simulator pattern (not try to coerce pdf-lib's read-path objects).
fix: For any pdf-lib content-stream analysis task: (1) PDFContentStream.operators is WRITE-PATH ONLY — don't expect it on streams loaded via PDFDocument.load. (2) Use decodePDFRawStream(stream) to get a DecodeStream / Stream that exposes .decode() returning the raw operator bytes. (3) Write a minimal tokenizer that handles literal strings with balanced-paren + backslash-escape rules, hex strings, names, arrays, numbers, operators, comments, and nested-dict skip. (4) Run a text-state simulator over the tokens; track BT/ET, text matrix, leading, font; emit one run per show-text operator. (5) Bbox width approximation 0.5em × charCount × fontSize × horizontalScale is the honest fallback when glyph widths aren't loaded — document it in the handler header. Bake into the agent prompt: pdf-lib's write path is rich, read path is mostly opaque — when in doubt, decode the bytes and parse them yourself. Second lesson: when a dispatch brief and a higher-level brief BOTH reference the same buggy file path (the case here: 'src/ipc/contracts.ts:230-234' when the matching comment is in 'src/client/types/ipc-contract.ts:230-234'), the safest move is to (a) stay strictly within your owned file boundaries, (b) deliver a CORRECT comment at that boundary describing the same narrative, and (c) flag the routing typo loudly in the build-report status row so reviewers can apply a micro-patch if needed. Do NOT cross file-ownership lines to 'fix the originally-intended file' — that creates a parallel-write conflict with the owner and breaks trust in the file-ownership contract.

task: Wave 10 Phase 2.5 cleanup re-audit (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Re-audited David + Riley + Diego Wave 10 patches against the 14 acceptance criteria in phase-2.5-brief.md §5. Verdict GREEN — all criteria closed. 2 new MEDIUM findings flagged for Phase 2.5.1 / Phase 3 absorption: M-1 (path-sanitizer 'KNOWN GAP' test block now publishes 13 unaddressed attack vectors as documentation), M-2 (David's 615-LOC content-stream tokenizer for pdf:identifyTextSpan has no fuzz coverage of malformed PDF bytes; no exploitable surface today but worth a fast-check harness when Phase 3 lights up save-time use). 1 new LOW finding L-1 (Riley's identity-pinning dedup test depends on Object.is; a future structuredClone refactor of history-middleware would break the ~25 MB memory ceiling AND the test catches it but the symptom is confusing). RULED on David's D-10.3 routing-typo flag: false positive — David thought src/client/types/ipc-contract.ts:230-234 contained a stale 'Main's image-cache holds the real bytes' comment, but that quote never existed verbatim in source; my Wave 8.5 audit text was a paraphrase the brief author treated as a literal source citation. Riley's gatekeeper-side comment at line 230-279 is the compactImageOpForHistory JSDoc which she correctly updated in R-10.1 with the Wave 10 banner. David's new comment at src/ipc/contracts.ts:230-244 describes the complementary IPC-boundary perspective. No Wave 10.5 micro-patch needed. Phase 2 ship status: YELLOW → GREEN. Phase 3 dispatch GREEN. Non-obvious takeaway: when a code review's narrative cites 'doc comment at file:line', a future reviewer or build brief author may treat the citation as a verbatim quote — when the audit actually paraphrased an architectural intent. Citing audit text in dispatch briefs creates a citation-of-paraphrase risk that surfaces as 'wait, that comment doesn't exist' confusion two waves later. The reviewer's text becomes load-bearing in ways the reviewer didn't anticipate.
fix: For code-reviewer agents: when writing audit text that cites specific file:line locations, use one of two patterns: (a) quote the EXACT current source verbatim with quote marks, and timestamp the version (commit-hash or wave-number) the quote is from, OR (b) explicitly mark paraphrased architectural intent with 'paraphrased' or 'intent at FILE:LINE' rather than direct quote-marks. The Wave 8.5 audit text at lines 484-486 of code-review.md used quote-marks around a paraphrase of architecture-phase-2.md intent, and three waves later the brief author treated that as a literal source quotation. Cost: David spent time looking for a stale comment that didn't exist; flagged it for Julian to rule on; Julian spent time confirming the false positive. Prevention is cheap: discipline quote-marks to verbatim source only, mark paraphrases as such, or hash-pin the file version. Promote to global JSONL because this pattern recurs in any multi-wave audit chain where the auditor's text becomes a citation source for downstream agents — same risk class as 'docs say X is enforced but actually a manual review caught the only violation' (Wave 8.5 H-2 lesson). Second prevention: when reviewing patches that close a finding I raised, ALWAYS grep the source for the exact text I cited in the prior audit — if the grep returns zero hits, my prior audit text was either paraphrased or stale, and I should add a self-correction note to the new audit section.

task: Phase 2.5.1 micro-wave — path-sanitizer hardening, M-1 closure (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Closed 13 KNOWN GAP attack vectors in src/main/security/path-sanitizer.ts (UNC + Win32 device-namespace prefixes, 22 reserved DOS device names with case-insensitive segment-walk + extension-strip + trailing-space trim, percent-encoded bytes refuse-to-decode, Unicode bidi-override + zero-width + BOM via single regex U+200B-U+202E + U+FEFF). Added 4 new helpers (isUncOrNamespacedPath, containsReservedDosName, containsPercentEncoded, containsSuspiciousUnicode) + a new internal sanitizePathDetailed() returning a discriminated SanitizeResult with 10-variant SanitizeErrorCode union. Public sanitizePath() signature is UNCHANGED (string | null) — the 14 downstream callers needed zero edits because they already mapped null to their own contract error codes. Test count 47 -> 67 (delta +20: 13 KNOWN GAP flipped from .not.toBeNull() to .toBeNull(), 13 it.each expansions for the new vectors, 4 detailed-API error-code assertions, 3 brief-required positive tests, 3 false-positive guards for names that CONTAIN reserved substrings without being reserved like concord.pdf, communication.pdf, lpt.pdf). All verifications green: tsc clean, 67 path-sanitizer tests pass, 154 main suite, 132 IPC suite, L-001 + H-3 retain. Meta-lesson: test fixtures that document attack vectors with .not.toBeNull() assertions ARE attack-vector runbooks the moment they ship — Diego Wave 10 KNOWN GAP block was structurally correct (make the gap loud so the next wave HAS to address it) but the published-runbook tradeoff has a half-life: hardening MUST follow within one release cycle or the runbook becomes a security gift to any AI-tool training on or any reader of the repo. The first-match-rejection order also matters: when adding a new guard, put the cheapest/most-specific check earliest so the error code surfaced is the most precise; e.g. suspicious-Unicode goes BEFORE control-char regex because U+200B-U+202E sit outside the �- range. Second observation: refusing to decode percent-encoded bytes (rather than decode-then-recheck) is the right defense for an Electron app where renderer dialog paths are NEVER URL-encoded on the platforms shipped — refuse-to-decode prevents double-decode bugs AND collapses to a single regex test.
fix: For any future wave that adds an explicit KNOWN GAP test block (assertions that document CURRENT acceptance of attack inputs): require a one-release-cycle SLA on closing the gap. Publish the test block + the brief for the closing wave in the SAME PR. If the closing wave cannot be scheduled within one release cycle, REMOVE the test block (it becomes a published runbook). Second prevention rule: when hardening a sanitizer that returns string | null, ALWAYS layer the detailed error-code API as an internal-only export rather than widening the public return type — this preserves the zero-ripple closure path for every existing caller. Third: when checking case-insensitive reserved-name lists, walk SEGMENTS (split on both backslash and forward-slash) not just basename — Windows treats CON in a directory position the same as in a filename position. Strip BOTH the extension AND trailing spaces from each segment before the lowercase set lookup — Windows ignores trailing spaces in DOS device name resolution. Fourth: when adding any new Unicode-range check, include the explicit codepoint list as a comment block at the regex; future readers (especially AI tools) need to understand WHICH characters are blocked and WHY (TR36 section 3 visual-spoofing reference). Fifth: 3 false-positive guards (concord.pdf, communication.pdf, lpt.pdf — names containing reserved substrings without being reserved) caught a classic substring-vs-exact-match bug class; bake the guard pattern into any future reserved-name list addition.

task: Wave 11 Phase 3 architecture design (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Closed Phase 3 architecture design solo (analogous to Wave 1 and Wave 6) with 6 docs touched: 2 NEW (architecture-phase-3.md 751 lines, form-engine.md 932 lines) + 4 AMENDED additively (api-contracts.md +394, data-models.md +267, ui-spec.md +515, conventions.md +165). All 7 locked decisions encoded with cross-references. Three non-obvious takeaways. First: the Phase 3 plan rated pdf-lib AcroForm CREATE as HIGH risk based on outdated documentation. Verifying the actual published API surface (pdf-lib 1.17.x has form.createTextField/createCheckBox/createDropdown/createRadioGroup with addToPage helpers, native since 1.16) downgraded the risk to MEDIUM with a single residual gap (signature placeholder requires manual PDFDict authorship — same pattern as Phase 2 /Ink annotations). The lesson: when inheriting a risk-register entry from a prior plan, the FIRST action of the architecture wave should be to verify the underlying assumption against current library reality, not to assume the prior agent did. The cost of unnecessary risk mitigation (fallback library evaluation, contingency code paths) is real and avoidable. Second: the EditOperation-integration choice for form-fill (the headline P3-L-7 brief asked Riley to resolve) was NOT a binary A-vs-B choice. The HYBRID model (form-fill = batched-commit, form-design = per-op) emerged from explicitly enumerating the failure mode of each pure approach: per-op fill pollutes history with N micro-ops; separate-track requires bespoke undo plumbing; HYBRID keeps the undo middleware uniform while respecting the semantic editorial-act boundary. Generalizable: when a brief asks 'pick A or B', the more interesting answer is often 'A for X, B for Y, drawn at the semantic boundary that matters to the user'. Third: the mail-merge bypass of the dirtyOps funnel (the runner calls fillForm() directly per row, never producing EditOperations) is architecturally a third state-management pattern (alongside dirtyOps for document edits and direct SQLite for bookmarks). Documented in conventions.md section 14 as a first-class pattern, not a special case. The lesson: when an architecture grows to three orthogonal state-management patterns, surface the typology explicitly with a comparison table (storage / saves-through / undo-behavior columns) — the table is the prevention mechanism against future agents who try to force the third pattern through one of the first two.
fix: For any architecture wave inheriting a risk register from a prior plan: FIRST action is to verify the underlying library assumption against the current published API (read source if needed). The cost of unnecessary risk mitigation is real and silently shapes downstream waves. Promote: 'verify before mitigate' as a self-improvement pattern for architecture agents. Second prevention: when a brief asks the architect to pick between approaches (A vs B), explicitly enumerate the failure modes of each PURE approach before answering — a hybrid that draws the line at a semantic boundary the user cares about is often the correct answer, but it requires articulating both failure modes to find the boundary. Document the hybrid with a side-by-side comparison table in the architecture doc so future agents understand the boundary, not just the choice. Third prevention: when an architecture grows to three or more orthogonal state-management patterns, surface the typology explicitly in conventions doc with a comparison table (storage column, saves-through column, undo column). The table is what prevents future agents from forcing the new pattern through an old one. Fourth prevention: when amending Phase-1/2-frozen docs with Phase-N sections, ALWAYS preserve the freeze rule by adding an amendment banner with date and section number — do not edit Phase-1 or Phase-2 sections directly. The amendment banner pattern is now load-bearing across Phase 2 (Wave 6) and Phase 3 (Wave 11); any future Phase N follow-up agent must continue it. Fifth prevention: cross-reference every new IPC channel in api-contracts to (a) its architecture-phase-N.md mention AND (b) if applicable, its EditOperation variant in data-models.md. The cross-reference checklist at the end of each amendment doc is the verification mechanism; do not skip it.

task: Wave 12 Phase 3 database (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Shipped migrations/0003_phase3_forms.sql (form_templates table, 2 indexes) + new form-templates-repo.ts (6 methods: list/get/getByName/upsert/delete/updateColumnMappings) + 41 vitest cases across 8 describe blocks + Phase-3 types in src/db/types.ts (FormFieldDefinition + payload union, FormTemplateRow, 4 new SettingKeys). tsc clean for src/db. Non-obvious takeaway #1: David's src/ipc/contracts.ts already defines FormFieldDefinition + FormFieldValue + FormFieldType + FormFieldOption types as part of his Phase-3 in-flight work, with structurally-identical shapes to what data-models §8.1 specifies. Per conventions §4.3 (no deep imports across process boundaries) the db layer cannot import from src/ipc — so my types in src/db/types.ts must structurally mirror David's. They do. The two layers stay in sync via the shared spec (data-models §8.1), not via a shared module. Same pattern as Wave 7's BookmarkRow vs BookmarkNode duality. Worth documenting: structural-type duplication ACROSS the bridge boundary is legal and correct, structural-type duplication WITHIN the bridge boundary (e.g. two copies in src/db/) is a bug. Non-obvious takeaway #2: the discriminated-union lesson from Wave 7 (MoveBookmarkResult) generalized cleanly to upsert. UpsertFormTemplateResult carries name_in_use (UNIQUE collision pre-checked inside the transaction) + not_found (UPDATE with stale id) — distinct because the IPC layer wants to surface name_in_use to the user as a typed error code (api-contracts §13.7) while not_found stays a soft-bug bridge-internal mapping. Throwing on UNIQUE was rejected because the bridge would have to string-match the better-sqlite3 message — fragile. Wave 7 cycle_detected lesson directly applicable. Non-obvious takeaway #3: the FormFieldDefinition JSON normalization decision (flat fields_json blob, NOT a separate fields table) was an upstream design call (data-models §8.4 + architecture-phase-3.md §7.3 explicitly chose flat). Confirms the 'trust the doc' Wave-2 principle: the typical N is small (<2500 field-rows total per user), the renderer always loads the full template, and schema-evolving FormFieldDefinition shape per Phase would be heavyweight if normalized. Don't second-guess the upstream design — implement to the spec and document the choice. Non-obvious takeaway #4: pre-emptive SettingKey additions for Phase 3 (forms.dateLocale, forms.flattenOnExportDefault, mailMerge.lastOutputFolder, mailMerge.defaultOutputMode) verified via grep that David hasn't wired them into src/ipc/contracts.ts yet — but per Wave 7 takeaway #3 the order doesn't matter as long as src/db/types.ts is the canonical registry. Did the same thing in Wave 7. Will save one round-trip when David's Phase-3 IPC contract ships.
fix: For any future migration that adds a JSON-blob column with caller-side parse/serialize semantics: store TEXT (or NULL), do NOT parse in the repo. Document the byte-for-byte preservation contract in the test suite (test 'preserves the JSON payload byte-for-byte'). The bridge is the JSON boundary; the repo is the SQL boundary; mixing the two creates spooky-action-at-a-distance bugs. Also: when a doc spec includes `INSERT INTO schema_migrations (version=N, applied_at=...)` inside the migration SQL, ALWAYS check the existing 0001/0002 convention before copying — they omit it (the runner records the row). Including it crashes the runner on retry with PRIMARY KEY conflict. Same lesson as Wave 7 takeaway #1.

task: Wave 12 Phase 3 main-process implementation (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Shipped 26 NEW + 7 EDITED files implementing the full Phase 3 main-process surface: form-engine.ts (detect/fill/flatten/createField/removeField/editField pure functions over bytes), field-dict-authoring.ts (manual /Sig field PDFDict authorship — the one gap pdf-lib's high-level API leaves), mail-merge-runner.ts (cancellable, template-cached, progress-streaming runner that BYPASSES the dirtyOps funnel per conventions §14.4), csv-excel-parser.ts (built-in RFC 4180 CSV tokenizer + dynamic-import exceljs path with actionable error), 10 IPC handlers + 1 progress event stream, 5 new EditOperation variants in replay-engine step 3.6, FormTemplatesRepo adapter in db-bridge that wraps Ravi's RaviUpsertFormTemplateResult discriminated union end-to-end. Verifications green: tsc main 0 errors, tsc preload 0 errors, 373/373 tests pass (was 286 baseline; David +87), L-001 2/2 + H-3 retirement 3/3 unchanged. 100-row mail-merge perf measured at 250-370ms (form-engine.md §9 budget was 30s; we're 80x under). Three non-obvious takeaways. First: jsdom Uint8Array != node Uint8Array — runner tests that pass instanceof checks must use '// @vitest-environment node' at top-of-file (only h3-retirement.test.ts and my new mail-merge tests do; other suites either don't use instanceof or use enc(string) helpers that go through the node Uint8Array path). Lost 5 minutes diagnosing this on first test run. Second: pdf-lib's doc.getForm() NEVER throws — it creates an empty AcroForm dict when none exists. The form-engine.md spec said 'catch and return form_not_present' but that branch is unreachable in pdf-lib 1.17; the engine must explicitly check 'form.getFields().length === 0' after the getForm call to detect 'no AcroForm in the source'. Got this wrong in the first implementation, surfaced via the 'fails form_not_present on a doc with no AcroForm' test. Third: csv-parse and exceljs are not pre-installed; the brief expected them as wishlist deps with Diego adding them post-wave. Built-in RFC 4180 tokenizer is 60 lines of pure code (handles BOM, quoted fields with embedded commas, escaped quotes, CRLF/LF, three delimiters, ragged rows, duplicate-header rename), sufficient for Wave 12 ship without an external dep. exceljs dynamic-imported via indirect specifier ('exceljs' assigned to variable then await import(specifier)) — bypasses TS resolver's typecheck-time requirement and surfaces an actionable 'install exceljs to enable XLSX mail-merge' error at runtime until Diego brings the package in. Fourth lesson: when the brief flags a sequence dependency on Ravi's repo, READ THE REPO FILE FIRST — Ravi's upsert returns a discriminated UpsertFormTemplateResult not a bare number, and the adapter shape mirrors that union verbatim to preserve name_in_use end-to-end (Wave 8.5 H-1 pattern). My initial adapter assumed a number return and would have lost the variant.
fix: For any future agent writing main-process IPC handlers that use Uint8Array via instanceof checks: ALWAYS add '// @vitest-environment node' at the top of the .test.ts file. The default jsdom env redefines TypedArrays and instanceof returns false across the realm boundary. Diego's vitest.config.ts uses jsdom as default. Second: when pdf-lib's API surface differs from a spec written by an architecture wave, the spec is the contract but the implementation has to bridge to actual library behavior — surface the divergence in the engine's error mapping (form-engine.ts checks both 'catch' and 'getFields().length === 0' before returning form_not_present). Third: when a brief says 'dep X required, flag in build report', don't BLOCK on the install — build a contract-not-implementation surface that works without the dep (built-in tokenizer for CSV, dynamic-import for Excel with actionable error), then surface the dep request in the build-report status row. The renderer's wizard step 2 still gets a clean error message and the rest of the pipeline (folder mode, concat mode, progress events, cancellation) is fully testable. Fourth: when adapting another agent's repo through a bridge, the adapter MUST preserve every discriminated-union variant the repo's return type carries — collapsing 'name_in_use' / 'not_found' / 'other-error' to a generic 'invalid_payload' breaks the IPC contract. Always pre-read the repo's source before writing the adapter signature.

task: Wave 12 Phase 3 renderer implementation (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Closed Phase 3 renderer Wave 12 — 5 new components (FormsPanel, FormFillOverlay, FormDesigner trio, MailMergeModal 4-step wizard, SaveTemplateModal, FlattenOnExportCheckbox), 3 new slices (formsSlice, mailMergeSlice, formsTemplatesSlice) + selectors, 1 new middleware (formCommitMiddleware), 10 new thunks, 5 new EditOperation inverse branches. 77 new tests added (99 → 176 total, 0 regressions, all 22 files green). Renderer typecheck 0 errors. ESLint 0 errors / 0 warnings on all new files. L-001 untouched. Three non-obvious takeaways. First: a circular-import landmine surfaces when middleware needs slice selectors. The store wires middleware which imports a function from forms-selectors which imports RootState from store — the eager-import chain breaks at test time with `each middleware provided to configureStore must be a function`. Fix is to INLINE the small comparator (valuesEqual) into the middleware itself rather than importing from selectors. Generalizable: any middleware that needs a small pure helper used elsewhere must NOT import that helper from a selectors module (which transitively pulls RootState/store). The right pattern is: small pure helpers live in the module that needs them, with a copy in selectors if convenient for components. Cost is ~5 LOC of duplication; benefit is the import graph stays acyclic and store.ts stays loadable in unit-test contexts. Second: the HYBRID commit-boundary contract per conventions §14 ships as a PAIRED test — one in the slice/middleware layer asserting `dirtyOps.length === 1` after 3 keystrokes + commit, and one in the component layer asserting `dirtyOps.length === 0` after 3 keystrokes BEFORE commit. The paired test pattern (which I codified in Wave 8.5 as the prevention for B-1/B-2) extends naturally to renderer-side semantic contracts. The two assertions together encode the HYBRID guarantee — neither one alone is sufficient (a slice test could pass while a component bypasses the slice; a component test could pass while the slice silently produces multiple ops). Generalizable: for any cross-layer semantic guarantee (commit boundary, byte-zeroing, idempotency, etc), write tests at BOTH endpoints of the layer transition. Third: jsx-a11y has a recurrent allergy to dynamic-boolean ARIA attributes (`aria-expanded={booleanVar}` and `aria-valuenow={number}`). The Phase 1 sidebar workaround (drop the ARIA tab semantics) recurs here as: (a) stringify boolean via `expanded ? 'true' : 'false'` for aria-expanded, (b) drop role='progressbar' entirely on the running view's progress bar (announce progress via the textual 'Processing row N of M' instead). The cost is a small a11y compromise that Phase 7's audit will revisit with a fully-typed pattern. Locking these workarounds with comments naming the Phase 7 follow-up is the trust-floor honest pattern Nathan calls out in Wave 9. Fourth (mid-task observation): the contract gap with David's MailMergeJob type — David's MailMergeJob does NOT carry `flattenForms?: boolean` at the top level, but the ui-spec §12.6.4 wizard shows the checkbox prominently. My thunk currently `void flatten`-discards the slice value, with the runner inheriting the global `forms.flattenOnExportDefault` setting per architecture-phase-3.md §6.5. UX-acceptable for Phase 3; needs a 1-line David amendment in Phase 3.1 to be honored per-job. Flagged in build-report's contract-issues row. The lesson: when the renderer renders a UI control whose value DOESN'T have a place to land on the wire type, the control still ships (UX won't tolerate hiding it) but the renderer documents the void-discard with a TODO. This is the inverse of Wave 8.5 H-1 (David promoting `invalid_parent` to a wire variant when the bridge had it but the wire didn't); now the renderer has a UI for an effect that the wire MailMergeJob doesn't carry. Same class of gap, opposite direction.
fix: For any renderer wave that introduces a middleware needing a small pure comparator: INLINE the comparator at the middleware module rather than importing from selectors. Selectors transitively pull `store`'s RootState which itself depends on every middleware — the import cycle breaks at test time with a cryptic 'middleware is not a function'. Bake into the conventions §6 (or equivalent) module-graph rule: 'middleware modules MUST NOT import from selectors modules; small helpers used in both should live at the slice level OR be duplicated.' Second prevention: for any cross-layer semantic guarantee in a multi-layer codebase, write a PAIRED test at BOTH endpoints of the layer transition. Slice-level test pins the produced ops; component-level test pins the absence of ops before commit. Either alone is insufficient — the gap between them is where the contract violation lives. Bake into testing convention: 'every cross-layer contract (commit boundary, byte-zeroing, idempotency) ships with two tests — one per layer endpoint.' Third prevention: when shipping a UI control whose value can't yet land on the IPC contract, void-discard the slice value at the dispatcher level WITH a comment naming the follow-up. Don't hide the UI (that surprises the user); don't fake a contract field (that masks the gap from the contract owner); document the gap loudly. Pattern: `void slice.value; // TODO Phase N.M: contract addition by <agent>`. Fourth prevention: a dynamic ARIA attribute landmine — jsx-a11y/aria-proptypes is strict about boolean/number-typed values that look dynamic to the lint. Workarounds: stringify booleans (`expanded ? 'true' : 'false'`), and drop ambient role attributes whose values can't be statically expressed (announce semantics via adjacent live-region text instead). Document the compromise inline with a Phase 7 a11y follow-up reference.

task: Wave 13 Phase 3 code review (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: RED verdict. 1 BLOCKER + 4 HIGH + 6 MEDIUM + 5 LOW + 3 NIT across 41 Phase 3 files (David's 26 main-process + Ravi's repo+migration+types + Riley's 10+ renderer slices/middleware/components). The single BLOCKER (B-3.1) is the kind of bug that survives every wave because the production wiring is shadowed by permissive test injection: register.ts:509 wires the mail-merge runner with the default sanitizePath which only accepts `.pdf` extensions (path-sanitizer.ts:31, 258-262 — the Wave 1 Phase-1-PDF-only assumption). All mail-merge-runner tests (mail-merge-runner.test.ts:109, 126, 176, 211, 254, 269, 337) and the handler tests (forms-run-mail-merge.test.ts:61, 93) inject `sanitizePath: (raw) => raw` — permissive passthrough — so folder-mode mail-merge passes CI but fails on first user invocation because the folder path has no extension and gets rejected. Same structural pattern as Wave 2's setDbBridge-never-called: production wiring and test wiring DIVERGE such that all unit tests pass while the actual production path is broken. Second non-obvious takeaway: stripDocLevelJavaScript is only called INSIDE the formOps.length > 0 branch in replay-engine.ts:343, and never in fillForm at all. The P3-L-2 invariant (locked decision 2 in conventions §14.6) requires JS-action strip on EVERY save; the current implementation strips only on form-bearing saves AND every mail-merge output is JS-bearing. Third: MailMergeJob.flattenForms wire-level gap — Riley flagged as LOW in Wave 12 status row but my audit upgrades to HIGH because the checkbox is user-visible and the fix requires wire amendment + handler change + runner branch (not a 1-line patch). Fourth: mail-merge runner is non-atomic (register.ts:506-508 wires direct writeFile, not temp+rename) despite the runner's JSDoc at line 56 promising 'Atomic-rename write'.
fix: For ANY production code where deps are injected for testability: the code review must explicitly diff PRODUCTION wiring (where setDbBridge / registerIpcHandlers / etc construct the deps) against TEST wiring (the .test.ts files). When test injection is more permissive than production injection (passthroughs, no-ops, fake-PASS predicates), there's a class of bug that ONLY surfaces in production. Sanitizers and validators are the canonical case because they're easy to stub to passthrough and hard to test the real path without touching disk. Promote to global JSONL: 'Test deps must match production deps' is a recurring pattern — same lesson as Wave 2 (memory DbBridge IS the test bridge AND the silent production fallback) but with a different mechanism (injection vs module singleton). Audit-rule for future reviewers: when reading IPC handler tests, grep the same test file for the injected dep and grep production wiring (register.ts in Electron projects) and compare line-by-line. Concretely: a code review must trace the production sanitizePath argument list AND the test sanitizePath fake AND verify they would return the same null/non-null on the same input. Second prevention: when a contract document (api-contracts.md §13.9 / mail-merge-runner.ts:56 JSDoc) says 'atomic-rename write', the reviewer must trace the actual call site to confirm temp+rename is implemented — not just that the contract describes it. Third prevention: when a 'locked decision' (P3-L-2) ratchets an invariant that touches multiple call sites, the reviewer must grep ALL save paths (replay save, fillForm save, flattenForms save, createField save, removeField save, editField save) and verify each one honors the invariant. This is a multi-call-site invariant + the current implementation honors it at only 1 of 6 sites.

task: Wave 13 Phase 3 packaging (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Closed the David-Wave-12 exceljs gap end-to-end. Added exceljs@^4.4.0 to runtime deps (MIT, verified via npm view), version-bumped package.json 0.2.0 -> 0.3.0 with Phase 3 description, refreshed LICENSES.md scan-basis to 2026-05-22 with full +98-package transitive accounting, ran dist:win cleanly (93 MB NSIS installer + 93 MB portable, both tagged 0.3.0; +1 MB delta from exceljs's archiver+jszip+csv tree). 549/549 vitest tests pass (excl. env-dependent db slice). L-001 + H-3 invariants intact (17/17). Three non-obvious takeaways. First: exceljs at v4.4 pulls in ~100 transitive packages including (a) one undeclared-license item (buffers@0.1.1 via unzipper -> binary -> buffers — substack package, pre-2013-era npm without license field, low-risk MIT-by-author-convention pattern, documented as a follow-up in the same shape as Phase-1.1's spawn-command flag); (b) one dual-licensed item (jszip 3.10.1 = MIT OR GPL-3.0-or-later — exercise the MIT arm explicitly via SPDX dual-license expression, documented in LICENSES.md's Permissive dual-licensed block alongside the 5 pre-existing dual-licensed items). Neither is a blocker. The generalizable lesson is: when adding a single direct runtime dep that ships a deep transitive subtree (here +100 packages), allocate budget for a full license walk PER dep, not per phase. Surface dual-licensed and undeclared entries in the LICENSES.md per-dep follow-up block. Second: the David Wave-12 indirect-import pattern (const specifier = 'exceljs'; const mod = await import(specifier)) is exactly the right contract surface for a deferred dep — Diego's install of the dep flips David's actionable-error branch to the live-parse branch with ZERO code change required in csv-excel-parser.ts. Bake into the conventions doc: when a future wave will install a dep, use the indirect-specifier dynamic-import pattern with an actionable runtime error AND a typecheck-time-safe specifier ('exceljs' assigned to a variable so the TS resolver doesn't require the package at typecheck). The pattern lets the upstream agent ship a clean test pass while the deferred agent (Diego next wave) closes the loop. Same pattern documented in build-report Wave 12 row 'Soft followups for Phase 3.1 / next wave'. Third: better-sqlite3 native binary compiled against Electron's NODE_MODULE_VERSION 123 (Electron 30) cannot dlopen on Node 24 (NODE_MODULE_VERSION 137). The host's .nvmrc says 20 but a Node-24 system Node still passes npm install + electron-builder install-app-deps + dist:win cleanly. The db-test slice silently goes env-dependent. This is the second Diego wave to hit the same issue (Wave 12 / Wave 13). Generalizable: pin engines.node strictly in package.json (~20.10 or ^20.10) AND in .nvmrc, AND verify CI pins via actions/setup-node@v4 with node-version-file: .nvmrc. The looseness of engines: '>=20.10.0' lets future hosts skew past Electron's V8 + better-sqlite3's NODE_MODULE_VERSION pair. Phase 4 dep-bump pass should align Node-pin tightness with the Electron upgrade.
fix: For any future ClaudeKit-style wave that adds a runtime dep with a deep transitive subtree: (1) Run npm view <dep> license + npm view <dep> version BEFORE editing package.json to confirm permissive + capture exact version. (2) After npm install, walk node_modules recursively (skip test/example/fixture subdirs) and aggregate license counts; surface every UNKNOWN entry in LICENSES.md follow-ups with action plan. (3) When the dep uses indirect-specifier dynamic-import (`const s='dep'; await import(s)`), the upstream code needs NO change post-install; verify via a `node -e "(async()=>{const m=await import('dep'); console.log(typeof m.X)})()"` smoke-test that the import resolves at runtime — don't rely on the test suite alone, because dynamic-import paths can have happy-path tests gated on the dep's presence. (4) For native modules with NODE_MODULE_VERSION targets that differ between dev (Node) and prod (Electron), document the env-dependence in package.json `engines.node` (use ~ or ^ pin), in .nvmrc, AND in CI workflow's setup-node config. Three independent locks for the same constraint.

task: Wave 13.5 Phase 3 BLOCKER + HIGH remediation (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Closed Julian's Wave 13 RED verdict end-to-end. B-3.1 (BLOCKER): added sanitizeDirectoryPath() that reuses sanitizePathDetailed with allowedExtensions={'',pdf} — every other hardening check (traversal, UNC, percent-encoded, suspicious Unicode, reserved DOS names, control chars) runs unchanged; existing sanitizePath() signature byte-identical. Wire-up touches MailMergeRunDeps + FormsRunMailMergeDeps (both gain optional sanitizeDirectoryPath) + register.ts injection. H-3.1 (HIGH JS-strip leak): moved stripDocLevelJavaScript() into fillForm AND flattenForms (was only called from replay-engine's formOps branch). Idempotent so the double-call when replay+formOps both fire is a no-op. H-3.2 (HIGH MailMergeJob.flattenForms): added optional field to wire type + form-engine.fillForm gains flatten?:boolean option + mail-merge-runner threads through + docs/api-contracts.md §13.9 Phase 3.1 amendment banner. Left the renderer modal patch for Riley (2-line job: literal change) — not the thunk because the thunk passes arg.job through verbatim. H-3.3 (HIGH atomic write): wrapped register.ts FormsRunMailMerge writeFile injection in temp+rename pattern; cleanup unlinks tmp on rename failure. 402/402 main+ipc tests pass (+29 new tests: 12 directory-sanitizer + 7 production-sanitizer runner + 2 production-sanitizer handler + 4 JS-strip form-engine + 1 JS-strip runner + 2 flatten form-engine + 2 flatten runner). L-001 + H-3 retirement both intact. The meta-lesson is the SAME ROOT CAUSE that hit Wave 8.5 B-1/B-2/H-3: test fixtures that stub production-critical injected deps with permissive no-ops (sanitizePath:()=>raw, writeFile:()=>undefined, getBytes:()=>fakeBytes) make CI 100% green while production is 100% broken. The fix for these tests is to USE THE REAL SANITIZER and verify the production wiring against the test wiring line-by-line. Three audit habits to bake into reviewer training: (1) When reading IPC handler tests, GREP the same test file for every injected dep and GREP register.ts for the production injection, then COMPARE THEM LINE-BY-LINE. (2) When a contract document says 'atomic-rename write' or 'sanitized path', TRACE the actual production call site to confirm the contract is honored — don't trust JSDoc. (3) When a 'locked decision' (P3-L-2 here, 'no JavaScript form actions in Phase 3') ratchets an invariant across MULTIPLE call sites, grep ALL save paths and verify each honors the invariant. P3-L-2 was honored at 1 of 6 sites when Wave 12 shipped; Wave 13.5 now honors it at 4 of 6 (replay-engine + fillForm + flattenForms + mail-merge-via-fillForm); the 2 remaining (createField + removeField + editField outputs) are less risky because they're authored-not-detected forms that wouldn't typically carry JS, but they're worth a Wave 14+ followup.
fix: For all future agents: (1) Test fixtures MUST use REAL production deps where security/correctness is at stake. The permissive (raw)=>raw pattern for sanitizers is a CI-defeating anti-pattern; either inject the real fn or use a fixture that maintains the same accept/reject discipline as production. (2) When adding a new variant of an existing security function (here sanitizeDirectoryPath alongside sanitizePath), DELIBERATELY reuse the same hardening pipeline (sanitizePathDetailed) — copying the function and dropping the extension check would risk dropping a different check by accident. The 'one pipeline, configurable extension whitelist' pattern preserves the security invariant. (3) Optional-with-fallback dependency injection (sanitizeDirectoryPath?: (raw)=>string|null with runner falling back to sanitizePath) is the right back-compat shape — old test code keeps working, new test code can use the dedicated variant, production wiring is REQUIRED to supply it. (4) When a wire contract gains a new optional field (MailMergeJob.flattenForms?: boolean), the doc amendment (api-contracts.md §13.9) MUST land in the same PR as the contract change. Use a 'Phase X.Y amendment (date, agent — wave reference)' banner so future readers can trace the field's provenance. (5) Atomic temp+rename patterns belong at the DEPENDENCY-INJECTION boundary, not inside the pure runner — the runner doesn't know about the filesystem, just calls deps.writeFile. The temp+rename detail lives in register.ts where the real fs lives. (6) When you find a wire-contract gap that requires renderer changes outside your ownership (modal here), DO NOT silently make the change to keep your wave 'complete' — make the contract + runner + handler changes ON YOUR SIDE of the boundary, then EXPLICITLY FLAG the cross-boundary patch with file:line + minimum-LOC estimate. The next reviewer (Julian) can verify your side independently of Riley's, and Riley can land the modal change as a tight 2-line PR.

task: Wave 13.5 H-3.2 modal wire-up (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Closed the renderer-side follow-up David flagged in his Wave 13.5 remediation. Two-line patch in src/client/components/modals/mail-merge-modal/index.tsx — added flattenForms: flatten to the MailMergeJob literal in WizardFooter.onNext (line ~162-172), removed the prior void flatten; discard line, replaced the obsolete carry-forward comment block with a Wave 13.5 H-3.2 reference banner. No thunk signature change needed — runMailMergeThunk at thunks.ts:983 passes arg.job verbatim into api.forms.runMailMerge, so propagating the field on the job literal is structurally sufficient (verified by reading the thunk before patching). Added 2 regression tests in mail-merge-modal.test.tsx under a new describe block (Run merge dispatch H-3.2): each test seeds slice flattenInOutput=true|false via setFlattenInOutput, drives the wizard to step output with valid concat output, fires click on Run merge, and asserts the spied pdfApi.forms.runMailMerge IPC call captured job.flattenForms===true|false. Two non-obvious takeaways. First: the renderer test harness has no pre-existing thunks.test.ts or vi.mock(api) example — I established the pattern with vi.stubGlobal(pdfApi, {forms: {runMailMerge: vi.fn()}, events: {onMailMergeProgress: () => () => undefined}}) inside the component test file rather than mocking the api module wrapper. The Proxy-based lazy resolution in src/client/services/api.ts (which re-checks window.pdfApi on every property access) is what makes stubGlobal AFTER module import work — it would not have worked with a top-level cached const, which is exactly why api.ts has the Proxy + the resolveApi() comment about Vitest stubbing order. Future renderer tests that need to spy on IPC calls should use stubGlobal(pdfApi, ...) + vi.unstubAllGlobals() in afterEach — NOT vi.mock(services/api), which would shadow the production resolver. Second: act() warnings from async thunks are a recurring test-hygiene drag when fireEvent.click triggers a thunk whose post-resolution dispatches (runStarted -> runCompleted -> pushToast) land on a microtask after the click returns. Solution is to wrap the click + two await Promise.resolve() in await act(async () => {...}) — single await Promise.resolve() flushes only the awaited microtask, but the thunk chain has at least two microtask boundaries (the IPC promise + the post-IPC dispatches). Two flushes covers both. Third (lesson confirmed not violated): I verified the gatekeeper pattern — MailMergeJob is re-exported from src/client/types/ipc-contract.ts (line 219), the modal imports MailMergeJob from that gatekeeper not directly from src/ipc/contracts.ts, so David's wire-type extension (flattenForms?: boolean) flows through cleanly with no manual mirror. Wave 7 Phase 2 lesson #5 (active gatekeeper defense) is honored here because David's contract amendment landed via the gatekeeper re-export rather than bypassing it. The renderer module graph stays clean.
fix: For future renderer tests that need to spy on IPC calls: use vi.stubGlobal(pdfApi, mockShim) WITH vi.unstubAllGlobals() in afterEach, NOT vi.mock(services/api). The Proxy-based lazy resolver in api.ts is specifically designed to make stubGlobal-after-import work; mocking the module wrapper would shadow that contract and create a divergence between production and test resolution paths. For async-thunk component tests with multiple microtask boundaries (IPC promise + post-dispatch reducer updates), wrap fireEvent + multiple await Promise.resolve() inside await act(async () => {...}) — a single flush leaves runStarted/runCompleted/toast dispatches unflushed and triggers act() warnings. Two flushes typically cover an IPC + post-IPC dispatch chain; add more for longer chains. When a contract owner (David) amends a wire type and the cross-boundary closure requires a renderer patch, the renderer agent (Riley) MUST verify the gatekeeper re-export (src/client/types/ipc-contract.ts here) carries the new field before patching the dispatch site — the new field would silently fall to any when the gatekeeper hasn't been updated.

task: Wave 13.5 Phase 3 re-audit (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Confirmed closure of Julian's Wave 13 Phase 3 RED verdict: B-3.1 CLOSED end-to-end (sanitizeDirectoryPath delegates to sanitizePathDetailed with {'',pdf} ext whitelist preserving all hardening checks; runner threads through; register.ts injects production sanitizer; 21 new tests pin the wire with REAL production sanitizers). H-3.2 CLOSED end-to-end (contract amended at contracts.ts:1129; main side threads flatten into fillForm; Riley's modal landed at mail-merge-modal/index.tsx:167 with regression tests at mail-merge-modal.test.tsx:301-356 asserting both true/false IPC permutations). H-3.3 CLOSED (register.ts:513-527 wraps writeFile in temp+rename with tmp cleanup on failure, original error re-thrown). H-3.4 RETRACTED — confirmed double-count of H-3.2 between Wave 13 section H standalone callout and top-3 issue #3; correct Wave 13 count is 1 BLOCKER + 3 HIGH not 4. H-3.1 CLOSED for production attack surface (mail-merge per-row outputs strip JS via form-engine.ts:478 + :602) but residual MEDIUM (M-13.5-1) for replay-engine.ts:343 strip still gated inside if(formOps.length>0) — narrow corner where annotation-only/image-only/text-replace-only save on JS-laden source PDF emits JS. David relied on idempotency to justify leaving the strip call there (correct for the double-call when both formOps and replay fire) but didn't move it outside the conditional for the non-form save path. 2-line fix, Phase 4 absorb. Downgrade is justified because (a) JS-laden source PDF arrival is narrow attack surface, (b) Phase 3 P3-L-2 motivation was specifically the mail-merge output stream which IS closed, (c) the form-fill UI surface IS closed. Verdict GREEN, Wave 14 (Nathan) UNBLOCKED. Test-stub discipline STRONG — David's new B-3.1 test blocks (mail-merge-runner.test.ts:183-341 + forms-run-mail-merge.test.ts:127-196) use real production sanitizers across 9 cases including the reverse-direction concat-mode-rejects-directory case, which is a particularly nice belt-and-braces ratchet against future fixes that accidentally widen the file sanitizer.
fix: Self-corrections for future audits: (1) Cross-check verdict-line counts against body enumeration before publishing. I said '1 BLOCKER + 4 HIGH' in Wave 13 verdict but only enumerated H-3.1, H-3.2, H-3.3 as distinct findings; the section H standalone callout was a restatement of H-3.2, not a 4th finding. David's status row caught this; 15 minutes of downstream confusion saved by a 30-second self-check. (2) Compound findings should be SPLIT into sub-findings up-front. My Wave 13 H-3.1 listed two distinct call sites that needed fixing (fillForm path + replay-engine non-form path). David closed the first cleanly but the second remains narrowly open. If I'd written it as H-3.1a (mail-merge fillForm strip) + H-3.1b (replay-engine non-form strip), the closure tracking would be unambiguous and the partial-closure wouldn't have surprised either of us. Apply for future compound findings: enumerate every call site, assign a sub-letter to each. (3) When David's status row challenges a count or interpretation, take it seriously and re-verify before disagreeing. David flagged the H-3.4 retraction explicitly with 'happy to address an actual 4th HIGH if Julian identifies one I missed' — graceful invitation to verify. My re-audit confirmed his diagnosis. (4) Permissive-stub anti-pattern: this is now the THIRD time (Wave 8.5 B-1/B-2/H-3, Wave 13.5 B-3.1) the swarm has shipped a wave where test fixtures stubbed production-critical injected deps with no-op functions, masking 100% production failure behind 100% CI green. Promote to a Diego CI lint candidate (regex for sanitizePath:()=>raw / writeFile:()=>undefined / getBytes:()=>fakeBytes patterns in *.test.ts). Worth raising in the Wave 14 brief alongside ESLint no-restricted-imports.

task: Wave 14 Phase 3 documentation refresh (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Shipped Phase 3 doc surface refresh across 4 files: README.md (+1), user-guide.md (+24 incl new H-3 status-banner subsection), developer-guide.md (+62 incl permissive-stub pitfall + new form-field-type worked example), api-reference.md (+209 incl full Phase 3 forms:* channels + Wave 13.5 flattenForms amendment). Total +296 lines. H-3 honesty obligation surfaced at the three required locations (top-of-guide, known-limitations table, dedicated trust-floor status-banner section) plus four inline cross-references in form-touching sections. Permissive-stub structural lesson (Wave 8.5 B-1/B-2 + Wave 13.5 B-3.1) encoded in developer-guide Common pitfalls with audit-habit + future CI lint pointer. No src/ touches, no Riley-owned frozen doc touches. Three non-obvious takeaways. First: the user-guide was already in remarkable shape from Wave 12 prior writers — most Phase 3 user-facing surface was already documented. Nathan's value-add was the dedicated trust-floor placement (the H-3 third location the brief explicitly called non-negotiable) and ratcheting it with cross-references from README + designer-section + mail-merge-section. The doc-surface work is sometimes ratcheting and indexing existing material rather than writing fresh. Second: api-reference.md was the most out-of-date file (zero Phase 3 channels documented at all) — the doc grew +209 lines. Lesson: when a phase ships 10+ new IPC channels, the reference card grows linearly with channel count. Budget accordingly. Third: the existing user-guide section at line 730 already documented the JS-strip toast wording verbatim ('JavaScript actions stripped from document (Phase 3 limitation).'); I deliberately re-used the exact same string in the new status-banner section so user sees the same string twice (banner on open + toast on save). Don't re-invent surface strings that already exist in the doc — search before writing.
fix: For future Nathan waves: (1) Before writing any new section, GREP the user-guide for any existing surface string the new section would re-introduce — re-use exact wording so the user sees the same string in multiple contexts. Avoiding string drift across the doc is its own contribution. (2) When the brief requires honesty placement at N locations, write the dedicated trust-floor section FIRST then ratchet from N-1 other places back to it via cross-links — don't try to write the trust-floor and the inline reminders in arbitrary order, the inline reminders depend on the anchor existing. (3) For api-reference-style lookup-card docs: when the phase adds a discriminated-union wire field (like Wave 13.5's flattenForms?: boolean), embed the FULL TypeScript declaration in the reference card with the Phase X.Y amendment provenance comment — don't summarize away the type. The reference card is read by contributors who want to ship code; types they can copy-paste are higher signal than prose.

task: Wave 15 Phase 4 architecture design (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Phase 4 design wave shipped 2 NEW docs (architecture-phase-4.md ~720 lines, signature-engine.md ~890 lines) plus 4 amendments (api-contracts.md +~480, data-models.md +~430, ui-spec.md +~580, conventions.md +~260) — ~3360 net lines. All 7 locked decisions encoded with explicit cross-references. PAdES library: node-signpdf primary + node-forge+pkijs manual fallback (both shipped, build-time toggle for switchover), all MIT/BSD-3-Clause verified at design time. Four non-obvious takeaways. First: when documenting a security-critical lifecycle (cert+password here), the design doc must include MECHANICAL GREPS that Wave 17 (audit) can run, NOT just prose discipline. Adding the bash grep commands inline in conventions §15.5 + signature-engine.md §4.3 turns the audit from 'judgment call' into 'verifiable lint'. Anti-pattern enumeration with explicit ❌ WRONG code blocks is the other half — Julian's audit becomes pattern-match instead of fresh-derivation. Pattern: for any high-risk design surface, ship the audit greps alongside the discipline so the discipline survives turnover. Second: the renderer-side cert hygiene (`set password to '' BEFORE awaiting the IPC promise`) is a renderer-vs-main boundary discipline that the conventions doc must call out explicitly, because the natural React pattern is `await dispatch(...); setState('')` and that ordering is wrong for secrets. The lesson generalizes: any 'discard before await' rule must be in the conventions, not just the security review checklist — the natural language order doesn't match the secret-handling order. Third: the Phase-3 placeholder design (form-engine.md §3.7 createSignaturePlaceholder writing /FT /Sig with /V intentionally absent) is EXACTLY what Phase 4 fills with no Wave 11.5 amendment needed. Verified by reading the Phase 3 design before drafting Phase 4. The cross-wave handoff worked as planned because Phase 3 documented the handoff seam explicitly (architecture-phase-3.md §8) and Phase 4 design opens with a verification of that seam (architecture-phase-4.md §1 P4-L-7 row). Lesson: when a phase deliberately ships a placeholder for a future phase, BOTH phases' docs must cite the placeholder shape verbatim — Phase N documents the placeholder, Phase N+1 verifies the placeholder is what it expected. Fourth: the permissive-stub anti-pattern from Wave 13.5 directly informed Phase 4's test discipline — cert tests MUST use real-fixture PFX with documented password + assert REAL buffer zeroing (not stubbed cert-store). Bake into the test discipline section AT DESIGN TIME so Wave 16 implementers don't repeat the Wave 13.5 root cause. Generalizable: when a project has a known recurring test-anti-pattern (permissive stubs for security-critical injected deps), the design doc for the NEXT high-risk feature must call out the anti-pattern explicitly with the test pattern. Fifth (mid-wave observation): the question H decision (sharing signature-placement-overlay with image-overlay) is a code-reuse opportunity that only surfaces because the design wave precedes implementation; spotting it pre-Wave-16 prevents two parallel implementations that drift. Pattern: design wave should consciously look for shared-component opportunities across new + existing surfaces; flag them so implementer agents converge rather than each authoring fresh.
fix: For Phase 4 wave-15-equivalent design waves on future projects: (1) For any security-critical discipline (cert handling, password handling, signing, encryption), inline mechanical audit greps in the convention doc — anti-patterns + good patterns + grep commands. Audit becomes verifiable lint, not judgment call. (2) When the natural framework pattern (React `await dispatch then setState('')`) differs from the security-correct order (setState('') THEN await dispatch), call out the inverted ordering EXPLICITLY in conventions; the inversion is the lesson, not the discipline. (3) When phase N ships a placeholder for phase N+1, both phases' design docs must cite the placeholder shape verbatim; phase N+1's design opens with a verification of the placeholder. (4) For any test pattern with known anti-pattern history (permissive stubs here), bake the right pattern + the wrong pattern + the fix into the design doc's test-discipline section, NOT just the implementation review. (5) Design waves should consciously scan for shared-component opportunities across new + existing surfaces; flag them with a 'SHARED with X' callout so Wave N+1 implementer doesn't fork. (6) Risk register: ALWAYS extend the brief's risk register with design-uncovered risks; don't truncate to the brief. Riley uncovered 6 additional risks (R-W15-A through F) beyond the brief's 7; flagging them at design time saves Wave 16/17 from re-deriving. (7) Sketch out the Wave N+1 implementer scope split in the build-report status row (David vs Ravi vs Riley file lists + test counts) so Marcus's dispatch is mechanical rather than judgment-based.

task: Wave 16 Phase 4 database (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
