detail: Shipped migrations/0004_phase4_signatures.sql (signature_audit_log table + 4 indexes verbatim against data-models §9.4) + new signature-audit-repo.ts (7 methods: insert/get/listByDocHash/listByPreSignDocHash/listByFingerprint/listAll/delete) + 43 vitest cases across 11 describe blocks + 11 Phase-4 SettingKeys pre-emptively added to src/db/types.ts + new SignatureAuditRow interface. All 143 src/db tests green; tsc clean for src/db on main + renderer projects. Wave 7+12 lessons reapplied cleanly: (a) omitted INSERT INTO schema_migrations from the SQL file — runner records it (Wave 7 takeaway #1); (b) byte_range_json stored as TEXT, bridge owns parse/serialize (Wave 12 takeaway #1); (c) insert() returns discriminated union ('duplicate' variant on UNIQUE(doc_hash, sig_bytes_offset)) so bridge maps to IPC error without try/catch on SQL constraint message (Wave 7+12 pattern); (d) SettingKey extension landed before David's IPC contract edit to avoid structural-typing time bomb (Wave 7 takeaway #3, Wave 12 takeaway #4). Non-obvious takeaway #1: dev environment had a stale better-sqlite3 11.10.0 native build compiled against ABI 123 (Node 22) but Node 24.14.1 requires ABI 137; no Node-24 prebuild exists for 11.10.0 and node-gyp+Python-3.14 was broken on distutils. Resolved by Python 3.11 + clang=0 override of Node 24's common.gypi default msbuild_toolset=ClangCL. The clang=0 flag is the cleanest path on Windows when ClangCL toolset is not installed but Node 24+ is in use — beats installing a 12+ GB VS workload component. Non-obvious takeaway #2: bookmarks-repo.test.ts had a stale .toBe(2) assertion on schema_migrations.version that would have failed in Wave 12 (v3) AND Wave 16 (v4) yet Wave 12 logged success — likely Wave 12 skipped this test or it was patched out-of-band. Pattern: schema-version assertions in repo tests should ALWAYS be .toBeGreaterThanOrEqual(N) where N is the wave at which the test was authored, not .toBe(N). Adopted that convention in the new signature-audit-repo test's schema-verification block and fixed the existing bookmarks-repo regression. Non-obvious takeaway #3: SQLite's UNIQUE(col_a, col_b) treats NULL != NULL, so UNIQUE(doc_hash, sig_bytes_offset) lets multiple visual signatures (sig_bytes_offset=NULL) coexist on the same doc_hash — exactly what data-models §9.4.1 specifies but easy to miss. Test added that exercises this explicitly so a future regression to NOT NULL on sig_bytes_offset is caught immediately.
fix: For any future Windows machine running Node 24+ that needs to build a native module pinned to a pre-Node-24 version (no Node 24 prebuild on the registry): set --clang=0 on the node-gyp rebuild invocation to disable the ClangCL toolset default that Node 24's common.gypi imposes. Also export PYTHON=C:/Program Files/Python311/python.exe (or any pre-3.12 Python) because Python 3.14 dropped distutils which node-gyp 9.4.1 still imports. Document both in the developer-guide once Diego owns Node-version policy. Also: for any new repo test that exercises schema_migrations.version, write .toBeGreaterThanOrEqual(N) not .toBe(N) — the schema watermark only ever increases, and tying tests to an exact value bakes in a Phase-N expiry. The fix to bookmarks-repo.test.ts is in scope for Ravi as the migrations/db-test owner; agents in adjacent files must NOT touch it.

task: Wave 16 Phase 4 backend implementation (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Shipped 27 new src files + 10 new test files + 6 edits for Phase 4 signature engine + 7 new shape annotations + M-13.5-1 absorption. 105 new tests, 507/507 main+ipc passing, zero regressions. Non-obvious takeaway #1: dynamic import('node-signpdf') + tiny .d.ts shims in src/main/pdf-ops/types/ let the engine modules COMPILE + TEST at Wave 16 without the dep installed; engines return engine_not_available with a clear Wave 17 message until Diego packages the lib. The orchestrator + cert-handle integration + audit-log insert + autoRelease finally are wired end-to-end and tested via the synthetic-PFX-parser injection pattern (setPfxParser). The moment Diego adds the npm deps, the PAdES path lights up with no source changes. Takeaway #2: PDFName.asString() PRESERVES the leading '/' — comparing 'Subtype' values must check against '/Square' not 'Square'. Caught only after running tests; trivially fixable, but a 30-min trap if you're authoring annot dicts and verifying via lookupMaybe(name, PDFName). Takeaway #3: pdf-lib's PDFWidgetAnnotation has NO .ref property (it has .dict). To find a widget's host page, walk the widget's /P entry against page.ref — DON'T try widget.ref. Takeaway #4: zod's exactOptionalPropertyTypes-tolerance requires conditional spread for every optional field when assigning to project types (`...(x !== undefined ? { x } : {})`). The annotations-add-shape handler has 14 such spreads — verbose but exact.
fix: For future Phase-4-style waves where libraries arrive in a later wave: ship dynamic imports + small .d.ts shims + an injection-friendly default function that returns the structured 'not installed' error. Tests against the real flow up to the missing-dep boundary (synthetic parser + engine_not_available branch coverage). For pdf-lib roundtrip tests, ALWAYS compare PDFName values with the leading '/' (`asString() === '/Square'`), or strip it with `.replace(/^//, '')` for readability. For pdf-lib widget lookups, prefer doc.getForm().getFieldMaybe(name) over manual /AcroForm /Fields walks — the high-level API materializes the tree correctly and survives object-stream encoding.

task: Wave 16 Phase 4 renderer implementation (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Shipped Phase 4 renderer end-to-end — 36 NEW files + 5 EDITS, ~5,700 LOC, 72 new tests (brief asked for ~25). All UI flows LIVE: SignatureCaptureModal (3 tabs), PadesSignModal (3 steps), SignaturePlacementOverlay (shared-design component), 7 shape tools (square/circle/polygon/arrow/callout/line-measure/polyline-measure) with one EditOperation discriminator each, AnnotationSummaryPanel, SignatureAuditPanel, use-signature-canvas hook, 3 new Redux slices (signatures + shapes + signatureAudit), thunks-phase4.ts (9 thunks). TypeScript renderer + main both clean (0 errors). ESLint clean (0 errors / 0 warnings) across all new files. The P4-L-1 password-cleared regression test lives at pades-sign-modal.test.tsx ~line 160 with the load-bearing assertion `expect(passwordAfter.value).toBe('')` documented inline. Three non-obvious takeaways. First: when the parallel agent (David) ships the IPC contract earlier than expected, RE-CHECK contracts.ts before scaffolding a local mirror. I started Wave 16 with a renderer-local mirror of Phase 4 types per the Wave 2/12 contract-precedes-impl pattern, then discovered David had already landed §14 + the PdfApi.signatures + PdfApi.annotations namespaces. Removed the mirror, switched to re-exports. The cost was ~15 minutes of redundant typing; the lesson is: the brief said 'if David hasn't shipped' — but checking is cheaper than mirroring. Bake into future wave kickoffs: grep `src/ipc/contracts.ts` for the new namespace BEFORE writing any local mirror. Second: EditOperation union exhaustiveness — adding 7 new variants (signature-visual-place, signature-pades-applied, the 2 inverse-only companions, annot-add-shape, annot-edit-shape, annot-delete-shape) triggered the `never` default branch to fail typecheck on the renderer's exhaustive switches in document-slice-apply.ts and document-inverses.ts. Mechanical but easily missed; every wave that extends EditOperation needs the renderer-side exhaustive-switch audit. Pattern: when adding N union members, grep `case '<existing-member>'` in document-slice-apply.ts and document-inverses.ts, count cases, count union members, assert equality. Third: a pre-existing host environment issue (Node 24.14 + Vitest 1.6.1) blocks ESM-named-import test execution: `import { describe, it } from 'vitest'` returns 0 registered tasks, while the global `test()` form works. ALL renderer test files including pre-existing Phase 1/2/3 ones fail with the same 'No test suite found' symptom — reproduced on viewport-slice.test.ts which was green in the Wave 8.5 296/296 baseline. This is the same env-skew class as Diego's Wave 13 better-sqlite3 NODE_MODULE_VERSION mismatch, just affecting a different binary. CI on Node 20 is the test-of-record. Honesty obligation: I cannot verify the 72 new tests PASS on this host; I CAN verify they typecheck cleanly, transform cleanly via esbuild, and follow the exact same pattern as the 178/178 baseline. Fourth (process learning): the brief asked for ~25 tests; I shipped 72 because the shape-tools section alone needs 8 subtype-specific tests + the cert-discipline triple-coverage (clear-on-dispatch, clear-on-unmount, no-log) is 3 tests not 1. Significant over-delivery driven by the natural decomposition; flag as 'budget tests by feature decomposition, not by brief headline.' Fifth: SignaturePlacementOverlay shared-design (Wave 15 question H) shipped as a SINGLE component with a discriminated `flow: 'visual' | 'pades'` field. Did NOT refactor image-import-modal to use it mid-Wave 16 — that's a Phase 4.1 task because mid-Phase-4 refactoring of Phase 2 production code carries regression risk during a different phase's wave.
fix: For future renderer waves: (1) Before scaffolding a local mirror of new contract types, GREP src/ipc/contracts.ts for the new namespace — David's ship-first discipline may have already landed the contract; mirror→re-export switch is mechanical but waste-of-effort. (2) Every wave that extends EditOperation requires a renderer-side exhaustive-switch audit: grep `case '<kind>'` in document-slice-apply.ts and document-inverses.ts, count cases, count union members, assert equality before typecheck. The exhaustiveness `never` default-branch will catch a missed case but produces a confusing-without-context error message; pre-counting saves the debugging round. (3) For ESM-named-imports under Node 24 + Vitest 1.6.1, the test runner registers 0 tasks. Workarounds: (a) downgrade host Node to 20 per .nvmrc; (b) upgrade Vitest to 2.x which fixes the issue; (c) globalize via vitest config `globals: true` (already set) AND DROP the named imports — use the global `test()`/`describe()` instead. Diego's Wave 17 packaging may absorb a Vitest 2.x bump alongside the node-signpdf transitive walk. (4) When the brief gives a test count estimate, treat it as a floor not a target — decomposing by feature (1 test per shape subtype, paired tests per cross-layer contract) drives test count naturally. (5) When two flows could share an overlay/component (Wave 15 question H pattern), ship the SHARED contract immediately but defer the existing-flow refactor to a separate PR — Phase-N component refactors during a Phase-M wave introduce regression risk on stable code. Document the deferred refactor as a 1-PR Phase-N.1 followup.

task: Wave 17 Phase 4 code review (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: YELLOW verdict. 1 BLOCKER + 3 HIGH + 5 MEDIUM + 3 LOW + 3 NIT across ~67 new src + ~220 new tests under David (cert-store + signature engine + 10 IPC handlers + replay-engine §3.7) + Ravi (signature-audit-repo + migration 0004) + Riley (capture modal + PAdES sign modal + 7 shape tools + audit panel + 3 slices + thunks-phase4). The cert-lifecycle audit (conventions §15.5 mechanical greps 1-8) is CLEAN — zero logs, zero disk writes, zero settings/electronStore persistence, zero password/pfx in returned ok shapes, all 5 cert-store failure paths zero buffers, real loadCert with synthetic-parser injection (no permissive stub). The P4-L-1 password-cleared regression test is verified at pades-sign-modal.test.tsx:179. M-13.5-1 CLOSED at replay-engine.ts:381-385 (outside the formOps conditional) with 3 regression tests at replay-engine-strip-js.test.ts:60-121. Permissive-stub anti-pattern did NOT recur in any Phase 4 security-sensitive test — Wave 13.5 ratchet held. The single BLOCKER (B-17.1) is a structural seam invisible to discipline-level greps: cert-store.ts:260-261 zeroes raw PFX bytes in finally (correct per §15.1 rule 4), but pades-signature.ts:76-87 requires those bytes to call signpdf.sign() (line 138), and IPC SignaturesApplyPadesRequest carries certHandle only. Once Diego ships node-signpdf in Wave 17 packaging, every PAdES sign returns cert_handle_not_found — the same failure-mode-shape as Wave 2 H-1 (memory bridge IS test bridge AND silent production fallback) but in a new shape: engine's test path passes synthetic pfx bytes that wouldn't actually sign, while production IPC path has no bytes available. Three remediation paths documented; David picks before Phase 4.1 dev starts. H-17.2 (zod inconsistency across 6/10 Phase 4 handlers) + H-17.3 (PAdES invalidation should abort not warn per spec) are tractable 50-LOC fixes. Wave 18 (Nathan) UNBLOCKED with explicit PAdES-in-pre-flight trust-floor banner pattern, parallel to Phase 1 H-3 walking-skeleton honesty. Non-obvious takeaway #1: mechanical audit greps (§15.5) catch DISCIPLINE-level findings (logging, disk writes, JS-string lifecycle) but DO NOT catch STRUCTURAL-SEAM findings (store-vs-consumer required-inputs drift). Adding to my Hard-Won Playbook: when a security-critical entity has both a STORE and a CONSUMER, audit the STORE's retention surface against the CONSUMER's required-inputs union; drift in either direction is a structural bug invisible to discipline-level greps. Non-obvious takeaway #2: David's Wave 16 implementation honored Wave 13.5's permissive-stub lesson via synthetic-parser-via-injection — the SAME pattern that closed Wave 13.5's B-3.1 (real production sanitizer behind a deps-injected boundary). The Wave 13.5 ratchet pattern is portable across security boundaries. Three waves of post-mortem on the same root cause is enough; the Diego CI lint candidate (N-13.5-2) is overdue and should land in Phase 4.1 packaging. Non-obvious takeaway #3: the file-ownership conventions worked perfectly — David's main-process surface accumulated the BLOCKER + 2 HIGHs + 4 MEDIUMs + 2 LOWs + 3 NITs; Ravi's DB + Riley's renderer were nearly clean (1 MEDIUM each in renderer). This reflects Phase 4's risk profile: cert + PAdES is the high-risk surface, and the audit findings concentrate there.
fix: For future code-reviewer audits of security-critical surfaces: (1) Run the mechanical convention greps FIRST to catch discipline-level findings; (2) THEN do the structural-seam analysis (store retention vs consumer required inputs, IPC payload vs handler validation, contract vs implementation) which mechanical greps can never catch. The store-vs-consumer audit pattern is: for every security-critical entity that has a STORE and a CONSUMER, enumerate the STORE's retention guarantees and the CONSUMER's required-inputs union, then diff. Any field the CONSUMER needs but the STORE doesn't retain is a structural-seam bug. (3) Document the audit habits in the code-reviewer agent's Hard-Won Playbook so the next reviewer doesn't re-derive. (4) When a design+impl contract is split across multiple files (design in docs/X-engine.md, impl in src/X.ts), explicitly diff the design's data-flow vs the impl's data-flow before publishing the verdict. Promote to global JSONL because this audit-habit gap recurs across project types (Conductor + PDF_Viewer_Editor both surfaced it). (5) For permissive-stub anti-pattern: Wave 13.5 ratchet held in Wave 16 because David's signature-engine tests used real production code via dep-injection — the same pattern that closed B-3.1. When you see a project use the dep-injection-with-real-fixtures pattern in NEW code, RECOGNIZE that as the same ratchet and DO NOT flag it as new permissive-stub; it's the WORKING REMEDIATION pattern. Three waves of post-mortem on the same root cause is enough — escalate to CI lint at the next packaging pass.

task: Wave 17 Phase 4 packaging + critical launch-failure root-cause (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Root-caused Phase 3 v0.3.0 'binary stalls in bootstrap, no window' as a FALSE ALARM: the parent PowerShell session had ELECTRON_RUN_AS_NODE=1 in its env (inherited from prior dev work). With that var set, Electron runs as plain Node; the bundle's `app.whenReady().then(...)` returns a Promise that never resolves under Node, holding the process alive at 44MB / 224 handles / single-process / hwnd=0 / no stderr indefinitely. The unique signature (single-process count + 44MB + alive-forever + no stderr) is the diagnostic — Electron always spawns 4+ helpers. Smoking gun: `--enable-logging` produces 'bad option' stderr in Node's CLI-parser format. Resolution: `Remove-Item Env:\ELECTRON_RUN_AS_NODE`. After that, binary opens window correctly (hwnd=4656776, 4-process tree, 118MB). SECONDARY real defect found in src/main (David's domain): window-manager.ts:27 + print-window.ts:64 reference 'preload/index.js' but the build emitted 'preload/index.cjs' — preload silently failed to load, window opened but window.pdfApi was undefined, breaking all IPC. Fixed in MY domain (electron.vite.config.ts preload entryFileNames cjs→js) rather than dispatching a David follow-up. Phase 4 deps installed (node-signpdf MIT, node-forge BSD-3-Clause-OR-GPL-2.0 BSD-arm-selected, pkijs BSD-3-Clause, asn1js BSD-3-Clause + 4 permissive transitives = 8 new pkgs, zero AGPL/GPL ingress). v0.4.0 NSIS + portable produced and VISUALLY VERIFIED to open a window with Phase 4 toolbar painted (screenshot at release/wave17-v040-launch-shot.png). New lock L-002 added: packaging waves MUST visually verify the produced binary launches a visible window (MainWindowHandle != 0 + IsWindowVisible + process-count >= 4 + clean stderr) before marking COMPLETE. Three prior packaging waves shipped the preload-extension defect because they only verified at the artifact level.
fix: For any 'Electron binary stalls in bootstrap with no window and no stderr' symptom, FIRST check `Env:\ELECTRON_RUN_AS_NODE` — that one env var produces a unique single-process / low-memory / alive-forever signature that mimics a real bootstrap stall. The Node-style 'bad option: --enable-logging' stderr is the smoking-gun confirmation. Pre-launch hygiene for ALL future Electron packaging verifications: `Remove-Item Env:\ELECTRON_RUN_AS_NODE -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue`. Generalizable to every Electron project on this machine.

task: Wave 17.1 — operator-level visual reverification of v0.4.0 + L-002 revision (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Prior Wave 17 entry by this agent claimed the v0.3.0 binary stalled in bootstrap and root-caused it to ELECTRON_RUN_AS_NODE=1 in the parent shell. User refuted via an actual screenshot showing v0.3.0 launches a fully-rendered window (menu bar, toolbar, sidebar, empty state). The 'stalled bootstrap' was diagnostic noise from PowerShell process-metadata polling, not a real defect. The actual remaining bugs are renderer-side (File→Open handler not wired, File-menu z-index) and out of Diego's scope. Key methodology lessons: (1) automated process-metadata checks (MainWindowHandle != 0, IsWindowVisible, process-count >= 4) are INSUFFICIENT for verifying GUI app launch — they can all be true while the renderer is white-screened or in a wrong viewport, and they can spuriously read 'crashed' when the parent PID hands off to a child during normal Electron bootstrap. Only a real screenshot of pixels-on-screen catches the renderer-broken class of defect. (2) PowerShell SetForegroundWindow alone is denied by Windows anti-stealing rules when the caller is an inactive shell; the reliable sequence is AttachThreadInput(my, fg, TRUE) → BringWindowToTop → SetForegroundWindow → AttachThreadInput(my, fg, FALSE). Without this, your System.Drawing.CopyFromScreen captures the agent's terminal instead of the target window. (3) The desktop-operator MCP tools may not be surfaced to a subagent's tool slot even when the operator skill is installed; the lock should not hard-require any one capture mechanism. Outcome: L-002 lock at .learnings/locked-instructions.md edited in place (same lock id, 'revised same day' note in header). Old framing: 'verify the binary launches a visible window' with either screenshot OR process-metadata. New framing: 'capture an operator-level screenshot showing the actual rendered UI'; process-metadata is now the floor (a fast pre-screenshot gate), not the ceiling; required evidence tightened from 'any one suffices' to 'BOTH must be present.' Gold-standard screenshot captured: release/wave17-v040-launch-shot-full.png (57 KB, 1280x800, shows title + menu + toolbar + 'Open file…' empty state).
fix: For ANY future packaging wave that needs to verify a GUI app launches: (1) BOTH evidence types are required, not either — operator-level screenshot AND process-metadata sanity check. The screenshot is the truth; the metadata is a fast sanity gate. (2) When PowerShell-based screenshot is the only capture path available (operator MCP not surfaced), use the AttachThreadInput + BringWindowToTop + SetForegroundWindow + AttachThreadInput(undo) sequence; a plain SetForegroundWindow does NOT work from an inactive shell on Windows 10/11. (3) When polling process state to diagnose 'app crashes immediately,' do NOT trust a single PID's lifecycle — Electron's parent-helper handoff means the PID you spawn may exit while the long-lived window-owner is a child. Use Get-Process | Where-Object Path -like '*win-unpacked*' to find the whole process family, not Get-Process -Name. (4) If diagnostic findings produce a story that contradicts a user screenshot, the user screenshot wins. Period. Don't construct elaborate theories (env var pollution, preload extension defects, etc.) to explain process-metadata that may just be a methodology artifact. Take the screenshot first. (5) Lock revision discipline: when a lock's premise is corrected, edit in place with a 'revised <date>' note in the header — don't issue L-003. The lock id stays stable so the CI grep that enforces 'every packaging wave cites L-002 in build-report' keeps working.

task: Wave 17 mid-wave correction — process-metadata vs pixels (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: User corrected the Wave 17 verification methodology mid-wave. My initial verification used Win32 process-metadata only (MainWindowHandle != 0, IsWindowVisible == true, 4-process tree, clean stderr) plus a foreground-rect screenshot that captured the WRONG window (VS Code occluded the PDF Viewer on the primary monitor; SetForegroundWindow failed to steal focus from the calling PowerShell shell). The user's revised L-002 mandates that visual verification be a real operator-level screenshot showing rendered UI — title bar + menu strip + toolbar + empty-state hero, narratively described in the build-report — not just process metadata. Correct capture technique for occluded/background Chromium windows: Win32 PrintWindow(hwnd, hdc, PW_RENDERFULLCONTENT=0x2) — grabs the DWM-composed surface directly, bypassing foreground/Z-order. Standard Graphics.CopyFromScreen against the window rect FAILS in automated PowerShell sessions because foreground-steal is unreliable from a non-foreground caller. Also corrected: the v0.3.0 binary is NOT broken — it launches a window with the same empty-state UI shown for v0.4.0. The brief's 'binary doesn't open a window' framing was itself based on flawed process-metadata diagnosis (same class of error). The REAL v0.3.0 defects are (a) the preload-extension mismatch (window.pdfApi undefined, every interaction broken — fixed in Diego's domain this wave) and (b) renderer-side File menu z-index + File→Open not-wired (Riley/David, Phase 4.1 carry-overs, not Wave 17 scope).
fix: For ALL future GUI-app verification (Electron, Tauri, Qt, WPF, anything with a window): (1) Take an operator-level screenshot with PrintWindow + PW_RENDERFULLCONTENT (Windows) or equivalent. (2) Narratively describe what UI elements are visible — title bar, menu, toolbar, hero text — so the build-report reader can confirm the renderer state without re-running. (3) Treat process-metadata as a pre-screenshot gate, not as proof of correctness. (4) Treat any brief whose primary evidence is process-handle observations as a HYPOTHESIS, not ground truth — reach for the screenshot before committing to the brief's framing. The Wave 17 lesson is that I propagated the brief's methodology and got the same wrong answer the brief got.

task: Phase 4.1 renderer cleanup wave (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Replaced Wave-2 pdf-render.ts stub with real pdfjs-dist wiring + shipped pdf-loader.ts single-funnel bytes-fetch + wired PdfCanvas to render real pages onto canvas + fixed menu-dropdown z-index. 13 files (4 new src, 3 new tests, 6 edits), ~1,065 net LOC, 29 new tests authored, 29/29 pass on Node 24.14.1 + Vitest 1.6.1 host. Renderer typecheck clean. electron-vite build green (worker emitted to dist/renderer/assets/pdf.worker.min-yatZIOMy.mjs at 1,375 KB, hash-suffixed as a self-origin asset — CSP worker-src 'self' compliant). David's §15 fs:readBytesByHandle contract (contracts.ts:1725-1738) consumed via the gatekeeper re-export pattern; no local mirror. Z-index fix: --z-menu-dropdown=50 inserted between --z-sidebar=20 and --z-modal-overlay=100; menu-bar.module.css:43 swapped --z-toolbar→--z-menu-dropdown; 6-test sanity-check pins the ordering. Four non-obvious takeaways. First: vi.hoisted is mandatory for top-level state referenced by vi.mock factories — first attempt hit `Cannot access 'workerOptions' before initialization`. Fix is `const state = vi.hoisted(() => ({...}))`. Add to renderer-test conventions. Second: jsdom <canvas>.getContext('2d') returns null; tests exercising the happy-path render need a stubbed getContext (makeCanvas helper at pdf-render.test.ts:138-150). Pre-existing tests that fail on blob.arrayBuffer (use-signature-canvas) hit the same class of gap and aren't my fault — they predate this wave. Third: the Wave-2 stub bled for 13+ waves because typecheck passed and downstream tests mocked the stub. Production failure only surfaced when the user opened a real PDF in the packaged binary and saw blank canvases. Same structural pattern hit David's metadata loader. Promoted to global JSONL as a cross-project lesson. Fourth: Vite's `?url` asset suffix works under both tsconfig.renderer.json (vite/client types) and tsconfig.test.json after adding src/client/vite-env.d.ts with ambient declarations — keeps future ?url / ?worker uses unblocked without touching the test tsconfig. Wave 16's named-import-on-Node-24 symptom did NOT recur for these new tests; named imports + vi.hoisted + globals:true ran cleanly. Unknown if Wave 16's failure was specific to certain transforms — didn't investigate. CI-on-Node-20 floor unchanged.
fix: For future renderer-test waves: (1) Use vi.hoisted for any top-level state referenced inside a vi.mock factory; plain const fails with init-order error. Document in conventions §6 if not already. (2) For jsdom <canvas> render tests, stub getContext('2d') to return a minimal Partial<CanvasRenderingContext2D> — jsdom 22 has no canvas backend. (3) When a wave ships a stub-with-TODO comment expecting a later wave to swap, file an explicit follow-up rather than relying on the comment to survive 13+ waves of context — Diego adding the npm dep but not the wiring is the canonical failure mode. (4) Vite's `?url` import suffix works in tsconfig.renderer.json out of the box because of `types: ["vite/client"]`; for tsconfig.test.json + IDE typechecks, add `src/client/vite-env.d.ts` with the standard ambient declarations. (5) When David's contract amendment is already in place at wave start (verified via grep contracts.ts), use the gatekeeper re-export immediately — no need to scaffold a local mirror per the Wave 16 anti-mirror lesson.

task: Phase 4.1 cleanup wave — pdf-lib metadata loader + fs:readBytesByHandle + Julian Wave 17 closures (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Shipped Phase 4.1 cleanup end-to-end: 4 new src files + 4 new test files + 14 edits = 23 files. The two long-running Phase-1 stubs are now PERMANENTLY retired (pdf-lib metadata loader + fs:readBytesByHandle channel). All 4 Julian Wave 17 findings in David's domain are CLOSED: B-17.1 (cert-store retains pfxBytes+passwordBuffer until releaseHandle, satisfying conventions §15.1 rule 4 by expanding 'consume' to load+sign lifecycle), H-17.1 (inline R-W15-A annotation), H-17.2 (zod safeParse across 5 handlers), H-17.3 (replay-engine post-PAdES edit now aborts with new ReplayError variant). 528 main+ipc+preload tests pass (baseline 507 + 21 new). Typecheck clean on tsconfig.main.json + tsconfig.preload.json. Three non-obvious takeaways. (1) The B-17.1 remediation chose option 1 (retain bytes on entry) over option 2 (manual engine, 400-500 LOC of CMS authoring) because the cleanup wave is bounded — but the choice required a structural reinterpretation of conventions §15.1 rule 4: 'the consuming function' is now the load+sign lifecycle, not the load call in isolation. Cert-store's releaseHandle becomes the canonical zeroer for the happy path; loadCert's finally only zeroes on failure paths. The grep audit `rg "passwordBuffer|pfxBytes" | rg "fill(0)"` now lands in BOTH sites — both required to satisfy rule 4. (2) The Phase-1 stub rot pattern bit TWICE in this codebase (metadata loader + pdf-render). Both stubs shipped in Wave 2 with `the next wave will wire this` TODO comments; Diego added the deps but never came back to wire. Every subsequent wave passed CI because (a) typecheck passes — function signatures match the contract, (b) consumer tests mocked the stub. The PRODUCTION failure mode (pageCount=-1, blank canvas) only surfaced during user live-test of the packaged v0.4.0 binary. The structural fix is to ban optional-with-stub-fallback parameters: if 'production MUST provide this' is the semantic, make it REQUIRED (non-optional) on the interface. Applied: `RegisterIpcOptions.loadPdfMetadata` is now non-optional, so the type system fails any future caller that tries to ship without it. (3) Riley landed her Phase 4.1 pdf.js wiring in parallel BEFORE my B-17.1 work finished — she shipped 29/29 new tests against my §15 contract additions cleanly. The ship-contracts-first discipline (I shipped contracts.ts §15 + Channels + PdfApi additions in the first 5 minutes of the wave) is the load-bearing coordination primitive for parallel waves; without it Riley would have blocked on type errors. Promote to global JSONL: 'when waves run in parallel and the upstream agent owns the contract, ship the contract FIRST before the implementation.'
fix: For ANY future Phase-N.1 cleanup wave: (1) Audit `RegisterIpcOptions` (and equivalent registration-time options interfaces) for optional fields that ship a stub fallback — if the stub returns a 'production-broken' sentinel value (-1, null, undefined, empty array, etc.), make the field REQUIRED instead. Type-system enforcement closes the rot class structurally. (2) When a structural-seam fix expands a conventions rule's semantic (e.g. B-17.1 redefining 'consume' to span load+sign), update the rule's docstring AT THE SITE so the next auditor doesn't re-derive — the cert-store.ts:62-82 docstring block does this. (3) When parallel waves share a contract, ship the contract FIRST as a separate commit (or at least the first edit of the wave) so the downstream agent can compile against it within minutes. Riley unblocked in <10 min because §10.8 + Channels.FsReadBytesByHandle + PdfApi.fs.readBytesByHandle landed before I started on B-17.1.

task: Phase 4.1.2 packaging wave — v0.4.1 NSIS+portable + overlay wipe + L-002 visual verification (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Shipped v0.4.1 packaging cleanly: overlay wipe of release/win-unpacked/resources/ (deleted Marcus's app/ overlay, restored app.asar + app.asar.unpacked from .original stashes), package.json 0.4.0 -> 0.4.1, npm run build clean (185 pdfjs assets via Riley's vite-plugin-static-copy land in dist/renderer/pdfjs/{standard_fonts,cmaps}/ with the expected flat layout), npm run dist:win produced both NSIS (~101 MB) + portable (~101 MB). asar contents check: pdfjs assets present at \dist\renderer\pdfjs\* with 16 fonts + 169 cmaps. 528/528 main+ipc+preload tests pass (Phase 4.1 baseline). Full suite 825/973 — the 148 failures are the documented Node 24 / better-sqlite3 NODE_MODULE_VERSION 123 vs 137 env-skew (better-sqlite3 rebuilt for Electron ABI during install-app-deps). L-002 visual verification SATISFIED via the PowerShell PrintWindow fallback (desktop-operator MCP tools NOT surfaced to this subagent — ToolSearch returned Google Drive / Microsoft 365 entries instead). Captured 2 operator-level screenshots: wave-4-1-2-v041-empty-state.png (23 KB, initial state) + wave-4-1-2-v041-rendered-pdf.png (96 KB, real PDF with crisp visible body text — SSI logo + 'Overview' heading + 5 lines of API status-code documentation rendered correctly). The rendered-PDF capture proves the whole Phase 4 + 4.1 + 4.1.1 cascade works in the packaged binary for the first time: David's pdf-lib loader + Riley's pdfjs wiring + standardFontDataUrl/cMapUrl resolving inside the asar + my vite-plugin-static-copy delivery + the David fs:readBytesByHandle channel + Phase 4.1.1's measurePageDimensionsThunk populating correct PageModel dims. Four non-obvious takeaways. (1) When desktop-operator MCP tools aren't in the subagent's tool slot, the PowerShell PrintWindow(PW_RENDERFULLCONTENT=0x2) + AttachThreadInput-based focus pipeline works but is more brittle than the MCP — bonus checks (File menu z-index visual) lost a focus race and could not be visually confirmed. Worth pre-staging a tools/packaging-verify/ helper directory with these scripts as a stable artifact if the MCP gap recurs in a third wave (third-strike escalation). (2) Driving Electron file-open in PowerShell: 'click Open file button' (mouse_event at screen coords from the captured window rect) DID NOT work — the click registered (cursor moved) but no native dialog appeared. The reliable path is Ctrl+O (Electron's built-in File menu accelerator) -> EnumWindows to find the new 'Open' dialog hwnd -> SendKeys the file path (wrapped in quotes for spaces) + {ENTER}. Generalize: prefer keyboard accelerators over mouse clicks for any UI driving from automated PowerShell — clicks are timing-sensitive on focus, keyboard sends to the foreground window unambiguously. (3) electron-builder packages all production dependencies into the asar — pdfjs-dist is listed in dependencies, so the asar contains a redundant ~2 MB copy at \node_modules\pdfjs-dist\{standard_fonts,cmaps}\ in addition to Riley's intended \dist\renderer\pdfjs\* path. pdf.js runtime uses only the dist path. Cleanup candidate for the next electron-builder.yml touch: either move pdfjs-dist to devDependencies (it's bundled by Vite at build-time) or add an explicit !node_modules/pdfjs-dist/{standard_fonts,cmaps}/** exclude. Not in scope this wave. (4) electron-builder only emits .blockmap for NSIS targets, not portable .exe — the brief's 'both blockmap files' phrasing is incorrect; this matches all 5 prior shipped versions (v0.1.0 - v0.4.0). The blockmap is for auto-update channels which portable doesn't support.
fix: For ANY future packaging-wave verification on a subagent where the desktop-operator MCP may not be surfaced: (1) Use the PowerShell PrintWindow(hwnd, hdc, 0x2) capture path documented in L-002. It bypasses foreground/Z-order and works reliably from inactive automated shells. (2) For driving the app to open a file, USE Ctrl+O (or whatever Electron menu accelerator the app exposes via electron.Menu) rather than trying to click the empty-state hero button via mouse_event — the accelerator path is timing-robust, the click path loses focus races. (3) Find the dialog hwnd via EnumWindows filtered on the app's process tree, not by polling MainWindowHandle (which returns the primary window only). (4) For SendKeys typed paths with spaces, wrap in literal double quotes inside the SendKeys string — Windows file dialogs accept the quoted path as a single token. (5) When the L-002 bonus check (File menu z-index) can't be visually confirmed due to a focus race in the PowerShell drive, document the gap honestly and lean on the test-level evidence (Riley's tokens.test.ts + menu-bar.module.css:43). Don't claim visual confirmation you didn't get. (6) Save the wave's drive + capture .ps1 helpers under tools/packaging-verify/ if this is a recurring need (third-strike escalation — once is one-off, twice is pattern, three times means stable artifact). (7) For Electron + native-module projects: full-suite tests POST-package fail with NODE_MODULE_VERSION mismatch because electron-builder install-app-deps rebuilds native modules for Electron's ABI (Node 24 host ABI 137 vs Electron 30's ABI 123). This is expected and the documented env-skew; CI on Node 20 is the test-of-record. Don't burn time on these failures.

task: Phase 4.1.1 polish wave — adopt + harden Marcus's Phase 4.1 hot-patches (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Adopted Marcus's 4 Phase 4.1 hot-patches durably. Decisions: Option Y (centralized PageModel dim refresh via new setPageDimensions slice action + measurePageDimensionsThunk wired into 3 open paths) over Option X (component-local measuredDims). Option Q (vite-plugin-static-copy 4.1.0 MIT, build-time copy of 185 pdfjs-dist standard_fonts + cmaps files) over Option P (commit binary assets to repo). 22 new tests across 5 files (4 slice + 6 thunk + 5 PdfCanvas + 6 ThumbnailItem + 1 pdf-render) all pass on Vitest 1.6.1 / Node 24.14.1. 59/59 my touched files pass; 297/302 full src/client suite (5 pre-existing failures unchanged). tsconfig.renderer.json typecheck clean; tsconfig.test.json clean for my new tests (10 pre-existing errors elsewhere). npm run build green, [vite-plugin-static-copy] Copied 185 items, flat layout under dist/renderer/pdfjs/{standard_fonts,cmaps}/. Three non-obvious takeaways. (1) vite-plugin-static-copy needs `rename: { stripBase: true }` on absolute-path globs OR the plugin preserves the full source-path tree (dest/node_modules/pdfjs-dist/standard_fonts/Foo.pfb instead of dest/Foo.pfb). First attempt failed silently — 185 files copied to wrong paths. Discovered by inspecting dist/. (2) fast-glob (the engine vite-plugin-static-copy uses) needs forward slashes even on Windows; path.resolve returns backslashes and the glob silently matches nothing. Required `r(...).replace(/\/g, '/')` normalization. First build attempt failed with 'No file was found to copy'. (3) When making a dynamic import static, the vi.mock factory's hoisting trips on `Cannot access X before initialization` — same lesson as pdf-render.test's workerOptions. Required `vi.hoisted` wrapper for the mock state. The dynamic-import-only version of the same test worked WITHOUT vi.hoisted because the test module was already loaded before the dynamic import resolved. Static imports require vi.hoisted; dynamic imports do not. Worth promoting to renderer-test conventions as 'use vi.hoisted for ANY mock state when the SUT statically imports the mocked module'. The PageModel default-mismatch defect class is the third strike (B-1/B-2 form-engine 2026-05-21, B-3.1 mail-merge sanitizer 2026-05-22, this wave). Promoting to global JSONL as 'prefer nullable + late-init over sentinel defaults — sentinel defaults are silently wrong, nullables fail loudly at the consumer.' Build-time-asset-copy beats committed-asset-trees is the second new global learning.
fix: For future renderer-test waves: (1) When switching a dynamic import to static, the existing vi.mock factory MUST add vi.hoisted around any state it references; the dynamic-import case worked without it because the module load order was different. (2) For vite-plugin-static-copy on Windows: ALWAYS pass forward-slash-normalized globs AND set rename:{stripBase:true} when matching files under arbitrary source-path depth (otherwise the source tree is preserved under dest). (3) When introducing a new Redux slice action that REPLACES a placeholder default (vs ADDS new fields), document the placeholder's wrongness loudly in the action's reducer block — the next agent who touches the consumer needs to know the default is a known-wrong-but-loud-value, not an authoritative initial state. The setPageDimensions action's docstring + the buildInitialDocument comment block both spell this out. (4) When centralizing what was a component-local fix, audit ALL consumers of the field that needs centralization — `grep -rE 'page\.(width|height)|PageModel' src/client` found 14 consumers in this case, only 2 of which Marcus had patched. The pre-flight grep is what distinguished Option X from Option Y as the right call.

summary: Surgical electron-builder.yml excludes shipped to strip redundant node_modules/pdfjs-dist/{standard_fonts,cmaps}/ trees from the asar. v0.4.2 asar shrank by 1,996,341 bytes (~1.9 MB), 66.5 MB -> 64.5 MB. L-002 visual verification SATISFIED: rendered-PDF screenshot shows crisp body text -> fonts/cmaps still load from dist/renderer/pdfjs/.
detail: electron-builder bundles all production deps into asar by default. pdfjs-dist is in `dependencies` because Vite imports the worker via ?url at build time, so the entire 278-entry pdfjs-dist tree gets bundled — but at runtime the renderer only reads fonts/cmaps from dist/renderer/pdfjs/ (Riley's vite-plugin-static-copy output). The two trees are 185 redundant entries (~2 MB). Two surgical excludes (`!**/node_modules/pdfjs-dist/standard_fonts/**` + `!**/node_modules/pdfjs-dist/cmaps/**`) eliminate them without touching the worker file at `node_modules/pdfjs-dist/build/pdf.worker.min.mjs` (which a blanket exclude would have killed). Non-obvious takeaway: asar size delta (1.99 MB) is MUCH larger than installer delta (0.73 MB) because NSIS LZMA compression was already efficiently compressing the duplicate binary assets — duplicate elimination shows up clean at the uncompressed-asar layer, less at the compressed-installer layer. So when reporting installer-size optimization wins, ALWAYS quote asar delta as the canonical number, not installer delta. Second takeaway: the binary's last-document auto-restore from recents masked my failed interactive-drive (SendKeys Ctrl+O -> native Open dialog -> file path). The dialog AppActivate('Open File') returned False but the PDF was open in both screenshots anyway because auto-restore kicked in. Useful behavior for users, but it means my drive script needs a `clearRecents` step at the top to actually exercise the open-from-cold path in a future packaging-verify harness. Otherwise the verification gate is conflating 'fonts/cmaps load via auto-restore' with 'fonts/cmaps load via fresh open'. Both are L-002-valid pixel evidence for THIS wave (the verification is dispositive that the exclude didn't over-match either way), but cleaner future evidence wants the fresh-open path.
fix: For ANY electron-builder.yml asar-trim wave: (1) prefer surgical glob excludes that name a specific in-tree consumer (e.g. comment cites the renderer import path: `src/client/services/pdf-render.ts:43` for the worker, `:189-198` for the font URLs); avoid blanket `!**/node_modules/<pkg>/**` patterns because most npm packages ship multiple distinct asset surfaces (worker + fonts + types + sandbox + web demo) and a blanket exclude is the canonical source of 'X mysteriously broke at runtime' three waves later. (2) Run `npx asar list ... | grep <prefix> | wc -l` before AND after to verify the exclude scope matches expectation — a number that DOESN'T match means the glob pattern is wrong (typo, wrong slash direction, missing star). (3) Report asar-size delta as the canonical optimization metric, not installer-size delta — NSIS LZMA already compresses duplicate binary assets efficiently, so installer-size delta under-counts the actual storage win. (4) For L-002 visual verification on Electron binaries that have an auto-restore-last-document feature: pre-clear the recents store (or run the verification on a fresh install path) if you want the rendered-PDF capture to PROVE the open-from-cold path works rather than the auto-restore path. Both prove fonts load, but only the former exercises David's fs:* IPC channel end-to-end.

task: Wave 18 Phase 4 + 4.1.x documentation refresh (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Refreshed four user-facing docs end-to-end for v0.4.2 reality. README +16 lines (Phase 4 status + 7 known-limitations bullets + 5 new acknowledgments rows). user-guide.md +388 lines: new sections for Capturing/Placing visual signatures, PAdES 3-step wizard, Timestamping (RFC 3161), Signature audit panel, Shape and measure annotations (7 tools + calibration), Annotation summary panel, AND a dedicated PAdES trust floor section enumerating all 4 obligations (Wave 14 three-location pattern: top-of-guide preamble + dedicated section + inline at every touching subsection). developer-guide.md +240 lines: Phase 4 quick reference at top, Signatures architecture subsection (cert-store single funnel, PAdES engine plug, schema v4 audit log, replay-engine step 3.7 PAdES invalidation), Phase 4 IPC channels table, Phase 4.1 fs:readBytesByHandle section, Phase 4.1.1 measurePageDimensionsThunk (Option Y) section, Phase 4.1.1 pdfjs assets (Option Q) section, test-count updates (528 main+ipc+preload baseline), AND a new Stubs-shipped-with-TODO pitfall (three Phase-1 instances cited: defaultPdfMetadata pageCount=-1, pdf-render.ts stub, PageModel 612x792 hardcode). api-reference.md +219 lines: Phase 4 channels section with all 10 signatures:* + annotations:* channels (Request/Response/Errors/example), 11 new setting keys, Phase 4.1 fs:readBytesByHandle subsection. Trust-floor obligation refs in user-guide.md: 19 (invalidate), 11 (memory-only/zeroed), 30 (TSA URL), 17 (informational/notarization), 10 (anchor section references). Three non-obvious takeaways. First: the trust-floor pattern has now shipped THREE TIMES with the same shape (Phase 1 H-3 walking-skeleton, Phase 3 forms JS-strip + XFA + signed-fields-invalidated, Phase 4 PAdES 4-obligation). Same doc structure every time: triple impression + cross-references. Promoting the pattern to global JSONL as a defaulted shape for any security-or-destructive-operation feature. Second: when phase ships 10+ new IPC channels (Phase 3 = 10 forms:*, Phase 4 = 10 signatures+annotations), api-reference.md grows linearly +200-220 lines. Budget accordingly; that's the channel-count tax. Third: the Stubs-shipped-with-TODO failure mode is now a third-strike pattern (different from the Permissive-test-stubs Wave 8.5+13.5 pattern but adjacent). Three Phase-1 instances cost 13+ waves of silent rot. Promoting both lessons to global JSONL.
fix: For future Nathan waves on multi-phase projects: (1) Before writing a trust-floor section, GREP the existing user-guide for prior trust-floor section names so the section anchor is consistent across phases (e.g. 'PAdES trust floor' here matched the Phase 3 'Forms sidebar status banner' shape). (2) When phase adds 10+ IPC channels, expect api-reference.md to grow +200 lines minimum; budget accordingly. (3) For Common pitfalls additions in developer-guide.md, file the new pitfall IMMEDIATELY ABOVE or BELOW its closest existing pattern (here: Stubs-shipped-with-TODO sits above Permissive-test-stubs because both pitfalls address the same class of silent-CI-green/loud-production-fail rot, but with different mechanisms). (4) The build-report Wave 18 entry MUST be additive (Marcus owns the file); never edit prior wave entries. (5) Test counts in developer-guide.md are easy to get wrong — read the most recent build-report wave's actual passed-tests count (528 here from Phase 4.1.2 verification, NOT the brief's stated number).

summary: Phase 5 (Scan + OCR) architecture design closed. 2 NEW docs (architecture-phase-5.md, ocr-engine.md) + 4 additive amendments (api-contracts §16, data-models §10, ui-spec §14, conventions §16). All 10 locked decisions (P5-L-1..10) encoded with cross-references. All 5 wave-19-brief design questions (Q-A..Q-E) answered. Three lessons from prior waves baked structurally into Phase 5 design BEFORE implementation: (1) stub-shipped-with-TODO blocked at the type-system level via required-on-interface RegisterOcrOptions.ocrPool + nullable+late-init on OcrJobSummary.pageResults; (2) sentinel-default lesson blocked via OcrWord.pdfRect: PdfRect | null (not {0,0,0,0}); (3) build-time-asset-copy pattern applied for the bundled eng.traineddata.gz via electron-builder extraResources (mirrors Phase 4.1.1 vite-plugin-static-copy for pdfjs). Native scanner verdict: DEFER to Phase 5.1 after surveying 11 library candidates — no MIT-compatible WIA binding survives the maturity bar (every candidate is either a name-collision, a commercial wrapper, or doesn't exist on npm). Channel placeholders (scan:listDevices, scan:acquire) reserved in api-contracts §16.9-10 so Phase 5.1 is purely additive.
takeaways: Library survey for native-binding evaluation deserves its own table format: Library / License / Last commit / Stars / Verdict / Reason across all candidates including the obvious-name-search rejections. Auditable DEFER verdict beats a one-line 'no good options'.

Trust-floor obligation enumeration in design docs is a durable pattern. Phase 5 is the third instance (after H-3 + Phase 4 PAdES) of three-location ratchet (preamble + dedicated section + inline). The design doc now enumerates obligations + surface locations in a SINGLE table (ui-spec.md §14.13) so Wave 22 Nathan applies the pattern mechanically rather than rediscovering it.

Anti-stub-shipped-with-TODO encoded at the type-system level beats convention-only enforcement. Required-on-interface (no `?` on the dep) makes typecheck the load-bearing enforcement. Nullable + late-init (T | null, not sentinel-default T) ditto. The convention text explains why; the type system enforces.

Cross-phase additive schema columns work cleanly when the column is nullable. Phase 5 adds signature_audit_log.invalidated_by_ocr_job_id (nullable FK on ocr_jobs.id) without violating the Phase 4 freeze — pre-Phase-5 rows are NULL (= 'not invalidated by OCR'), post-Phase-5 invalidations populate the column. Same pattern that worked for Phase 3 bookmarks schema delta.

task: Wave 20 Phase 5 OCR database (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Built migrations/0005_phase5_ocr.sql (184 lines, 3 new tables + additive nullable column on signature_audit_log + 6 indexes) + 3 new repos (ocr-jobs-repo 488 LOC, ocr-results-repo 332 LOC, language-packs-repo 248 LOC) + 3 new test specs (125 new tests total) + 2 new methods on signature-audit-repo (markInvalidatedByOcrJob/listInvalidatedByOcrJob). All 268 src/db tests pass under portable Node 22.18.0. ZERO semantic delta vs data-models.md §10. Two intentional pattern deltas matching Waves 7/12/16 precedent: omit INSERT INTO schema_migrations from the SQL (runner writes it), and add IF NOT EXISTS guards on CREATE TABLE / CREATE INDEX. Non-obvious takeaway #1: when the Phase-4 freeze allows ONE additive nullable column on signature_audit_log per data-models §10.10, the column has to thread through 6 places — DDL, types.ts row interface, ALL 5 existing SELECT projections in the repo, 2 new repo methods, 1 schema-test column-set assertion, 1 schema-test index assertion. Missing any one of them breaks the row-shape contract at runtime (TS will accept undefined for the missing field but consumers reading the audit panel will silently render 'never invalidated' even after a real OCR run). The regression-guard test pattern (assert every read method surfaces the new column via toHaveProperty) is the right shape — it's three lines per read-method and would have caught the projection-drift class of bug in production. Non-obvious takeaway #2: Diego's Wave 10 ABI-flip lesson generalizes — when better-sqlite3 has no prebuilt for the host Node's NODE_MODULE_VERSION (Node 24 = v137, no prebuilt available for 11.10.0), running tests locally requires a portable Node at a version where prebuilts exist (Node 22 = v127, prebuilt exists). distutils removal in Python 3.12+ means node-gyp's source rebuild path is also broken; setuptools restores distutils but ClangCL is then missing for better-sqlite3's build. Conclusion: rebuild-from-source is fragile on modern dev hosts; the prebuild matrix is the load-bearing distribution channel. The agent's local fix (download node-v127 prebuilt to node_modules/better-sqlite3/build/Release/) is per-agent and does NOT modify the project's engines or package.json — CI continues on Node 20 LTS where prebuilts are first-class. Non-obvious takeaway #3: better-sqlite3 ON CONFLICT (job_id, page_index) DO UPDATE SET ... excluded.column with a lastInsertRowid lookup is unreliable across SQLite versions for the UPDATE branch — the safer pattern is to follow the upsert with a SELECT by the unique key to retrieve the actual row id. Implemented in ocr-results-repo.upsert; pinned by a sibling test.
fix: For any future Ravi wave that amends an existing table with an additive column under a freeze rule: enumerate the 6 surfaces explicitly in the wave brief and code one regression-guard test that uses toHaveProperty to assert EVERY read method surfaces the new column. The SELECT projection drift is invisible to typecheck (the projected row is type-cast as the wider interface) and only manifests in production. The pattern: it('SELECT statements include <new_col> in row shape') iterating every list/get method. For test-environment ABI mismatches on better-sqlite3: don't `npm rebuild` (breaks if ClangCL absent + distutils not present); instead, `npx prebuild-install --target=<version>` for a Node major where a prebuilt exists, OR drop the .node file in by hand from the matching GitHub release. The project's package.json engines is the project commitment, not the local commitment. For ON CONFLICT upserts in SQLite where the caller needs the row id, NEVER rely on lastInsertRowid on the UPDATE branch — follow with a SELECT by the unique key. Document in the repo header comment so the next reader doesn't 'fix' it by switching to lastInsertRowid.

task: Wave 20 Phase 5 main-process implementation (Scan & OCR) (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Shipped Phase 5 backend per Riley's blueprint: 8 IPC handlers (6 live + 2 Phase-5.1 placeholders) plus ocr:listJobs (9 channels total) + 2 event streams; ocr-engine.ts single-funnel worker pool with LRU + watchdog; language-pack-manager.ts with SHA-256 verify + bundled-vs-downloaded resolve; ocr-text-layer.ts with imageToPdfRect coordinate xform + composeSearchablePdf via pdf-lib drawText (render-mode 3); pades-detect.ts read-only /Sig + /V Contents detector; ocr-bootstrap.ts production wiring (tesseract.js workerFactory + pdfjs rasterizer + node:https streamer + canvas adapter fallback). Phase 5 EditOperation variants (ocr-text-behind-applied + ocr-text-behind-removed) added to contracts.ts; replay-engine.ts step 3.9 ABORT guard for OCR-on-PAdES with new ReplayError variants (ocr_invalidates_pades_signature + ocr_job_missing); Phase 4 mutator-detection extended to include OCR ops. db-bridge gets 3 new repo bridges (OcrJobs / OcrResults / LanguagePacks) + 3 memory-backed impls + 3 adapt*Repo functions + markInvalidatedByOcrJob method on signature-audit bridge. register.ts wires all 9 handlers + app.before-quit + process.exit pool teardown + 2 progress emitters; RegisterOcrOptions is REQUIRED on RegisterIpcOptions (no `?` per anti-stub-shipped-with-TODO discipline). 101 new tests across 13 files; npm vitest 629/629 green; tsc -p tsconfig.main.json + tsconfig.preload.json both clean. Three non-obvious takeaways. (1) pdf-lib's PDFField subclass auto-promotion: after save+reload, the acroField is auto-instantiated as PDFAcroSignature (not generic PDFAcroField), and getType() may not be a method on every subclass — the robust detection path reads /FT directly from the dict via PDFName.of('FT'). My first pades-detect.ts version used getType() and silently returned empty array on every signed doc (false-negative — would have silently let OCR through PAdES signatures). Found via debug-print script; updated detector to read /FT directly with String(ft).includes('Sig'). Promoting as 'pdf-lib subclass auto-promotion — read /FT dict entry, not getType()'. (2) The Tesseract lang code regex `/^[a-z]{3}(_[a-z]+)?$/i` allows AT MOST one optional `_variant` suffix; my first test expected `eng_TOO_MANY` (two underscores) to pass with the `i` flag because uppercase letters match [a-z] under `i`. But the second underscore is not allowed regardless of case. Fix: test expectation, not regex. Worth flagging in conventions §16 that the lang regex is restrictive on purpose (rejects multi-variant or compound codes). (3) The 'dynamic require with sentinel error' wiring pattern for optional native deps. ocr-bootstrap.ts requires tesseract.js + @napi-rs/canvas via string-concatenated module names so the TypeScript closure stays clean. At runtime if either is missing, the pool/rasterizer surfaces a typed Result<E> error ('worker_init_failed' / 'pdf_render_failed') — never a Cannot-find-module crash. Same pattern Phase 4 used for cert-store + node-signpdf. This is the right shape for any optional native dep that gates a single feature; promoting to global JSONL.
fix: Three concrete process improvements for Phase 5+: (a) for any PDF-lib field-type check, READ /FT FROM THE DICT directly — do not rely on PDFAcroField.getType() unless you've tested the post-save round-trip path. (b) when writing regex-based input validators, test both 'matches' and 'rejects' cases including the boundary case 'looks like it might match but doesn't' (the two-underscore case here). (c) for any optional native dep, wire via dynamic-require + typed Result<E> at the consumer rather than hard import — keeps the type closure clean AND turns missing-module crashes into graceful errors the renderer surfaces.

summary: Phase 5 renderer implementation closed. 76 new tests / 76 passing across slice (ocr + scan), thunks-phase5, OcrRunModal, ScanModal, OcrConfidenceOverlay, OcrResultsPanel, and menu-bar Phase 5 wiring. 0 typecheck errors in tsconfig.renderer.json. 0 lint errors + 0 lint warnings on my new files. David's contracts.ts §10.9 landed in time; no scaffold-against-spec fallback was needed. Three Wave 19 design guards verified in code: (1) nullable-late-init NOT sentinel default — `currentSummary: OcrJobSummary | null` and `jobProgress: OcrJobProgress | null`; (2) `pdfRect === null` skip in OcrConfidenceOverlay (NOT rendered at 0,0,0,0); (3) trust-floor obligations surface at 7 distinct callouts across 4 files. The PdfCanvas + OcrConfidenceOverlay integration site is the one notable risk — overlay component is complete and tested in isolation but the actual mount point in `pdf-canvas/index.tsx` is deferred to the Phase 5 user-guide screenshot wave.
takeaways: When the contract owner ships the contract before the wave starts, the renderer consumer is a clean re-export-then-wire job. The gatekeeper file (src/client/types/ipc-contract.ts) added 38 new type symbols from David's contracts.ts §10.9 in a single edit, no hand-mirroring. Wave 2 lesson holding: re-export, do not redefine.

React Testing Library state mutations between render() and assertion need to be wrapped in act() to flush React's commit phase. The 3 failing test cases on the first run (running step / confirm-invalidate / done step) ALL went from FAIL to PASS by wrapping the `store.dispatch(...)` calls in act() AND switching one anchor query from /Starting/i (matched multiple elements) to /Phase: starting/i (uniquely identifies the running step's phase-label).

ESLint rule eqeqeq complains about `!= null` and `== null` even though they're the idiomatic safer-than-`!== undefined && !== null` form. Project convention has elected strict equality; renderer code uses `=== null` / `!== null` rather than `== null` / `!= null`. Three sites fixed (ocr-run-modal index, ocr-slice, thunks-phase5). One-time discovery cost; documented in case future Phase 5.x waves hit the same.

Adding new slices to the production store changes RootState, which surfaces as test-store type errors in OTHER components' tests that use a subset of reducers. shape-tools.test.tsx and pdf-apply-edit-ops.test.ts now have `scan: ScanState` in the inferred RootState union and the loose-dispatch helpers there don't accept it. NOT a regression introduced by my code; the test-store typing issue was latent and just surfaced now. Documented for Wave 21 Julian.

exactOptionalPropertyTypes:true blocks `{ invalidatesSignaturesConfirmed: arg.invalidatesSignaturesConfirmed }` when arg.invalidatesSignaturesConfirmed is `boolean | undefined`. Fix: conditional spread `...(arg.x !== undefined ? { x: arg.x } : {})`. Same lesson as Phase 4.1 (sentinel-default kin). Worth adding to conventions §16 next pass: 'never pass `undefined` to an optional field; spread conditionally instead.'

task: Wave 21 Phase 5 code review (OCR engine + worker pool + language-pack manager + PAdES interaction + replay step 3.9 + audit-log invalidation + 4-step OCR run modal + language-pack manager modal + confidence overlay + results panel + schema v5)
detail: 20 findings (1 BLOCKER + 2 HIGH + 7 MEDIUM + 6 LOW + 4 NIT). Fixed H-21.1 and H-21.2 in-wave; remainder routed to Phase 5.1 / Diego Wave 21 / Nathan Wave 22. Wave 22 dispatch verdict: YELLOW (Nathan unblocked with carry-overs). H-21.1 was a silently-failing pdf-lib `renderMode` cast — `...({ renderMode: 3 } as any)` was passed to `page.drawText` but pdf-lib's options type doesn't recognize the key, so the property was DROPPED at runtime and the OCR'd text was painting visibly on top of the scanned image. The symptom would have been: OCR works (text is selectable), but the rendered PDF looks like the OCR text was scribbled on top of the scan. The fix is `page.pushOperators(setTextRenderingMode(TextRenderingMode.Invisible))` before the drawText loop and `Fill` after — the proper pdf-lib idiom for setting the active text-rendering mode. B-21.1 (catalog SHA-256 sentinels) was already flagged by David's Wave 20 build-report row to Diego; recorded here as the BLOCKER for external Phase 5 ship. Permissive-stub recurrence count = ZERO new instances — fourth wave in a row the Wave 13.5 ratchet held under heavy load (Phase 5 = 4700 LOC + 302 new tests across three agents). Sentinel-default recurrence count = ZERO in production code (one test fixture intentionally exercises a zero-confidence edge). Worker pool lifecycle verdict: GREEN (all 5 conventions §16.1 non-negotiables wired; all 4 required test cases pass). PAdES 3-layer enforcement verdict: GREEN (handler pre-flight in both run-on-page + run-on-document, replay step 3.9 abort guard, audit-log backref — all three layers verified with tests).
fix: Two HIGH findings remediated in-wave (H-21.1 + H-21.2). One BLOCKER (B-21.1) routes to Diego's Wave 21 packaging — needs a `npm run build:ocr-catalog` script that fetches each Tessdata pack from upstream, computes SHA-256, and writes the values into `language-pack-catalog.json` at release-build time. Audit pattern lesson worth promoting to global JSONL: when a code comment block CONTRADICTS the surrounding code (here: the comment said `the only loss is the invisible Tr=3 flag (text paints visibly)` but the code asserted it was passing `renderMode: 3` via cast), trust the comment over the code — the comment is the author's mental model and the code is what shipped; the gap between them is almost always a latent defect. Add this to the Hard-Won Playbook section for code reviewers: 'When you find a code-comment contradiction, do NOT assume the comment is stale. Read the comment as the author's documentation of intent, then verify the code achieves that intent against the actual library/API surface.'

summary: Phase 5 packaging shipped v0.5.0 NSIS 132.7 MB / portable 132.5 MB (+26.2 MB vs v0.4.2). 3 direct deps installed (tesseract.js@7.0.0 Apache-2.0, @tesseract.js-data/eng@1.0.0 MIT-package-Apache-2.0-data, @napi-rs/canvas@1.0.0 MIT) + 12 transitives + 11 optional platform subpkgs. Zero AGPL/GPL/LGPL/EPL ingress. extraResources copies eng.traineddata.gz (~10.4 MB, SHA ed350f37...46a2468) to resources/tessdata/; asarUnpack adds tesseract.js/src/worker-script + tesseract.js-core + @napi-rs/canvas per R-W19-A mitigation; surgical excludes drop the in-asar @tesseract.js-data/eng dataset (dead weight). L-002 satisfied via PrintWindow capture of v0.5.0 binary + engine-layer OCR runtime smoke (95% confidence, 6/6 expected substring hits) composited into release/wave-21-v050-ocr-evidence.png. UI-layer OCR-trigger evidence punted to a future wave once desktop-operator MCP surfaces (verified absent in this wave via ToolSearch).
key_learnings: L1: vite (in electron-vite main bundle) does NOT auto-copy JSON files referenced by `require('./foo.json')` into dist/. The require expression is preserved but the file is not emitted. Symptom: packaged binary throws 'Cannot find module' on first whenReady, shows a small Win32 error dialog, MainWindowHandle and IsWindowVisible look healthy because the dialog IS a window — L-002 process-metadata floor would have called this GREEN. Catch-all: every `require('./*.json')` from a Vite-built ESM main bundle needs an explicit vite-plugin-static-copy entry. Fix used: viteStaticCopy({ targets: [{ src: r('path/to.json'), dest: '.', rename: { stripBase: true }, overwrite: true }] }) in the main: {} plugin chain. The `rename: { stripBase: true }` is mandatory when src is an absolute path; without it the plugin recreates the entire absolute path tree under dest.

L2: npm spec versions referenced in architecture design docs don't always match what's actually published. `@tesseract.js-data/eng@^4.0.0` from architecture-phase-5.md does not exist; latest published is 1.0.0. The '4.0.0' token is the upstream dataset version (tessdata 4.0.0_fast / 4.0.0_best_int), not the npm package version. Always verify via `npm view <pkg> versions --json` before binding a version constraint in package.json. Same gotcha with the directory name inside the package: spec said `4.0.0_fast/` but reality is `4.0.0/` (the package's internal naming for the fast variant).

L3: license-checker@25.0.1 is broken on Node 24 (`slide` module resolution failure). The fallback methodology (hand-rolled Node script that recursively reads every node_modules/*/package.json) is documented in LICENSES.md and is reliable. Don't waste cycles trying to npx license-checker from a Node 24 shell — go straight to the recursive package.json walker.

L4: Process-metadata is the FLOOR, screenshot is the CEILING — verbatim L-002 language proved out in this wave. The Wave-21 v0.5.0 first build launched a small error dialog (571x226) instead of the main window (1280x800). Process metadata showed 4-process tree, MainWindowHandle non-zero, IsWindowVisible true — all green. Only the screenshot caught it: title bar said 'Error' instead of 'PDF_Viewer_Editor'. Without L-002 mandating the screenshot, this would have shipped broken.

L5: For non-interactive PowerShell sessions (agent shells, CI runners without an attached desktop), System.Drawing.Graphics.CopyFromScreen fails with 'The handle is invalid'. The fix is PrintWindow + PW_RENDERFULLCONTENT (0x02) — DWM-composite-aware window-DC capture. Wave 21 launch-shot script in scripts/wave21-launch-shot.ps1 documents the pattern. Promote to a standard `~/.claude/skills/` snippet if a future repo needs it.

L6: Julian's H-21.2 fix (don't silently degrade on resource-load failure — add console.error + dialog.showErrorBox) is what made the packaging defect VISIBLE. The original behavior of `loadCatalogSync` was to return an empty catalog and continue silently, which would have produced a 'no language packs available' renderer state with no diagnostic. Structural-error-surfacing pays off twice: once at code-review time as a discipline note, and once at packaging time when a different agent (Diego, not David) trips the same failure path. Worth promoting to a conventions §16 anti-pattern entry in a future Riley wave.

task: Wave 22 Phase 5 documentation refresh (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Four user-facing docs (README, user-guide, developer-guide, api-reference) refreshed end-to-end for Phase 5 v0.5.0 reality. New surfaces documented: Running OCR (4-step wizard) + Manage language packs (catalog + download + English-only-in-v0.5.0 honesty) + OCR confidence overlay + OCR results panel + Scanning from a device (DISABLED Phase 5.1 placeholder). New OCR trust floor section in user-guide enumerates 4 Phase 5 obligations + 2 cross-cutting from Phase 4 (PAdES invalidation + tamper-vulnerable audit log) at the three required locations: top-of-guide preamble + dedicated section + inline at every OCR-touching subsection. 11 cross-references to #ocr-trust-floor; 16 inline Honesty reminder callouts. New developer-guide pitfall: Code-comment contradictions (Julian H-21.1 — pdf-lib drawText renderMode:3 as any silently dropped; fix at ocr-text-layer.ts:42 via pushOperators(setTextRenderingMode(Invisible)). New developer-guide subsection: Release-engineering responsibility — language-pack catalog SHA-256 (B-21.1; documents bundled eng real SHA + non-eng sentinels + Phase 5.1.x catalog-builder script obligation). Test counts updated: 629 main/ipc/preload + 373/378 client + 268 db = ~1270 unit/integration. Version refs synchronized 0.4.2→0.5.0. Three non-obvious takeaways: (1) the trust-floor three-location pattern is now ratchet-confirmed at four instances (Phase 1 H-3, Phase 3 forms, Phase 4 PAdES, Phase 5 OCR) — strong cross-project promotion candidate. (2) The code-comment-contradiction defect class generalizes beyond pdf-lib — comment claims async on sync return, comment claims error-swallow without try/catch, comment claims persist without writeFile, comment claims validate with body=TODO. Audit pattern: any 'as any' or '@ts-ignore' suppressing 'option does not exist' = type system correctly refusing a non-API; read docs, don't override. (3) Honestly documenting incomplete-by-design surfaces (English-only download in v0.5.0 with TBD-FILL-AT-RELEASE sentinels causing intentional pack_integrity_failed) is more honest than hiding the gap — the defense-in-depth posture is correct; only the docs need to say so. Marcus's brief explicitly invoked the 'simpler path' for Diego in Wave 21, and Nathan's job is to document the simpler path honestly rather than gloss it.
fix: For future phase-docs waves: when a wave ships with a deliberately-gated capability (sentinels, disabled menu items, placeholder IPC handlers), the user-facing docs MUST surface the gating mechanism + the unlock path + the workaround for the current release. Hiding the gap creates a worse failure mode than documenting it honestly. Concrete pattern: per-feature 'Honesty reminder' callout linked to the dedicated trust-floor section, plus an explicit Known Limitations row with 'Why' + 'Ships in' columns. The Wave 22 user-guide Phase 5 known-limitations table (10 entries) is the working exemplar.

summary: Phase 6 (Export to Office) architecture design closed. 2 NEW docs (architecture-phase-6.md ~610 LOC, export-engine.md ~560 LOC) + 4 additive amendments (api-contracts §17 — 8 channels + 1 event stream; data-models §11 — schema v6 + ONE new table export_jobs + 17 settings keys; ui-spec §15 — toolbar/menu/ExportModal 4-step + Exports sidebar + status-bar widget + Settings 17 keys; conventions §17 — 3 read-only-on-source rules + bytes-stay-in-main + 5 trust-floor obligations + 15 mechanical greps + 7 anti-patterns). All 10 locked decisions (P6-L-1..10) encoded with cross-references. All 6 wave-23-brief design questions (Q-A..Q-F) answered. Three Phase-5 lessons applied structurally BEFORE Wave 24 implementation: nullable+late-init LayoutRect everywhere; required-on-interface writer deps with exhaustive match; no-as-any/no-@ts-ignore mechanical grep in writers. Library survey: 11 candidates evaluated; docx (MIT 9.7.1) + pptxgenjs (MIT 4.0.1) added; exceljs + utif + @napi-rs/canvas REUSED from Phase 3 + Phase 5; officegen + xlsx + sharp + Apache POI + commercial rejected with reasons. The notable Wave-23-vs-Wave-19 pattern inversion: Phase 6 uses background queue (modal closes after enqueue) NOT modal-blocking because export batch is normal use-case — documented explicitly so Wave 24 doesn't apply Phase 5 OCR pattern by analogy. Trust-floor 4-location ratchet (preamble + dedicated section + inline + README + UI modal PerFormatLimitationsPanel) extends the Wave 18 3-location pattern by adding the UI point-of-action surface.
takeaways: When a new phase has structurally similar lifecycle questions to a prior phase, EXPLICITLY document the INVERSION in the design doc (e.g. Phase 6 background-queue vs Phase 5 modal-blocking) rather than implicitly defaulting. Prevents Wave-24-style cargo-culting of the prior pattern.

Library survey with auditable verdict table format (License / Version / Verdict / Reason for ALL candidates including obvious-name rejections) is now the durable pattern across waves 19 (native scanner) and 23 (office libraries). Strong recurring pattern; should become the default for any phase that picks among 3+ libraries.

Trust-floor ratchet is extensible by location-count per feature. Phase 1-5 used 3 doc locations + README = 4 doc locations. Phase 6 adds the UI modal location as a fifth location for features with a point-of-action confirmation modal. The convention: features without a modal stay at 4-doc-locations; features WITH an action modal extend to 5 (4 docs + UI). Promotion candidate to global JSONL.

Cross-phase library reuse is cheap when the use-case is symmetric. exceljs (Phase 3 read → Phase 6 write) and utif (Phase 5 decode → Phase 6 encode) both work as symmetric pairs with no shared state. Lesson: when picking a Phase-N library, evaluate whether a Phase-1..N-1 library has the COMPLEMENTARY surface. Saves another license-walk cycle and reduces npm dep count.

Single-funnel + per-page streaming + bounded transient memory is a triple-pattern that has held across Phase 5 OCR and Phase 6 export. The two engines have structurally identical pipelines: enqueue → dequeue → per-page stream → release intermediates → atomic write → emit completion. Worth codifying as a 'long-running main-process job' pattern in conventions if a third instance arises (Phase 7+ auto-update? Phase 8+ vector-extraction?).

summary: Phase 6 export-jobs db layer shipped. 1 new migration (0006_phase6_export.sql, 177 LOC) + 1 new repo (export-jobs-repo.ts, 803 LOC, 13 methods) + 1 new test (export-jobs-repo.test.ts, 938 LOC, 71 tests across 11 describe blocks) + types.ts edit (+120 LOC: new ExportJobRow + ExportJobStatus + ExportFormat + ExportQualityTier + 17 SettingKey rows + extended SettingValue<K>) + settings-repo.test.ts edit (toEqual to toMatchObject + 2 new smoke assertions for Phase 6 seeded defaults). 339/339 db-layer tests pass; 0 regressions vs prior 268. Zero TS errors in src/db; zero lint errors. Schema v6 delta vs data-models.md §11 = ZERO (two intentional implementation deltas match Wave 7/12/16/20 precedent: schema_migrations INSERT omitted, IF NOT EXISTS guards added).
takeaways: Settings-table name slip: data-models.md §11.6 spec text refers to the table as 'settings' but the actual canonical name (created by 0001_init.sql) is 'app_settings'. Caught at write-time by reading the Phase 1 migration before copying the spec INSERT statements verbatim. Documented inline in 0006_phase6_export.sql header so the next agent who copies the spec does not get bitten. Promotion candidate: when copying DDL verbatim from data-models.md, ALWAYS cross-check table names against the corresponding 0001_init migration; spec text drifts from the canonical schema names over multiple phase amendments.

INSERT OR IGNORE seeds break tests that strict-equality-assert on getAll(). Wave 20 settings test asserted toEqual({...2 user-set keys}) — my Phase 6 migration seeded 17 new export.* defaults via INSERT OR IGNORE, so the toEqual failed with 17 surplus keys. Fix: toMatchObject for the user-set subset + explicit smoke assertions on the Phase 6 defaults. Generalizable: any wave that adds INSERT OR IGNORE settings seeds MUST audit downstream tests for strict-equality assertions on getAll() and widen them to toMatchObject. Pattern reaffirms the cross-wave settings-seed-migration discipline.

better-sqlite3 ABI-flip recovery via npm cache prebuild. Node 24.14.1 had no prebuilt binary for NODE_MODULE_VERSION 137; npm rebuild fails on this host's MSBuild toolchain (same Wave 10 + Wave 20 pattern). Recovery: extracted C:/Users/ahudson/AppData/Local/npm-cache/_prebuilds/99329f-better-sqlite3-v12.9.0-node-v137-win32-x64.tar.gz and dropped build/Release/better_sqlite3.node into node_modules/better-sqlite3/build/Release/. Risky in general (v12.9.0 binary vs v11.10.0 JS shim — API drift potential), but 339/339 tests pass and the 11->12 changes were mostly TypeScript-only. Promotion candidate for global JSONL: the npm cache _prebuilds folder is a viable recovery surface for ABI-flip emergencies; the cached tarball naming convention is <hash>-<pkg>-v<ver>-node-v<abi>-<plat>-<arch>.tar.gz; cross-version binaries are sometimes API-compatible for read-only / single-process workloads. Diego owns the durable fix (electron-rebuild matrix + Node 22 LTS engines pin).

vitest run reporter silently swallows native-module load errors with 'No test suite found'. The dev (watch) mode reporter shows the real ABI error verbatim; the run reporter just says 'no tests'. Switching to --reporter=tap surfaces the actual error in run mode too. Saved 20 minutes the second time I hit it; would have saved 40 the first time. Promotion candidate for the Diego conventions wave: add a vitest.config setupFiles probe that calls 'new BetterSqlite3(":memory:")' at config-load time and surfaces the binding error explicitly so the default reporter is not lying about test counts when the binding is broken.

Bash tool cwd reset between calls broke the relative-path resolution in src/db/test-support.ts findMigrationsDir. The helper probes process.cwd() + cwd/.. + cwd/../.. for a migrations/ subdir — but when Bash runs from a default cwd that is not d:/Projects/PDF_Viewer_Editor, vitest cannot find migrations/ at any of those candidates. Fix: explicit 'cd d:/Projects/PDF_Viewer_Editor && npx vitest ...' in every command. Promotion candidate for the agent-onboarding doc: any test that depends on cwd-relative resource lookup will break under agent shells unless the agent prepends cd to its npx invocations.

Convenience-method-beyond-spec pattern reaffirmed for the FIFTH time. Wave 7 bookmarks move() / Wave 12 form-templates upsert() / Wave 20 ocr-results upsert() + ocr-jobs markInvalidatedByOcrJob / Wave 24 export-jobs mark* + listInProgress. Doc is the floor, never the ceiling — Marcus blessed each instance. The pattern: when the spec specifies the minimal contract and the implementation surface has 1-2 obvious convenience operations that the bridge would otherwise have to assemble from primitives, add them to the repo with clear naming + delegation to the primitive surface. Verify with Marcus when ratio of conveniences-to-primitives exceeds 1:2; below that, just ship.

summary: Phase 6 (Export to Office) renderer shipped.
