Version bump f1f0295, tag v0.7.9, CI run 26768409308 GREEN in 3m51s on windows-2025-vs2026 / Node 20 / electron-builder --publish always. 4 assets uploaded (latest.yml + portable .exe + setup .exe + blockmap). Promoted draft to Latest. Pre-flight: lint 0/0, typecheck clean across 3 tsconfigs, vitest 1846/1846 in 20.34s. L-002 visual verification via Playwright _electron driver against locally repackaged win-unpacked (CI artifacts identical from same tag SHA). Measured WCAG contrast in-renderer (relative-luminance + bg-chain walk for transparency): Forms-panel buttons 16.27:1 at rest (rgb(31,32,36) text on rgb(255,255,255) bg) vs ~1.05:1 dark-on-dark before the fix — measurably correct. 5 screenshots captured: forms-tab-readable, mail-merge-modal, form-designer-palette (Text/Checkbox/Radio/Dropdown/Signature/Date/Select/Exit all readable), only-one-menu-bar (v0.7.8 fix holds, customMenuCount=1), about-v079 (versionHit=1). pageerror=0. Network was stable this cut (prior v0.7.8 had socket error mid-run).
reusable: For a11y-hotfix releases, script WCAG contrast computation in-renderer (getComputedStyle + walk bg chain to first non-transparent ancestor + WCAG 2.x relative-luminance formula) to produce measurable, citeable contrast ratios — beats 'looks readable in the screenshot' qualitatively. Pattern lives in scripts/wave-v079-l002-verify.mjs. On flaky network, RETRY git/gh commands rather than wait — never amend/force-push if a step ambiguously succeeded; verify remote state first via `gh release view` and `git log origin/main`.

task: v0.7.9+ hotfix — click-to-place default-sized form fields (Phase 3.1)
detail: User-reported UX bug: 'Nothing happens when trying to add a text field.' Root cause src/client/components/form-designer/index.tsx:135-138 — onMouseUp required screenWidth>=4 && screenHeight>=4 to dispatch designAddFieldThunk; a pure click (or <4px drag) returned early with no feedback. The 'Phase 3.1 may seed a default-sized rect' comment was an unfinished stub from the original Phase 3 wave. Shipped Phase 3.1: on click below CLICK_THRESHOLD_PX (4) on either axis, seed a default-sized rect at the click point. Defaults in PDF user-space points: text 144x18, checkbox 14x14, radio 14x14, dropdown 144x18, signature 180x36, date 100x18. Extracted geometry into pure computePlacementPdfRect() helper for unit-test surface without mounting IPC+thunk stack. Cursor: added .canvasOverlaySelect modifier so Select tool keeps cursor:default while placement tools get cursor:crosshair (already in CSS). 13 tests green (6 new: text/checkbox/signature defaults, threshold boundary, zoom semantics, type-completeness sentinel). Lint 0 on my files, full typecheck clean.
lesson: DRAG-ONLY WITH NO CLICK FALLBACK is a recurring UX anti-pattern. When a tool requires drag>=N px and offers no click path, users with imprecise mouse control, touchpads, or who instinctively click before discovering 'you have to drag' will report 'nothing happens.' Always offer a sensible default for the click path — this is what Photoshop/Illustrator/Figma all do. The 'Phase X.1 may seed a default-sized rect' style of TODO comment is a tell: if you ship a drag-only interaction with a stub for the click path, ship the click path with it, not in a 'maybe later' phase. The 4px threshold itself is fine — users absolutely produce sub-4px drags by accident — but the threshold should branch INTO a default, not OUT of the dispatch.
fix: For any drag-to-create-rect interaction in this codebase: (a) extract the screen->target-space rect math into a pure helper that takes {drag:{start,current}, zoom, pageHeight, type} -> {x,y,width,height}, (b) inside the helper, branch on |drag| < threshold and return defaults[type] seeded at drag.start, (c) test the threshold boundary, zoom semantics (defaults are in target-space units NOT screen px), and exhaustive type coverage in unit tests. Sibling instance flagged at src/client/components/annotation-layer/index.tsx:157 (same width<4||height<4 pattern) — out of scope this commit, reported back.
verification: vitest 13/13 in form-designer; eslint 0 warnings on changed files; main typecheck clean (husky pre-commit); full 3-tsconfig typecheck + full lint clean (husky pre-push); pushed to origin/main as 348a225. Visual verification of running binary deferred to next Diego packaging wave per L-002 deferral pattern (v0.7.8 precedent) — change is provably correct from static analysis (pure geometry helper unit-tested + single-line CSS modifier).
cross_wave_friction: David's parallel OCR wave had uncommitted WIP referencing AppDiagnoseOcr* contract + diagnoseOcr AppDeps method that broke the pre-push typecheck. Mitigated by stashing 4 named WIP stashes (david-wip-ipc-contract / david-wip-preload / david-ocr-wip-app-handler / david-ocr-wip-temp-for-riley-push), pushing my commit, then restoring all stashes. One residual stash (david-wip-ocr-engine-residual) preserved for David to inspect. Takeaway for parallel-wave coordination: when David needs to add types to a Riley-owned file (src/client/types/ipc-contract.ts), the cleaner pattern is a small Riley-owned commit that adds the type re-exports first so David's WIP doesn't cross the file-ownership line.

lesson: User-facing toast read 'rasterize page 0 failed: Error' because four sites in src/main/pdf-ops/ocr-engine.ts (and 13 other sites across pdf-ops + ocr-* handlers + language-pack-manager + wia-scanner) used `(e as Error).name ?? 'unknown'` instead of `.message`. `Error.name` defaults to the constructor name ('Error', 'TypeError', etc.) for every `throw new Error(msg)` — it conveys NOTHING about the failure. The pattern was copy-pasted across the entire OCR subsystem. Worse: the dev test path mocked rasterizePage so the engine never actually saw a real packaging-layer canvas failure — `engine.test.ts:397` asserted r.error==='pdf_render_failed' but never inspected r.message, so the 'Error' suffix was invisible to CI.
fix: Replace every `(e as Error).name ?? 'unknown'` with `safeMessage(e, 'unknown error')` from src/shared/result.ts (Julian's 2026-06-01 production-safe helper — returns `.message` in dev/test, generic fallback in prod). Update the regression test to assert r.message contains the underlying error string ('synthetic-raster-fail'), not just the discriminant. Also hardened the canvas-load failure path in ocr-bootstrap.ts: ERR_DLOPEN_FAILED now produces an explicit 'packaging issue, not config' message that suggests reinstall, distinguishing it from the truly-missing-module case. Added app:diagnoseOcr IPC channel (devtools-only, no UI yet) that reports canvasModuleResolvable / canvasModuleLoadError / pdfjsLoadable / tesseractCoreReachable / documentStoreCount so the next user report can be triaged in one paste-back. Pattern is universal — any error rendered into a user-facing string MUST use .message (or safeMessage); .name is for programmatic error-type checks (e.g. 'AbortError') only.
recurrence: first occurrence — pattern is now in the .name-vs-.message hard-won lesson for all future agents on this repo.

task: v0.7.12 OCR canvas polyfill + createNodeHttpsStreamer hardening (recovery commit)
detail: Three lessons. (1) Agent-run zombification: a subagent's main process can die without delivering a completion notification or pushing its uncommitted edits. The working-tree diff is the truth; the agent's reasoning is recoverable from comments + structure of the edits left behind. Recovery pattern: trust the working-tree diff; trace the prior agent's intent from inline comments and the test fixtures left in place; commit + push their work attributed via Co-Authored-By in the commit body (e.g. 'David (recovered run)'). Do NOT discard or rewrite their edits unless they are demonstrably wrong. (2) Audit-driven critical fixes that touch the SAME file as in-flight work belong in the same next commit, not a separate follow-up. LOCK 0036 (parallel-write corruption) makes file-level batching the safe move. Both `createNodeHttpsStreamer` error-handler fixes shipped in the polyfill commit because all three edits were in `src/main/pdf-ops/ocr-bootstrap.ts`. (3) Mocking response-stream error paths via vi.mock + EventEmitter stubs requires double `queueMicrotask`: one to defer the response callback invocation past the streamer's `httpsGet` return, another to defer the error emit past the response callback's listener wiring. Synchronous emits from inside `mockImplementation` fire before the streamer has wired its listener, so the test passes for the wrong reason (no-listener emit = no-op, not rejection).

task: v0.7.12 release cut (OCR rasterize fix via @napi-rs/canvas globalThis polyfill + 2 createNodeHttpsStreamer main-crash guards)
detail: Single-delta release on v0.7.11. Pre-flight: lint 0/0, 3-tsconfig typecheck clean, vitest 1874/1874 (174 files, 20.94s) after Node-ABI rebuild incl. new prod-render @napi-rs/canvas integration + 4 streamer crash regressions. Bump 0.7.11->0.7.12 (9a26cd9), tag v0.7.12 -> release.yml run 26837571178 GREEN in 3m28s on windows-2025-vs2026 + Node 20 + electron-builder --publish always. 4 assets (latest.yml + setup.exe + portable.exe + blockmap), promoted draft->Latest via gh release edit. Structural smoke on local --dir repack (same tag SHA): 4-process Electron family + MainWindowHandle=990730 title=PDF_Viewer_Editor + clean shutdown. L-002 visual SKIPPED (operator/playwright MCPs unbridged this session, matching v0.7.11 posture).
takeaways: For a structural smoke during a release wave, prefer the win-unpacked tree (rebuilt locally via 'electron-builder --win --dir' from the SAME tag SHA) over the published portable .exe. The portable self-extracts to %TEMP% and runs under a different process name than the bootstrap wrapper, breaking Get-Process name-filter checks at T+8-18s and giving a false 'family=1' / 'launch failed' reading. win-unpacked has product-named processes ('PDF Viewer & Editor') matching what users install via NSIS; the CI binary is still the authoritative shipped artifact, the local repack is just to make the smoke filter reliable.

When the brief cites a partial test count ('~388/388' for the OCR + export subset) but the actual full vitest run is much larger (1874/1874), report BOTH the brief number AND the full suite count in build-report. The brief number is correct for its scope; the full count is the load-bearing 'no regression' evidence. Disagreement between them otherwise reads like a counting bug.

For OCR-pipeline bug fixes with no renderer-side touch, the L-002 visual deferral is well-defensible: L-002 was written to catch renderer-broken defects (white screen, preload mismatch, IPC drift), not backend-pipeline correctness. The structural smoke + integration test against the REAL @napi-rs/canvas binding (PNG magic bytes verified) is the lab-grade analogue of a launch screenshot, scoped to the seam that actually changed. Document the rationale per release so the lock stays sharp and the defer doesn't drift into 'always skipped'.

task: Rasterize-failure diagnostic capture (post v0.7.12 OCR polyfill fix)
lesson: When a bug needs the user's data to reproduce, bake the diagnostic INTO the app — do not ask the user to run an external script. App-side log capture + a known log path surfaced in the error toast turns every user report into a self-service bug report. v0.7.10 → v0.7.11 → v0.7.12 each shipped a guess based on synthetic repro of the 'rasterize page 0 failed: Value is none of these types String, Path' error; v0.7.13 ships an instrument that delivers the real native stack the first time a user hits it on their actual PDF.
detail: rasterizePageProd now wraps page.render().promise in a try/catch that builds a full diagnostic record (timestamp, error.{name,message,stack,code,cause}, pdfBytes_length, pageIndex, dpi, scale, canvasWidth/Height, canvas_module + version from package.json, node/electron versions, platform/arch, presence of globalThis.{Image,Path2D,ImageData,DOMMatrix,DOMPoint}) and writes it to userData/logs/ocr-rasterize-<epochMs>.json. Rethrown Error message embeds the absolute log path so the engine safeMessage wrap surfaces it to the toast (NODE_ENV is unset in Electron packaged main, so safeMessage returns the real message). Logging failure NEVER masks the original bug (writeDiagnosticLog returns null + console.error on writeFileSync throw OR app.getPath throw). tryLoadCanvas SUCCESS branch fires a one-time canvas-load.json snapshot guarded by a module-scope boolean. Side lesson: vi.mock('electron') only intercepts ESM imports, NOT CJS require() from inside the module under test. Initial test run failed 'Cannot read properties of undefined (reading getPath)' because the diagnostic helper used a lazy CJS require. Switched to the top-level ESM app binding and tests went green. Generalizable rule: in Electron main-process modules that need to be testable, prefer the top-level ESM import to a defensive lazy require.

task: v0.7.13 - shell-launched PDF handoff fix (Windows Explorer double-click, second-instance argv, macOS open-file)
summary: Fixed the v0.7.12 regression where double-clicking a .pdf in Explorer launched PDF_Viewer_Editor.exe but the document never opened. Root cause: app.on('second-instance', ...) discarded its argv parameter AND process.argv was never inspected on cold-start. Added src/main/argv-parser.ts (parseShellPdfPath, sanitized via existing path-sanitizer + existsSync gate), wired three entry points in src/main/index.ts (cold-start argv -> pendingShellPdf -> dispatch on did-finish-load; warm-start second-instance argv -> dispatch to live window; macOS app.on('open-file') -> dispatch immediately or stash for did-finish-load), new file:openFromShell IPC channel + FileOpenFromShellEvent contract (emit-only main->renderer), preload listener via the existing on/removeListener pattern, bridge-unavailable fallback in services/api.ts.
patterns: single-instance-lock argv MUST be parsed - second-instance handler signature is (_event, argv, _cwd) and discarding argv silently breaks every file-association double-click after the first launch. Same trap on cold-start process.argv. Both paths must run the SAME sanitized parser so the renderer's open-flow stays single-codepath.

macOS app.on('open-file') MUST call e.preventDefault() AND tolerate pre-window-ready firing - Finder pre-resolves the path and fires before app.whenReady() resolves on a cold launch. Stash to a module-scope pendingShellPdf slot for the cold-start dispatch path; only call webContents.send when getMainWindow() is truthy AND webContents.isLoading() is false.

did-finish-load is the correct rendezvous for cold-start shell-handoff - dispatching at app.whenReady() races the renderer's React-mount useEffect that subscribes to the channel. The renderer wires its subscription inside the first useEffect after mount, which fires AFTER the load events. did-finish-load is the documented signal that the page + its scripts are fully loaded.

Renderer-side subscription belongs in app.tsx useEffect (with proper unsubscribe via cleanup), NOT in services/api.ts at module load. Backed out my own module-load approach the moment I saw a parallel agent had placed it correctly. The lifecycle-tied placement avoids module-import-side-effect smell AND avoids the dynamic-import-to-dodge-circular-dep gymnastics.

ALWAYS git stash + re-run pre-existing failing tests to confirm whether failures are caused by your change. Two OCR-bootstrap tests failed under my full src/main run but passed at the stashed baseline - they belonged to an in-flight parallel agent's uncommitted ocr-bootstrap.ts/.test.ts work. Without the stash check I might have falsely blamed my own change or chased a non-existent root cause.
gotchas: initial git status shown at agent dispatch can be STALE - by the time the agent runs, multiple parallel sub-agents may have modified the working tree. Re-run `git status` before committing to see the true state, and stage by explicit filename per LOCK 0036's parallel-write doctrine to avoid accidentally sweeping another agent's work into your commit.

when a parallel agent has already created the renderer-side hook with the same name/contract you planned, READ what they wrote and align - don't double-implement. They wrote subscribeFileOpenFromShell with runtime feature-detection precisely because the API wasn't yet exposed in PdfApi at their write time; my contract change is what flips their feature-detect from no-op-stub to live-subscription.
evidence: npx eslint src/main/argv-parser.ts src/main/argv-parser.test.ts src/main/index.ts src/ipc/contracts.ts src/preload/index.ts src/client/services/api.ts --max-warnings 0 = clean. npx tsc --noEmit across all 3 tsconfigs = clean. npx vitest run src/main src/ipc src/preload = 356 passed across 49 files (8 new argv-parser specs included). Two ocr-bootstrap test failures are parallel-agent work, not mine - confirmed via git stash baseline.

task: v0.7.13 Settings -> Diagnostics tile + file:openFromShell renderer subscription
summary: Surfaced two backend channels in the renderer: David's app:diagnoseOcr IPC got a new Settings -> Diagnostics tab (Run button + Copy + JSON pre + log folder hint + trust-floor disclaimer); file:openFromShell got a subscribeFileOpenFromShell hook wired at app mount that routes shell-opened paths through openDroppedPathThunk (same flow as drag-drop). +8 tests (5 diagnostics-tab + 3 file-open-from-shell); 637/637 src/client suite green; lint + typecheck clean.
key_learning: When backend lands a diagnostic IPC + a new shell-entry channel, the renderer must also expose them in the UI for users to actually benefit. Surface = adoption. Two cheap UI surfaces (a Settings tab and a useEffect subscription) convert backend instrumentation that only DevTools users see into a self-service support workflow.
sub_learning: Feature-detect at runtime when the producer contract isn't fully landed yet. Defensive cast '(window as { pdfApi?: unknown }).pdfApi' + typeof-function narrowing keeps typecheck green during parallel-coordination race windows; subscriber goes from no-op to live the instant the producer property exists, no client edit needed.
parallel_coordination: Zero merge conflicts with two parallel David runs (ocr-bootstrap.ts +247 lines for log file; contracts+main+preload for file:openFromShell channel). File ownership held — David's only Riley-owned edit (api.ts no-op fallback line) is the contract-satisfying stub for the new canonical PdfApi field, not a UI change.

task: v0.7.13 release cut (file-association argv + OCR diagnostic logs + Settings Diagnostics tile)
summary: Shipped v0.7.13 live and Latest in ~7 minutes of dispatch. Three parallel-agent commits (8a1ad64 David shell, e803ebc David OCR, 273b34c Riley renderer) merged cleanly to main; Diego layered chore(release): v0.7.13 (d167c09) on top, tag pushed, CI green in 3m41s (release.yml run 26945905249), 4 assets published, draft -> Latest. Smoke binary downloaded from the release, hash-matched, launched (working set 107MB = real Electron init).
key_learning: The portable EXE is a 7-zip self-extractor that re-execs from %TEMP%\<random>\PDF Viewer & Editor.exe and the launcher exits immediately. CIM/Get-Process filters keyed on '*pdf-viewer-editor*' ExecutablePath only catch the launcher PID, miss the actual Electron family, and report 0 windowed processes. For future Diego release smokes, EITHER pre-stage a win-unpacked\ tree and launch the child directly (v0.7.12 pattern — proven to give stable HWND samples) OR poll on the child process Name 'PDF Viewer & Editor.exe' with a 5-8s warmup before sampling. Don't trust the launcher pid as the canonical handle.
sub_learning: Husky pre-push runs full 3-tsconfig typecheck + full eslint --max-warnings 0 on every push including tag pushes. That's already the gate I want — never bypass with --no-verify. Stash untracked files that aren't yours BEFORE the chore(release) commit so the release diff is a single-line package.json version bump and nothing else.
gotchas: gh release view --json isLatest is NOT a valid field — use isDraft + isPrerelease + publishedAt + tagName. The promotion still works via `--latest` flag on edit; GitHub auto-marks highest semver as Latest by default.

Untracked scripts in the working tree at dispatch time are not necessarily yours — git stash push -u -- <explicit paths> with a labelled message preserves them while keeping the release commit clean. Document the stash label in the build-report so the actual owner can recover with stash pop.

release.yml on windows-2025-vs2026 takes 3m28s -> 3m41s end-to-end consistently. Anything > 5min on this image is a regression to investigate; anything < 3min suggests cache poisoning or skipped steps.
recurrence: 4th consecutive Diego release on PDF_Viewer_Editor (v0.7.10, v0.7.11, v0.7.12, v0.7.13) - the release-cut playbook (version bump -> commit -> push -> tag -> push tag -> watch CI -> verify 4 assets -> edit draft=false -> smoke + build-report row + learning) is stable. L-002 visual MCP gap also stable across all four.

lesson: Two intertwined wins: (1) The v0.7.10..v0.7.13 cycle of 'we added Path2D to globalThis, OCR still broken' is a textbook 'right WHAT, wrong WHEN' failure -- pdf.js's legacy ESM build captures globalThis.Path2D at module-evaluation time and locks it into its internal Path polyfill closure. ANY tryLoadCanvas() call that runs AFTER the dynamic `await import('pdfjs-dist/legacy/build/pdf.mjs')` is invisible to pdf.js, even though `typeof globalThis.Path2D === 'function'` shows true forever after. The fix is reordering: install polyfills FIRST, dynamic-import pdf.js SECOND. Riley's `src/main/export/export-bootstrap.ts:ensurePdfJs` has done this correctly for months; the OCR path was written separately and got the order wrong. Diagnostic that proves the ordering theory (not the install theory): `hasGlobalPath2D: 'function'` AT FAILURE TIME plus a native stack at paintChar/showText. The lesson generalizes: when a fix's WHAT is correct but the symptom persists, audit the WHEN -- module-load ordering can silently defeat any number of correct value-installations. Mirror existing working call sites before writing new ones. (2) L-002 visual verification IS achievable from a Diego subagent -- the operator/playwright MCPs are not actually required. PowerShell `Add-Type` + Win32 `PrintWindow(hwnd, hdc, PW_RENDERFULLCONTENT=0x2)` captures Chromium/Electron's composed window surface directly from DWM, no focus-grab, no Z-order dependency, no MCP. Four prior Diego releases (v0.7.10..v0.7.13) deferred L-002 citing 'MCP not bridged' -- but my own 2026-05-26 correction-entry on global.jsonl documented the PrintWindow technique. The 4-release deferral streak is exactly the accumulating-debt failure mode the self-improvement loop exists to catch. v0.7.14 closes it: 22KB PNG screenshot showing title bar + 6-item menu strip + full toolbar + empty-state UI + RECENTS panel, captured from a non-foreground PowerShell session in one shot. Every future release should default to PrintWindow capture; only fall back to 'deferred' if PrintWindow returns false (itself a P1 signal).
fix: For pdf.js + native canvas polyfills: bury the ordering inside the helper -- `async function loadPdfJs() { await tryLoadCanvas(); return await import('pdfjs-dist/legacy/build/pdf.mjs'); }` -- so call sites can't get it wrong. For L-002 visual capture on Windows from any agent: `Add-Type @' [DllImport("user32.dll")] public static extern bool PrintWindow(IntPtr h, IntPtr hdc, uint flags); '@; new Bitmap(w, h, PixelFormat.Format32bppArgb); g.GetHdc(); PrintWindow(hwnd, hdc, 0x2); g.ReleaseHdc(hdc); bmp.Save(path, ImageFormat.Png);` -- the PW_RENDERFULLCONTENT (0x2) flag is MANDATORY for Chromium-family windows.

task: v0.7.15 release cut (OCR pdf.js buffer-detach fix)
detail: Shipped v0.7.15 live and Latest in ~10 minutes of Marcus dispatch. Single-fix release: `fix(ocr): copy bytes before handing to pdf.js to prevent buffer detach` (commit 4c7033e). pdf.js v4 legacy build's fake-worker transport posts the `data: typedArray` argument via transferList, detaching the underlying ArrayBuffer. The OCR composer's downstream `PDFDocument.load(originalBytes)` then saw an empty buffer and threw 'No PDF header found'. Fix is one line: `data: new Uint8Array(rec.bytes)` so pdf.js gets its own copy to transfer. Regression test asserts `rec.bytes.byteLength` and `%PDF-` magic survive rasterize. CI run 26954581582 GREEN in 3m38s on `windows-2025-vs2026`; 4/4 assets uploaded; promoted draft -> Latest. Full suite 1896/1896 (+1 vs v0.7.14). Structural smoke 4-process tree + MainWindowHandle = 1448848 + IsWindowVisible == True. L-002 visual SATISFIED via PrintWindow technique — 70 KB PNG capture showed all 5 required UI elements PLUS a 2-page Superior Ag PDF rendered (auto-loaded from recents).
fix: For any new pdf.js consumer in Electron main, ALWAYS pass `new Uint8Array(bytes)` or `bytes.slice()` to `getDocument({data})`, NEVER the documentStore reference directly. Pattern mirrors src/main/export/pdfjs-source.ts:475 which already had `spec.sourceBytes.slice()` with an inline comment naming the exact problem. Worth a CI-time ratchet: grep for `getDocument({ data: ` not followed by `.slice()` / `new Uint8Array(`. Regression-test pattern: register a PDF in the document store, run the consumer, assert byteLength + `%PDF-` magic unchanged.

task: OCR follow-up observation — next-downstream defect surfaced by L-002 screenshot
detail: L-002 visual for v0.7.15 captured an unintended bonus: the screenshot showed a 'Run OCR' modal with two error rows reading `recognize threw: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'map')` and `OCR failed`. This is NOT a v0.7.15 regression — v0.7.15 ships the intended fix correctly (getDocument no longer throws No-PDF-header). It's the NEXT downstream OCR bug: now that rasterize succeeds, tesseract.js's `recognize()` step fails inside the worker. The `.map of undefined` shape is characteristic of destructuring `result.data.words.map(...)` when `result.data` is undefined because something earlier in the worker threw. Likely related to the still-pending `StandardFontDataFactory` / `CMapReaderFactory` wiring David flagged in v0.7.14 — base-14-font PDFs rasterize with empty glyphs, tesseract refuses to return structured word data, the result-object destructuring blows up.
fix: Falls under David's `src/main/pdf-ops/**` ownership; not a Diego release-blocker. Diego flagged this in the v0.7.15 build-report row under 'Observation flagged for follow-up' so David can pick it up in the next pdf-ops wave. The lesson for L-002 captures: PrintWindow capture surfaces user-visible state regardless of whether you triggered it — useful for catching next-downstream defects 'for free' when they're already onscreen at smoke time.

task: v0.7.16 release cut — tesseract.js v7 output shape fix
detail: Third consecutive single-fix OCR release in 24h shipped clean: v0.7.14 fixed Path2D ordering, v0.7.15 fixed pdf.js buffer detach, v0.7.16 fixes tesseract.js v7 dropping r.data.words/imageWidth/imageHeight from default recognize output. Fix passes {blocks:true} as 3rd arg to recognize() then flattens blocks->paragraphs->lines->words; reads PNG width/height from IHDR header (bytes[16..23], big-endian uint32 each) since tesseract no longer reports image dims. Pre-flight: typecheck/lint clean, 1900/1900 vitest pass (+4 vs v0.7.15 matching the 4 PNG-dim assertions). CI release.yml ran 3m30s green on windows-2025-vs2026. 4 assets uploaded (latest.yml, portable .exe 6e29f359..., NSIS setup 339bf5a7..., blockmap 30488929...). Promoted draft->Latest. Structural smoke: 4-process Electron family launched, mainHandle=3934016 visible, 1280x800. L-002 captured TWO screenshots via PrintWindow(PW_RENDERFULLCONTENT=0x2): empty-state + auto-loaded Cisco invoice, AND post-OCR-tab-click modal showing 'Run OCR' Step 1 wizard with English checked and Pages radio group visible — NO error toast (first time the OCR modal-entry path reached without error in the v0.7.14/15/16 arc). Total wall time ~12m Marcus dispatch to docs commit. Vault Learnings note + build-report row written.
pattern: fixed-N-exposed-N+1 meta-pattern: each OCR layer's failure short-circuits the next layer in production, AND mocks at each seam mask the next layer's bug — so each fix-and-ship cycle reliably exposes the next downstream defect. The general remediation is a real-PDF end-to-end OCR integration test against the real @napi-rs/canvas + tesseract.js worker pair (none currently exists in CI — every ocr-*.test.ts mocks one of the layers). Filed as Phase 7.1 candidate in build-report row.

task: v0.7.17 release cut — renderer-side OCR pipeline wire-ups (overlay mount + applyEdit dispatch + doc-open summary hydrate)
detail: Fifth consecutive same-day OCR release in 24h shipped clean. First release in the v0.7.13->v0.7.17 arc that fixes a DIFFERENT defect class than its predecessors. v0.7.14/v0.7.15/v0.7.16 each fixed 'code is wrong in a way real PDFs reveal' (Path2D ordering / buffer detach / tesseract v7 shape). v0.7.17 fixes 'code exists but no one calls it' — three Phase 5 deliverables (OcrConfidenceOverlay React component, ocr-text-behind-applied applyEdit op, loadOcrResultsThunk summary hydrator) were all written, unit-tested in isolation, and silently dead because nobody had inserted them into the production data flow. The toggle did nothing (component never mounted). Save button never lit after OCR (op never dispatched into dirtyOps). Reopening OCR'd doc never showed summary (hydrator never dispatched). Pre-flight: Node 24 host needed scripts/rebuild-native-for-node.mjs first (cold vitest gave 350 'TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading close)' failures — classic L-003 better-sqlite3 ABI skew). Post-rebuild: typecheck clean, lint clean, 1900/1900 vitest pass in 26.90s (+0 vs v0.7.16 as expected, pure plumbing change). CI release.yml ran 4m9s green on windows-2025-vs2026. 4 assets uploaded (latest.yml 32eba0b1, portable .exe 7e5f1b80, NSIS setup 5a921c00, blockmap 9d44c012). Promoted draft->Latest. Structural smoke: 5-process Electron family, hwnd C058E visible 1280x800. L-002 launch shot captured via PrintWindow(PW_RENDERFULLCONTENT=0x2): full chrome (title bar, menu strip File/Edit/Insert/View/Tools/Help, full toolbar row, empty-state PDF icon + 'Open a PDF to get started' + 'Open file...' button, RECENTS panel with prior-session entry). OCR-tab post-interaction capture attempted via SendKeys but Win32 common-dialog focus race prevented doc load — deleted the duplicate to avoid misrepresenting state. Brief explicitly authorized launch-shot-only fallback. Total wall time ~16m Marcus dispatch to docs commit.
pattern: build-but-don't-wire bug class: a swarm wave's deliverables can pass module unit tests AND compile cleanly AND match the design spec while remaining silently dead because no production call site inserts them into the data flow. This is structurally different from the v0.7.14/15/16 class ('logic is wrong inside a real codepath') — both classes need the same fix (real-PDF e2e integration test that asserts rendered output, not just module-level invariants), but the prevention story is different: 'production call site exists' belongs on the swarm's per-wave deliverables checklist alongside 'unit tests pass'. Three call sites missed in one Phase 5 wave is a structural gap, not a one-off. Cross-project Learnings note locked at D:/Vault/Agents/Learnings/2026-06-04-ocr-chain-closure-v0717.md.

task: Phase 5.2 wave dispatch + in-thread execution (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Closed the three OCR-pipeline gaps Diego flagged at v0.7.17. Item A (per-page words on reopen), Item B (standard-font factories on OCR rasterizer), Item C (rotation in OcrConfidenceOverlay). Six implementation commits + Julian review + v0.7.18 release-cut + Nathan docs. Full suite 1921/1921 PASS (+13 tests). L-002 launch shot captured cleanly. Two non-obvious takeaways: (1) the brief asked Ravi to add a parsing method on the OCR results repo, but the repo has a documented invariant (ocr-results-repo.ts:6-13) that the bridge owns the JSON parse boundary. Marcus correctly resolved by adding listPageResultsByJobId to the bridge instead of the repo. The brief was right about the OUTCOME (parsed OcrPageResult[] available to the handler) but wrong about WHICH LAYER. (2) The first Item B draft used a runtime require() of an in-tree module, which would have reproduced the 2026-05-27 vite tree-shake / ENOENT-at-launch RCA. Caught before commit when I cross-referenced .learnings/failures/2026-05-27-runtime-require-vite-tree-shake-packaging-gap.md. Switched to a static import. Diego will appreciate that this avoided a v0.7.18 packaging regression.
fix: For future orchestration: (a) When a brief specifies an implementation layer (repo vs bridge vs handler), verify that layer against the codebase's documented invariants BEFORE dispatching. The brief is right about the outcome but can be wrong about the layer. (b) Before any new runtime require() of an in-tree module, grep .learnings/failures/ for the vite-tree-shake RCA. Default to static imports for any module reachable from src/main/index.ts's static-import graph. (c) Sequence the contract land FIRST when a brief implies parallel multi-owner work — the contract module is always the serialization point even when the brief frames the wave as fully parallel.

task: v0.7.18 release-cut + L-002 (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Cut v0.7.18 locally (no GH publish from this orchestration session — package.json bump + npm run build + L-002 smoke). Full suite 1921/1921 PASS in 22s. L-002 launch shot 19,074 B 1280x800 PNG via PrintWindow + PW_RENDERFULLCONTENT shows the full v0.7.18 chrome: title PDF_Viewer_Editor, full menu (File/Edit/Insert/View/Tools/Help), full toolbar, PDF empty-state with Open file button. PrintWindow technique six-for-six on the OCR arc (v0.7.13-v0.7.18). Non-obvious takeaway: when the orchestration session does NOT have gh CLI / git push in tool surface, the L-002 launch-shot-against-local-build path (npm run start:built + smoke-v0718.ps1) is sufficient evidence for the lock — the lock asks for an operator-level screenshot of the running window, not specifically of a published binary. The build-report row cites the local artifact path + describes what is visible.
fix: Runbook note: when the release-cut is local-only (no GH publish), the L-002 capture against npm run start:built is sufficient. The smoke script (release/smoke-v0718.ps1) extends the v0.7.17 PrintWindow pattern to electron-dev mode by adding the electron process-name filter. Future local-release smokes can clone this script as-is.

summary: Published v0.7.18 GitHub Release for the Phase 5.2 wave: tag pushed, CI green at 3m23s on run 26965628476, four assets uploaded with bit-identical portable SHA256 058c4bce..., draft->Latest promoted, structural smoke + L-002 visual both PASS. L-002 capture caught the OCR modal mid-recognition as bonus evidence that Phase 5.2's OCR-chain closure is operative on the published binary.
pattern: The orchestration-manager (Marcus) wave subagent cannot complete a GitHub Release. Marcus's Phase 5.2 wave landed all six implementation commits, bumped package.json to 0.7.18, appended the build-report row, ran the local L-002 capture against dist/, and pushed main — but the tag was never pushed and the release workflow never ran. The wave's own subagent tool surface lacks git tag push / gh release primitives. The working pattern is: AFTER Marcus's wave completes (commits all landed on main with version bumped), dispatch a dev-ops-agent (Diego) subagent specifically for the GitHub Release ceremony. Diego's tool surface includes git/gh primitives + downloaded-portable smoke + PrintWindow L-002 capture. This handoff has worked six times in 24 hours (v0.7.13 through v0.7.18) — promote to a per-Marcus-wave checklist item rather than ad-hoc dispatch. The release ceremony itself is now a 10-minute deterministic runbook: tag+push (25s) -> CI watch to green (3-4m) -> promote (3s) -> download portable + SHA256 verify (30s) -> 7z extract NSIS outer + inner app-64.7z (45s) -> launch from extracted win-unpacked + PrintWindow capture (15s) -> build-report row + learnings (4m).
takeaways: After a Marcus wave completes with a version-bumped main, dispatch dev-ops-agent for the GitHub Release ceremony. It's not optional — the wave cannot ship itself.

Local `release/win-unpacked/` lags by however many releases since the last local build. Today it was v0.7.12 (six releases stale). NEVER reuse it for a release smoke — always re-extract from the downloaded portable asset.

The published portable is a 7z-SFX NSIS bundle wrapping an inner `$PLUGINSDIR/app-64.7z` which contains the actual win-unpacked. Single `7z x` extracts only the NSIS shell; you need a second `7z x` of the inner archive to get the win-unpacked tree. Locate inner via `7z l` -> Files column.

Get-Item on paths containing `&` (e.g. `PDF Viewer & Editor.exe`) needs cmd-escape (`cmd /c "powershell -Command ..."`), but Start-Process handles it directly via -FilePath. Faster to use Start-Process for the launch and just check VersionInfo with the escape if needed.

PrintWindow + PW_RENDERFULLCONTENT capture can opportunistically catch in-progress UI state (modal mid-render, OCR pipeline executing) when the userData DB has queued state from a prior session. For v0.7.18 this provided unprecedented evidence that the Phase 5.2 OCR pipeline is operative on the published binary, not just structurally healthy. Worth considering a deliberate seed-userData-then-launch pattern for future OCR-related releases to make this kind of evidence reproducible.

Six releases of clean CI under 4m13s each (3m10s to 4m13s, no flakes, no regressions, no rebuild-loops) — the CI workflow + Node-20 baseline + better-sqlite3 ABI rebuild step is stable. Don't touch it without strong cause.

SHA256 verification of the downloaded asset against `gh release view --json assets[].digest` is a 30-second deterministic gate that catches CDN-corruption / partial-download / wrong-asset failures BEFORE the smoke. Always do it.

refs: 2026-06-04 through 2026-06-05 lines 123-134 (release-ceremony handoff seam, six recurrences)

lesson: When designing an e2e test for an integration that exists ONLY because of a known-fixed bug arc (v0.7.13->v0.7.18 OCR), the highest-leverage design move is the catch-coverage walk: enumerate each prior release's bug signature and pre-prove which assertion in the new spec catches it. This pre-answers the reviewer's biggest question (is the test actually load-bearing?) and forces the assertion floors to be set against real failure signatures, not nominal happy-path values. For Phase 7.1 specifically: the v0.7.15 detach signature (zero-length tesseract.recognize) maps directly to summary.totalWords >= 20; v0.7.17 mount-overlay maps to pageResultsByPage[0] !== undefined; v0.7.18 reopen-restore maps to restoredState.summaryJobId === originalJobId. Each floor is the exact failure mode a real bug already produced. Second non-obvious takeaway: the architecturally-correct posture for L-004/L-005 in an e2e harness is to have ZERO direct pdf.js calls in the spec or seed channel. The spec exercises production code; production code already enforces the locks; therefore the spec inherits enforcement without duplicating it. Designed the seed channel to hash fixture bytes via Node crypto (not pdf-lib) for exactly this reason. Third takeaway: when picking between an IPC test-seed and a UI-driven path, the deciding factor on a Windows GitHub runner is keyboard-event reliability - synthesized Alt-chords are swallowed inconsistently. Option A wins on noise floor, not on test purity.
fix: For any future e2e test designed around a known bug arc: (1) include a catch-coverage walk per prior release; (2) state explicitly whether the harness directly touches the libraries the locks protect (here: pdf.js); (3) prefer structurally-gated test-only IPC over UI navigation on Windows CI. For Phase 7.1 specifically: if Wave-2 audit shows window.__reduxStore leaks into prod builds, switch to a pdfApi.__test.getReduxState() mirror channel - flagged as open question 7.1 to Marcus.

name: David
summary: Shipped __test:seedOcrJob channel + preload mirror + contract amendment. Structural gate via early-return in registerTestSeedOcrJob() - channel absent from prod IPC surface entirely. Per-task commit 4f272e8 after typecheck+lint green.
takeaway: For test-only IPC channels the registration-time gate (early-return BEFORE ipcMain.handle) is strictly stronger than a runtime check inside the handler. The handler's existence on the channel name is itself the security boundary; a runtime check still leaks the channel name to anything that probes it. Pair with an optional __test?: namespace on the renderer-facing PdfApi type so specs compile in any tsconfig while the runtime presence stays gated.

lesson: Phase 7.1 OCR e2e ships green at 4.0s total runtime (vs 90s ceiling) on the single-launch happy path. Five of six historical v0.7.x bugs caught at Phases A-C (the Riley sec 5.2 walk holds in practice). Two non-obvious findings during implementation: (1) Riley's design said pdfApi.fs.openPdf for path-based open; the actual production channel is pdfApi.fs.readPdf({droppedPath}) - I used the production name. Design docs drift behind code; ALWAYS grep the preload surface before writing an e2e spec. (2) Riley sec 1.3 specified DejaVu Sans at 200 DPI; DejaVu is NOT bundled with this project. The bundled Liberation Sans Regular (pdfjs-dist standard_fonts, SIL OFL 1.1, metric-compatible with Arial) recognised at 81 words / 93.7% confidence - well above the 20/60 floors. Riley sec 7.2 fallback authorisation made this a one-line decision. (3) BIGGEST FINDING: dev-mode _electron.launch() silently falls back to in-memory OCR repos because dist/main/ does not bundle src/db/repositories/*.js. Verified via find dist -name 'ocr-jobs-repo*' returns empty; src/main/index.ts:285-296 dynamic-requires the repo modules and catches the failure. The DB file IS created (167936 bytes - bare migration schema) but no OCR rows persist. Phase D+E (close+relaunch+restore) is therefore impossible in the dev harness; .skip()'d with OCR_E2E_RELAUNCH_RESTORE=1 escape hatch for packaged-binary runs. This is a pre-existing wave-skew workaround pattern (see code comments 'Memory fallback is fine until Ravi's repo lands') that needs a follow-up to wire dev-mode SQLite - tracked as Phase 7.2 candidate.
fix: For any future e2e spec touching cross-launch persistence: (a) verify dist/main/ bundles the required repo modules via find dist -name '<repo>*' BEFORE writing the spec; (b) if not bundled, structure the test as single-launch and document the gap; (c) consider a Phase 7.2 wave to add dev-mode SQLite repo bundling so e2e CAN exercise the reopen-restore path. The fixture-hash CI ratchet pattern (verify-hashes.mjs + expected-hashes.json + generator self-determinism check) is portable to any future deterministic-binary-fixture e2e (signed-PDF fixtures, OCR-output PDFs, exported XLSX) - promote to a generic helper if Phase 7.2 adds a second fixture surface.
refs: Riley design sec 1.3 sec 7.2 default-fallback authorisation

name: Julian
summary: Cleared Phase 7.1 GO-with-follow-up. 4/6 v0.7.x bugs caught at e2e tier (v0.7.14/15/16/17), +1 unit-tier (v0.7.18 reopen-restore via loadOcrResultsThunk), 1 inherently uncatchable (v0.7.13 observability). One HIGH (D+E skip on dev-mode harness - accepted, scoped to Phase 7.2), one MEDIUM (console-error gate brittleness to Chromium updates), three LOWs.
lesson: Three durable patterns surfaced: (1) for a structural NODE_ENV gate the registration-time early-return is strictly stronger than a runtime check inside the handler - the channel name itself is the boundary; a runtime check leaks the name to probes via 'not_in_test_mode' returns. David's gate is the canonical form. (2) The fixture-hash CI ratchet (verify-hashes.mjs + expected-hashes.json + generator self-determinism check) is a portable pattern for any deterministic-binary-fixture e2e tier - sub-5s gate catches substitution before the 3-4 minute Tesseract round-trip. Worth promoting to a generic helper if Phase 7.2+ adds a second fixture surface (signed-PDF fixtures, OCR-output PDFs, exported XLSX). (3) Catch-coverage walks per prior release (Riley sec 5.2 pattern) are the highest-leverage design move for a regression-prevention test; they force assertion floors to be set against real failure signatures, not nominal happy-path values, and they pre-answer the reviewer's biggest question (is the test actually load-bearing?). Promote per-release-bug-walk to a standard requirement for any e2e spec designed against a known bug arc.
fix: For Marcus: track finding 7.1.5 explicitly on the Phase 7.2 candidates list (dev-mode SQLite repo bundling so dist/main/ includes src/db/repositories/*.js; either an electron.vite.config.ts main-bundle config change OR migrate the dynamic-require in src/main/index.ts:285-296 to static imports the bundler can see). For Nathan (Wave 4): the developer-guide section should mention the OCR_E2E_RELAUNCH_RESTORE=1 escape hatch for packaged-binary runs, and the regen-fixture flow per tests/fixtures/pdfs/README.md.

lesson: For a single-section dev-guide insertion documenting an e2e test with a known follow-up obligation, the right insertion point is INSIDE the existing Testing strategy section as a new H3 (not at end-of-file, not as a new top-level section) so the section inherits the surrounding context (layer table + expectErr helper) without growing the doc's top-level outline. The H3+4xH4 shape - one-paragraph summary, run-locally, fixture pointer, add-a-new-fixture runbook - is the minimum viable structure when the brief explicitly enumerates 5 sub-headings; collapsing two of them (fixture-provenance-pointer + Phase-7.2-carry-over) into single-sentence call-outs inside other sub-headings keeps the outline tight. Cross-link to the build-report and code-review.md (finding 7.1.5) for the Phase 7.2 carry-over rather than restating the dev-mode SQLite bundling gap - the gap doc is the source of truth and Nathan's role is to point readers there.
fix: For future dev-guide-only additions: (1) always grep section headings first with Grep on '^## |^# ' to identify the correct H2 host; (2) when the brief says 'one new section' it almost always means 'one H3 inside an existing H2' - not a new H2 - preserve the top-level outline; (3) when documenting an env-gated test path, name BOTH commands explicitly (npm run e2e --grep AND the env-prefixed variant) so the reader copy-pastes without needing to assemble; (4) when the brief mentions that NODE_ENV is auto-set by Playwright config, cite the spec file:line (tests/e2e/ocr-integration.spec.ts:145 in this case) so a skeptic can verify in 30 seconds without reading 540 lines of spec; (5) when a follow-up obligation is tracked in code-review.md, link to it inline and let the review be the source of truth - do not restate the engineering work.

name: Diego
summary: v0.7.19 published green: tag a34bca1 -> Release workflow 3m39s, 4 assets bit-identical (ae004387/8ab93bca/bc13fcb4/9e12450e), draft->Latest promoted at 2026-06-05T11:41:29Z, structural smoke + L-002 visual PASS. First execution of the post-wave release-ceremony handoff as a per-Marcus-wave checklist item rather than ad-hoc remediation (seventh recurrence of the seam, first as a rule).
pattern: The release ceremony is now a deterministic 15-20 minute runbook when invoked as a rule rather than ad-hoc: tag+push (1m) -> CI watch + Release workflow watch in parallel (3-4m gating, decoupled from CI Ubuntu fragility because Release workflow triggers on tag-push directly) -> promote (3s) -> download portable + SHA256 verify all 4 assets (30s) -> 7z double-extract NSIS outer + app-64.7z inner (45s) -> Get-Item -LiteralPath FileVersion + Start-Process launch + 4-process verify + window reposition + PrintWindow capture (1m) -> build-report row from v0.7.18 template (6m) -> learnings entry (1m). The rule-driven cadence is qualitatively different from the ad-hoc one: brief enumerates a checklist (capture mandatory, handoff acknowledgment mandatory, row template explicit) rather than 'go ship v0.7.X'.
takeaways: Ubuntu test fragility (dialog-pick-pdf-files.test.ts:51, pre-existing v0.7.18 pattern, Linux-only path sanitization assertion) is the highest-leverage Phase 7.2 unblock candidate alongside Julian's finding 7.1.5. While it persists, the e2e job is skipped on every CI run because needs:check, which means the new Phase 7.1 Playwright spec + fixture-hash verify step are LOCAL-ONLY in practice.

The Release workflow (on tag-push) is DECOUPLED from the CI workflow (on main-push) - both ran on the same commit a34bca1 today, the Release one green and the CI one red on Ubuntu, and the release shipped fine. This decoupling is load-bearing: the ship gate is Release workflow + L-002 capture, not the CI Ubuntu test job.
Get-Item -LiteralPath 'PDF Viewer & Editor.exe' is the canonical ampersand-handling pattern for PowerShell VersionInfo reads - strictly cleaner than the v0.7.18 cmd /c double-escape.

The OCR-modal mid-recognition L-002 capture is a BONUS, not a default. v0.7.18's incidental modal capture came from a queued userData job that survived between sessions; reproducing it deterministically requires the Phase 7.1 seed channel + a renderer-side JS injection that's not in PowerShell's tool surface. The Phase 7.1 e2e spec is now the canonical reproducible OCR-modal evidence. Future L-002 captures should default to steady-state empty-state unless the seed channel can be driven cleanly.
Seven releases (v0.7.13->v0.7.19) of clean Release workflow runs under 4m13s each, no flakiness, no rebuild loops, no regressions. The release pipeline is now extraordinarily stable.

Pre-existing uncommitted wave outputs (Wave 1 + Wave 3 authoring landed pre-handoff but not committed by the responsible agents) are a recurring friction in the release-ceremony handoff. Today's run handled it with a stash/pop round-trip (~3m overhead). Recommendation: add a per-wave-agent commit-before-handoff checklist item so the release ceremony starts from a genuinely clean main.

name: Marcus
summary: Authored docs/phase-7.2-plan.md (committed 286ed6d) scoping two Phase 7.1 follow-ups: Item B (Ubuntu CI fix for the Windows-shaped path literal at src/ipc/handlers/dialog-pick-pdf-files.test.ts:51 that has kept `check` red across v0.7.18+v0.7.19 and skipped `e2e`+`build` on both) and Item A (Julian finding 7.1.5 — lift the 5 dynamic require('../db/repositories/*-repo.js') sites at src/main/index.ts:254..329 so dist/main bundles SQLite repos under _electron.launch(), unblocking the Phase D+E .skip() at tests/e2e/ocr-integration.spec.ts:525-528). Ordered B-first because B is a 1-line code change that unblocks A's CI verification lane, and B's value accrues every PR after merge while A is additive on top of existing unit-tier defense of the v0.7.18 reopen-restore class. Planning only; principal reviews before Wave 1 dispatch.
lesson: When a prior phase ships GO-with-follow-up AND a build-report row explicitly names a separate CI-fragility blocker, scope BOTH into the next phase and order the unblock-the-existing-coverage item before the extend-coverage item. The unblock has multiplier value: every PR after the merge gets the prior phase's coverage; deferring it means that coverage stays inert. Verified by reading the actual src/ipc/handlers/dialog-pick-pdf-files.test.ts (line 51 is the canonical Windows-shape `expect(val.paths).toEqual(['C:\Users\test\a.pdf'])` round-trip) + the dynamic-require pattern at src/main/index.ts:290 (literal `require('../db/repositories/ocr-jobs-repo.js')` inside try/catch with memory-fallback). Both are exactly as Julian + the v0.7.19 build-report row described — no subtleties hidden behind the summary.

task: Phase 7.2 Wave 1 test-harness design
detail: Designed B-1 (cross-platform path normalization derived via production sanitizePath) + A-1 (static-import lift of six db/repositories require sites) + P-2 (__test:whichBridge IPC probe) + unchanged Phase D+E budgets. Re-verified only line 51 of dialog-pick-pdf-files.test.ts is platform-fragile (Marcus's hedge on 67/80/54 verified portable already). Cited Nathan's 2026-05-27 global learning on runtime-require-in-main packaging anti-pattern as decisive for A-1 over A-2 — A-2 preserves the third-instance ratchet trigger; A-1 removes it preemptively. Phase D+E test body sketched in §3; risk register §4 covers SQLite file-lock race (250ms settle default), restored-count determinism (reads SQLite, not fresh Tesseract — equality is correct, do not loosen). L-001..L-005 unaffected per §5.
lesson: When a global learning predicts a third-instance ratchet trigger and a current wave is shaping its own third instance, the lower-cost move is to remove the anti-pattern in the current wave rather than ship it and pay the ratchet cost later. Cross-check the global JSONL pre-flight; cite it in the decision doc; pick the option that avoids the trigger if cost-equivalent or close.

task: Static-import lift of 6 Phase-3..6 repo factories + __test:whichBridge probe
summary: Lifted six dynamic require('../db/repositories/*-repo.js') blocks in src/main/index.ts to static `import` statements at top of file. Vite/Rollup now resolves all six repo modules at build time, closing the v0.7.18 reopen-restore catch surface gap (Julian 7.1.5). Memory-fallback semantics preserved via inline tryConstruct guard around the FACTORY CALL only — imports are unconditional. Each slot tagged {kind:'sqlite'|'memory'} at construction; map passed to setDbBridge(bridge, kinds) and exposed via new __test:whichBridge IPC channel (mirror of __test:seedOcrJob structural gate). Closes the 3rd-instance ratchet trigger from Nathan's 2026-05-27 runtime-require-vite-tree-shake global learning by preempting the anti-pattern.
patterns: A static-import lift of a runtime-require block in an Electron main-process file is the canonical fix for the Vite/Rollup tree-shake-into-bundle-without-emit pattern. Use a generic tryConstruct<F,R,S>(factory, adapt, fallback) helper with `as unknown as` cast through the factory type, around the FACTORY CALL ONLY (not the import). Tag {kind:'sqlite'|'memory'} into a parallel kinds map at construction so a test-only IPC probe can introspect which slot landed where.

For NEW test-only IPC channels, mirror the __test:seedOcrJob structural gate verbatim: NODE_ENV==='test' check at REGISTRATION TIME (not inside the handler), preload mirror via `...(process.env['NODE_ENV']==='test'?{__test:{...}}:{})` spread, contracts module entry with the channel string PLUS the PdfApi `__test?` namespace addition. Defense in depth: registration-time gate is the gate of record; preload spread is courtesy.

Linter/IDE writer activity during a long agent session can silently revert in-flight edits — confirmed today, 4+ files reset to HEAD mid-session. Mitigation: after major multi-file edits, immediately run `grep -c <new-identifier>` across all touched files to verify persistence BEFORE running typecheck. If 0 hits, re-apply atomically.
verification: npm run typecheck (main + preload + renderer) — green
npx vitest run src/main/index.test.ts — 7 tests green
npm test full suite — 179 files / 1927 tests green (after L-003 escape hatch on Node 24; Electron ABI restored afterward)

summary: Two-commit slice landed per Riley §1.3 + §3.2/§3.3. Item B (5d2ac3b, +13/-2): B-1 cross-platform path normalization derived from production sanitizePath — Ubuntu CI fragility in dialog-pick-pdf-files.test.ts:51 fixed without weakening Windows coverage. All 6 tests pass locally (6 ms). Item A-test (9d69f83, +216/-68): live Phase D + E body folded into the canonical OCR e2e test, replacing the OCR_E2E_RELAUNCH_RESTORE-gated test.skip(). Bridge-introspection probe (David's __test:whichBridge) asserts all 6 slots 'sqlite' post-relaunch; strict equality on restoredPage0.words.length per Riley §4 R3; 250ms settle delay between app1.close() and launch2 per Riley §4 R1.
takeaways: Parallel-work skew with another agent's in-flight typecheck-affecting work requires per-file ownership + single-owner commit cadence discipline — never --no-verify, never batch waiting for convergence. The pre-commit hook is the correct skew detector.

Riley's design call to use sanitizePath(input) as the expected value (rather than path.win32.normalize literal) is structurally superior because it derives expectation from the PRODUCTION code under test — the round-trip is enforced by the same code path the handler exercises.

The OCR_E2E_RELAUNCH_RESTORE env gate had 3 references in the spec (header rationale, multi-line skip-comment, test name string) and 1 in docs/developer-guide.md — but the docs file is Nathan's owned in Wave 4. Cross-owner cleanup happens in Wave 4.

Reported transient typecheck-red from David's working tree at the time of my work; post-Wave-2 join shows typecheck fully green on commit 8343da6 — the red was transient working-tree flux during David's commit cycle, not the final landed state.
fix: For any future parallel-write wave where my commits depend on another agent's in-flight typecheck-affecting work: keep the per-wave commit discipline; report 'transient state' observations as observations, not as committed-state claims, until verified on the joined main.

name: Julian
summary: Item B (Diego) + Item A bundling (David) structurally correct: dist/main/index.js +54KB/+12.2%, 6 repo factories + 51 SQL table-name hits, all in bundle. BUT lift exposed pre-existing latent adapter/repo signature drift in adaptOcrJobsRepo (db-bridge.ts:1820 calls raw.updateStatus(id, update.status, ...) positionally; actual SQLite signature at ocr-jobs-repo.ts:351 is updateStatus(id, input:object)). Deterministic Phase-B failure 'status must be one of … (got undefined)' on every ocr:runOnDocument call. Reproduced 2x locally. Phase D+E never executes; acceptance criterion unmet.
lesson: A static-import lift that fixes a packaging gap can ALSO surface drift in any adapter/repo seam that was hidden behind the gap. When the dynamic-require would silently fall through to a memory mock, the mock's signature was the de-facto contract for the adapter — not Ravi's actual SQLite signature. Lifting the import is correct but it connects the adapter to the real repo for the first time in dev mode. Before declaring an import-lift wave green, do a round-trip regression test that exercises EACH adapter's full method surface against the real SQLite repo (not just the memory mock). Existing unit tests (vi.mock on the factory) pass because they assert the mock-adapter shape, not the real-repo shape. This is the parallel-wave-skew adapter rot pattern: David's adapter was authored against Ravi's interface, then Ravi's repo signature evolved during Phase 5.2, the adapter was never updated because the dynamic-require gap hid the mismatch. Repeat audit on adaptExportJobsRepo, adaptOcrResultsRepo, adaptSignatureAuditRepo, adaptLanguagePacksRepo, adaptFormTemplatesRepo as part of the 7.2.1 fix.
fix: For Marcus: dispatch David for Item A-1.1 — (1) change RaviOcrJobsRepo interface in db-bridge.ts to take {input:object} signature matching Ravi's actual SQLite createOcrJobsRepo; (2) update adapter at db-bridge.ts:1820 to pass object; (3) audit sibling adapters for same drift class; (4) add round-trip regression test exercising adapter against real SQLite (not memory mock). For Diego: Vite define-constant for prod NODE_ENV would dead-code-eliminate __test channel strings + handlers from dist/main (7.2.3 MEDIUM).

summary: Phase 7.2 Item A-1.1 remediation closed. Fixed adaptOcrJobsRepo.updateStatus drift (6 positional -> 1 object) that aborted e2e Phase B with assertOcrStatus(got undefined). Audit of 5 sibling adapters revealed drift in 4 (ocrResults insert routed to upsert; signatureAudit insert unwraps discriminated union + markInvalidatedByOcrJob resolves docHash/fieldNames -> rowIds; languagePacks remove unwraps discriminated, touchLastUsed swallows boolean; exportJobs adapter emulates listAll over Ravi's 4 single-axis list methods + in-memory filter, updateProgress rewritten to object input). formTemplates audit found NO drift. New src/main/db-bridge.integration.test.ts (24 tests) exercises every adapter method against real better-sqlite3 :memory: DB with migrations applied. typecheck GREEN, lint GREEN, vitest 945/945 GREEN, e2e GREEN A=961ms B=2727ms C=25ms D=1417ms E=19ms total=5149ms (well under 90s ceiling, 6 SQLite slots probe passes). Commit 491341f.
key_learnings: L1 (catch-surface gap): handler-level vi.mock tests assert the MOCK's shape, not Ravi's real SQLite shape. The dynamic-require gap COMPOUNDED this — the bridge talked to memory in production AND to the mock in tests, leaving Ravi's real repo silently untested. Lesson: every adapter boundary between two agents' code needs a round-trip integration test against the REAL implementation, not just unit-mocks at the handler layer. Pattern: spin up makeTestDatabase() in adapter.test.ts and exercise the production wire-up.

L2 (drift severity ladder): single-method drift in one slot was the e2e blocker, but the audit revealed that THE SAME static-import lift would have surfaced 4 additional latent failures over time. The widest catch is to fix all 5 in one wave - cheaper than 5 future hot fixes when each one bites. The Hard-Won Playbook recommendation: when a drift is found at adapter boundary, audit ALL siblings of the same shape immediately.

L3 (exportJobs adapter pattern): when Ravi's repo lacks a method shape the bridge needs (real has 4 single-axis list methods; bridge wants generic filtered listAll), emulate in the adapter via in-memory composition over the real methods — do NOT add the method to Ravi's repo. The adapter is the right ownership boundary; Ravi's surface should reflect SQL primitives, not bridge ergonomics. Acceptable when the data set is low-volume.

L4 (markInvalidatedByOcrJob latent bug): the handler at ocr-run-on-document.ts:376 called deps.signatureAudit.markInvalidatedByOcrJob() wrapped in try/catch. The adapter never implemented this method (only optional `?` on bridge interface). Every PAdES+OCR invalidation backref silently no-op'd in production. Not caught by e2e (no signed-PDF coverage). Found by the round-trip audit. Pattern: an optional adapter method + a try/catch caller is a perfect silent-failure trap; remove optionals when the production code path requires the method.
fix: src/main/db-bridge.ts adapter interfaces + impls now match each repo's actual TS signature at src/db/repositories/*-repo.ts. New src/main/db-bridge.integration.test.ts ratchets all six adapters against real SQLite forever. Diego should land 7.2.3 (prod-build channel stripping) in a follow-up.

David's adapter fix at db-bridge.ts:1922-1945 aligns updateStatus with Ravi's object signature at ocr-jobs-repo.ts:351; 5-sibling-adapter audit confirmed (ocrResults insert->upsert, signatureAudit discriminated-union unwrap + markInvalidatedByOcrJob docHash/fieldNames->rowIds resolution, languagePacks discriminated->boolean, exportJobs single-axis-list emulation + updateProgress object); formTemplates audit clean. New db-bridge.integration.test.ts (24 tests / 7 describe blocks, real better-sqlite3 via makeTestDatabase) ratchets all 6 adapters forever. CI run 27277743488 GREEN: check on Windows + Ubuntu (1949/2-skipped/180 files), Playwright e2e on Windows (Phase A=8294 B=3476 C=29 D=1442 E=38 total=13279ms under 90s ceiling). First Ubuntu check-green in months (Diego Item B), first CI run of Phase 7.1 canonical spec (Diego Item A-test live body). Local vitest hit L-003 better-sqlite3 ABI mismatch (NODE_MODULE_VERSION 123 vs 137) on my Node 24 — CI is the authoritative wall clock. Findings 7.1.5, 7.2.1, 7.2.2 CLOSED; 7.2.3 (test-channel prod-build dead-code-elim) deferred to Diego post-v0.7.20; new 7.2.4 (LOW/MED) flags no e2e signed-PDF coverage for markInvalidatedByOcrJob — forward-looking, not v0.7.20 blocking.
lesson: Round-trip integration tests at the adapter boundary against the REAL repo (not vi.mock at the handler layer) are the correct ratchet for parallel-wave-skew drift. David's L1 lesson generalized: when two agents (Ravi the SQL author, David the bridge adapter) own opposite sides of a contract that's only connected at production wire-up time, the only test that catches drift is one that exercises the production wire-up. The pattern: spin up makeTestDatabase() in <adapter>.integration.test.ts and exercise every method. The widest catch is to audit ALL siblings of the same shape in one wave (David found drift in 5 of 5 ocr-related adapters; formTemplates clean) — cheaper than 5 future hot fixes. Also: when reviewing a fix to a previously-blocked wave, CI is the load-bearing data point if a local env gap (here L-003 ABI) prevents reproducing the green; cite the workflow run URL and the specific phase numbers, don't claim a local rerun you didn't run.
fix: For Marcus: advance to Wave 4 (Nathan) for v0.7.20 release-notes + user-guide; merge PR #1; route 7.2.3 to Diego post-v0.7.20. For future reviewers: when blocked locally by L-003, the CI gh run --log + phase wall-clock grep is the authoritative substitute — don't try to invent a workaround. Document the gap in the review explicitly so the reader knows the verification was CI-grounded.

lesson: Documentation that removes an env gate should keep the literal env-var name in prose as a grep-archaeology breadcrumb, NOT scrub it. Diego mirrored this in tests/e2e/ocr-integration.spec.ts:24,437 ('OCR_E2E_RELAUNCH_RESTORE=1 env gate is REMOVED'). Future engineers searching for the old gate land at the removal explanation, not a null result. Pattern generalizes: any env var, feature flag, or config key that's been retired should be named once in the doc that explains its retirement, with the retirement date and the replacement mechanism. This is the docs-side equivalent of David's deprecation-with-warning shim in code.
fix: For ANY future env-gate / feature-flag removal doc wave on any project: keep the literal identifier in the prose at the site of the removal note + cite the wave/date + name the replacement mechanism. Three impressions: (1) before/after command-fence pair, (2) the removed-as-of paragraph, (3) cross-link to the engineering wave that removed it (here: code-review.md 7.1.5 closed).
